front: In Neuraxial α -agonists, what distinction applies to Equivalent analgesia to morphine?
back: Much longer duration of action

front: In Neuraxial α -agonists, what should be recalled about Synergistic with morphine?
back: Clonidine (30 μg) prolongs duration and reduces morphine consumption

front: In Neuraxial α -agonists, what should be recalled about Side effect?
back: Hypotension (no bradycardia)

front: In Neuraxial α -agonists, what value(s) complete this note: “Also causes some sedation with spinal clonidine (peaking within ___ to ___ hours, lasting up to ___ hours)”?
back: Also causes some sedation with spinal clonidine (peaking within 1 to 2 hours, lasting up to 8 hours)

front: In Neuraxial α -agonists, what should be recalled about Dexmedetomidine?
back: Even 3 μg can prolong blockade by without hemodynamic compromise Ref

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what value(s) complete this note: “Contains FIVE main components (vWF, ___, ___, fibrinogen, fibronectin)”?
back: Contains FIVE main components (vWF, 8, 13, fibrinogen, fibronectin)

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what should be recalled about 100 IU of Factor VIII?
back: C

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what does 250 mg of Fibrinogen indicate or lead to?
back: Clot stabilizer and platelet activator

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what should be recalled about Administration?
back: As rapidly as possible (at least 200 mL/hr) through a filter

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what should be recalled about Indications (Source?
back: Barash, Clinical Anesthesia, 7th Edition, Table 16-7)

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what should be recalled about von Willebrand's disease?
back: DDAVP first line, then factor concentrates, lastly cryoprecipitate

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what should be recalled about Hypofibrinogenemia?
back: Usually < 80-100 mg/dL

front: In Cryoprecipitate, what should be recalled about Bleeding from excessive anticoagulation?
back: But FFP is a better choice

front: In Spinal Cord Stimulation, what does Aα, Aβ, and Aγ indicate or lead to?
back: Large, myelinated fibers → carry innocuous stimuli

front: In Spinal Cord Stimulation, what does Thinly myelinated Aδ and unmyelinated C-fibers indicate or lead to?
back: Carry pain stimuli

front: In Spinal Cord Stimulation, what does In chronic pain: pain signals from Aδ and C-fibers more intense indicate or lead to?
back: Pain signals go through

front: In Spinal Cord Stimulation, what does Larger fibers (Aα, Aβ) are more sensitive to external electrical stimulation indicate or lead to?
back: They crowd out or close the “gate” to pain signals from Aδ and C-fibers → decreased pain transmission

front: In Spinal Cord Stimulation, what does First trimester of pregnancy (some say all pregnancy) indicate or lead to?
back: Risk of preterm labor

front: In Robot-Assisted Gynecological/Prostate Surgery, what does Increased IOP and ICP indicate or lead to?
back: Can lead to aneurysmal rupture → contraindication

front: In Robot-Assisted Gynecological/Prostate Surgery, what does Marfan (fibrillin defect) indicate or lead to?
back: Echocardiography to evaluate for aortic root dilation

front: In Robot-Assisted Gynecological/Prostate Surgery, what does Decreased ERV indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased FRC → decreased TLC

front: In Robot-Assisted Gynecological/Prostate Surgery, what does Decreased lung compliance, increased airway pressures, increased V/Q mismatch indicate or lead to?
back: Use PEEP

front: In Robot-Assisted Gynecological/Prostate Surgery, what does Extraperitoneal insufflation indicate or lead to?
back: Larger increases in P CO than in intraperitoneal insufflation

front: In Conn’s Syndrome, what does Excess aldosterone indicate or lead to?
back: Distal tubule →

front: In Conn’s Syndrome, what does Absorbs Na + and water indicate or lead to?
back: Volume expansion → hypertension

front: In Conn’s Syndrome, what does Excretes H + and K + indicate or lead to?
back: Hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis

front: In Conn’s Syndrome, what does Negative feedback indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased renin

front: In Conn’s Syndrome, what should be recalled about Clinical?
back: Headache (HTN), fatigue (hypokalemia), muscle cramps (hypokalemia)

front: In Conn’s Syndrome, what should be recalled about Preoperative management?
back: Spironolactone, potassium supplementation, avoid hyperventilation

front: In Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome, what does Systemic venous blood indicate or lead to?
back: RA → mixes with oxygenated blood from LA via ASD/PFO → mixed blood enters RV → enters PA →

front: In Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome, what does Retrograde flow indicate or lead to?
back: Cerebral and coronary flow

front: In Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome, what does Parallel univentricular physiology: single RV indicate or lead to?
back: Systemic blood flow from pulmonary artery (PA) → PDA → aorta

front: In Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome, what does Survival depends on balance of resistance between systemic and pulmonary vasculature indicate or lead to?
back: Qp:Qs (ratio of blood flow between the pulmonary and systemic circulations) determined in the cath lab

front: In Herpes and Pregnancy, what does Primary maternal infection: viremia indicate or lead to?
back: Systemic symptoms (headache, fever)

front: In Herpes and Pregnancy, what should be recalled about Rare complications?
back: Hepatitis, meningitis, encephalitis

front: In Herpes and Pregnancy, what does Safety of neuraxial anesthesia unclear indicate or lead to?
back: Needle may introduce virus into CNS

front: In Herpes and Pregnancy, what does Recurrent infections indicate or lead to?
back: Localized vesicular lesions interspersed with asymptomatic periods

front: In Herpes and Pregnancy, what does Active genital HSV-2 lesions (primary or recurrent) or prodromal symptoms indicate or lead to?
back: Cesarean delivery

front: In Herpes and Pregnancy, what should be recalled about Fetal transmission risk factors?
back: Primary infections, active disease at labor, invasive fetal monitoring, vaginal delivery with active disease

front: How does Placental Abruption present?
back: Painful vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness

front: In Placental Abruption, what should be recalled about Diagnosis?
back: Mostly clinical, ultrasound is specific but not sensitive (only 24%; US negative = not ruled out)

front: In Placental Abruption, what value(s) complete this note: “Advanced maternal age (Age > ___y)”?
back: Advanced maternal age (Age > 35y)

front: In Pulmonary Vascular Resistance, what does PVR is lowest at FRC indicate or lead to?
back: Resistance increases as you move away from FRC in either direction

front: In Pulmonary Vascular Resistance, what does Alveolar (septal) vessels: compressed when volume increases (alveoli fill up) indicate or lead to?
back: Increased resistance

front: In Pulmonary Vascular Resistance, what does Extra-alveolar (corner) vessels: distended when lung volume increases (imagine the lung parenchyma expanding with volume indicate or lead to?
back: Stretching vessels) → decreased resistance

front: In Lateral Femoral Cutaneous Nerve (LFCN), what does Anatomy: branches from L2-L3 nerve roots indicate or lead to?
back: Courses along the lateral aspect of iliacus muscle above fascia lata (while intra-abdominal) → passes inguinal ligament → ascends over sartorius muscle → pierces through fascia lata

front: In Lateral Femoral Cutaneous Nerve (LFCN), what does LFCN block: inject 2-2.5 cm medial and inferior to the ASIS (0.5-1 cm deep) indicate or lead to?
back: Above and below fascia lata

front: In Lateral Femoral Cutaneous Nerve (LFCN), what should be recalled about Located between two muscles?
back: Sartorius (medially) and tensor fascia lata (laterally)

front: In Anesthesia for Cerclage Placement, what does Need to prevent increases in intra-abdominal and intrauterine pressures indicate or lead to?
back: General anesthesia

front: In Anesthesia for Cerclage Placement, what does Volatile anesthetics relax uterine smooth muscle indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased intrauterine pressure

front: In Anesthesia for Cerclage Placement, what does Avoid endotracheal tube-induced coughing indicate or lead to?
back: Raises intrauterine pressure

front: In Anesthesia for Cerclage Placement, what does Prophylaxis against PONV indicate or lead to?
back: Avoid associated increase in intrauterine pressure.

front: In Anesthesia for Cerclage Placement, what should be recalled about When cervix is NOT dilated?
back: Spinal, epidural, or general anesthesia may all be safely administered

front: In Anesthesia for Cerclage Placement, what does Sensory blockade from T10-S4 indicate or lead to?
back: For cervix (T10-L1) + vagina and perineum (S2-S4)

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Mechanism: ɑ -agonist (1:1600 ɑ :ɑ indicate or lead to?
back: 8x more specific than clonidine at this receptor) → analgesic (opioid-sparing effect), amnestic, sedative (good for MAC cases), anxiolytic, and hypnotic

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does ɑ -receptor indicate or lead to?
back: Presynaptic inhibition of norepinephrine and epinephrine release Receptor → ɑ A ɑ B ɑ C Effects Presynaptic inhibition of Analgesia, hypertension - Inhibition of adrenal norepinephrine release, seen initially with bolus dose catecholamine release, sedation, hypotension, analgesia anticonvulsant

front: What is the onset in Dexmedetomidine?
back: 15 minutes due to rapid distribution phase

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Dosage: continuous infusion at 0.2-0.7 mcg/kg/hr indicate or lead to?
back: 1 hour → peak concentration

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what should be recalled about Initial bolus?
back: 1 mcg/kg/hr over 10 minutes

front: What is the elimination half-life in Dexmedetomidine?
back: 2-2.5 hours (higher in kids)

front: What is the context-sensitive half-life in Dexmedetomidine?
back: 4 min after 10 min infusion, 4 hours after 8 hour infusion

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Metabolism: CYP450 (hepatic) to inactive metabolites indicate or lead to?
back: Excreted 95% urine + 4% feces

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Renal failure indicate or lead to?
back: No difference in renal impairment (because metabolites are inactive)

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Liver failure indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased metabolism → lower clearance → longer duration

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what should be recalled about Advantages?
back: Does not suppress respiratory drive, sedation mimics natural sleep

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what distinction applies to MV can decrease a little (TV goes down?
back: RR may slightly increase)

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Myocardial depressant, ↓ HR indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ CO; ↓ SVR → ↓ SBP → ↓ perioperative MI

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Biphasic response seen with initiation of dexmedetomidine (hypertension indicate or lead to?
back: Hypotension)

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Initially, ɑ B (peripheral) agonism indicate or lead to?
back: Vasoconstriction → hypertension (esp. With bolus dose)

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Eventually, ɑ A (central) agonism indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ sympathetic outflow → hypotension

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what does Avoid with spinal anesthesia indicate or lead to?
back: Sympathectomy from spinal + dexmedetomidine → cardiovascular collapse

front: In Dexmedetomidine, what should be recalled about Atipamezole?
back: Synthetic α -adrenergic antagonist that can reverse dexmedetomidine (not approved in humans)

front: In Ex utero intrapartum treatment (EXIT) surgery, what does Fetus partially delivered in Cesarean but umbilical cord left intact indicate or lead to?
back: For fetal bronchoscopy, intubation

front: In Ex utero intrapartum treatment (EXIT) surgery, what should be recalled about Anesthetic considerations?
back: Uterine relaxation provided with nitroglycerin or volatiles

front: In Alcohol, what does Hypocalcemia: Mg 2+ needed to absorb Ca 2+ indicate or lead to?
back: Correct hypomagnesemia first!

front: In Alcohol, what does Hypoglycemia indicate or lead to?
back: Replace thiamine (B ) first, otherwise they can get Wernicke’s Encephalopathy

front: In Alcohol, what does Acute intoxication indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased MAC (less anesthetic needed), delayed gastric emptying (reflux)

front: In Alcohol, what does Chronic abuse indicate or lead to?
back: Increased MAC (more anesthetic needed)

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does CNS depression: potentiates inhibitory channels indicate or lead to?
back: GABA agonism, glycine agonism

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what should be recalled about Spinal cord?
back: Hyperpolarizes (inhibits) alpha-motor neurons through interneurons

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what should be recalled about NMJ?
back: Makes postsynaptic nAChR less sensitive to ACh, and possible ↓ ACh release

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what should be recalled about Amnesia?
back: Supraspinal targets (hippocampus, amygdala, cerebral cortex)

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what should be recalled about Vapor pressures (at 20C)?
back: Desflurane (660) > isoflurane (240) > sevoflurane (160)

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does Vapor pressure depends on temperature (lower temperature indicate or lead to?
back: Lower vapor pressure)

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does Desflurane has very high vapor pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Vaporizes readily → temperature drops significantly → vapor pressure changes → hard to accurately control vaporization → so, heated to constant temp

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does Volatile anesthetics indicate or lead to?
back: Decrease CMRO → cause some decrease in CBF

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does However, volatile anesthetics indicate or lead to?
back: Direct cerebral vasodilation → increase in CBF

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does At < 1 MAC: increase and decrease in CBF balance out indicate or lead to?
back: CBF remains stable

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does At > 1 MAC: dose-dependent increase in CBF indicate or lead to?
back: “uncoupling” because CBF increases while CMRO decreases (CBF actually drops in response to CMRO , but vasodilation more prominent)

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does Increased CBF indicate or lead to?
back: Mild increase in ICP

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does Nitrous oxide: vasodilation (↑ CBF) + increase in CMRO (↑ CBF) indicate or lead to?
back: ↑↑ CBF → ↑↑ ICP

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does Ketamine indicate or lead to?
back: Increases CMRO , ↑ CBF → ↑ ICP (classic teaching)

front: In Inhalational Agents: Neuropharmacology, what does Propofol, etomidate and thiopental indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased CMRO , CBF and ICP

front: In Postoperative Acute Renal Failure, what value(s) complete this note: “Occurs in ___ of noncardiac surgery patients who had previously normal renal function”?
back: Occurs in 1% of noncardiac surgery patients who had previously normal renal function

front: In Postoperative Acute Renal Failure, what value(s) complete this note: “Age ≥ ___”?
back: Age ≥ 59

front: In Postoperative Acute Renal Failure, what value(s) complete this note: “BMI ≥ ___”?
back: BMI ≥ 32

front: In Postoperative Acute Renal Failure, what value(s) complete this note: “High risk (MACE > ___) or emergency surgery”?
back: High risk (MACE > 1%) or emergency surgery

front: In Postoperative Acute Renal Failure, what value(s) complete this note: “Emergency = life or limb threatened unless in OR within ___ hours”?
back: Emergency = life or limb threatened unless in OR within 6 hours

front: In Aortic Stenosis, what should be recalled about Clinical symptoms (classic triad)?
back: Syncope, angina and dyspnea

front: In Aortic Stenosis, what should be recalled about Maximal aortic jet (peak) velocity?
back: > 4 m/s

front: In Aortic Stenosis, what does Measured by continuous wave doppler (CWD) of LVOT indicate or lead to?
back: Velocity time integral (VTI) and Bernoulli equation of spectral tracings → final value

front: In Aortic Stenosis, what should be recalled about Mean aortic valve gradient?
back: > 40 mmHg

front: In Aortic Stenosis, what should be recalled about Aortic valve area?
In Aortic Stenosis, what should be recalled about Derived from three variables?
back: LVOT VTI, LVOT diameter and aortic valve VT

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Internal carotid artery stump pressures indicate or lead to?
back: Detects hypoperfusion

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Jugular bulb venous monitoring indicate or lead to?
back: Cerebral oxygenation monitoring

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Transcranial doppler ultrasonography indicate or lead to?
back: Detects thromboembolic events

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what should be recalled about Measure?
back: Blood flow velocities, brain emboli, shunt function/malfunction, and detect asymptomatic carotid artery occlusion and/or hyperperfusion syndrome

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what distinction applies to Can detect ischemic changes?
back: Not specifically thromboembolic events

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what should be recalled about Subcortex = below the cortex?
back: Internal capsule, thalamus, basal ganglia, brainstem, cerebellum

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Regional cerebral blood flow indicate or lead to?
back: Detects ischemic changes

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Measured by injecting radioactive xenon into ICA indicate or lead to?
back: Measuring decay over ipsilateral cortex

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what value(s) complete this note: “Critical rCBF of anesthesia with N O and isoflurane/sevoflurane is ___ mL/___ g/min”?
back: Critical rCBF of anesthesia with N O and isoflurane/sevoflurane is 10 mL/100 g/min

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what value(s) complete this note: “Normal CBF is around ___ mL/___ g/min”?
back: Normal CBF is around 50 mL/100 g/min

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEP) indicate or lead to?
back: Detects ischemia

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what value(s) complete this note: “Ischemia ⇒ ___ decrease in amplitude and/or ___ increase in latency”?
back: Ischemia ⇒ 50% decrease in amplitude and/or 10% increase in latency

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Volatiles indicate or lead to?
back: Dose dependent decrease in amplitude and increase in latency

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Propofol indicate or lead to?
back: Relatively unchanged SSEP compared to volatiles, but can cause some decrease

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what does Opioids indicate or lead to?
back: Minimal effect on SSEP

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what should be recalled about Summary?
back: Modalities for detecting →

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what should be recalled about Thromboembolic events?
back: Transcranial doppler ultrasonography

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what should be recalled about Subcortical ischemia?
back: SSEP

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what should be recalled about Global hypoperfusion?
back: ICA stump pressure, jugular S v O , EEG

front: In CNS Monitoring During Carotid Endarterectomy, what should be recalled about Regional hypoperfusion?
back: RCBF

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what distinction applies to RV has a higher systolic pressure?
back: End-diastolic pressures are similar to RA

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what distinction applies to PA wedge pressure (LA) looks similar to RA tracing?
back: The a-c and v waves appear later in the cardiac cycle

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what does A = left Atrial contraction indicate or lead to?
back: End-diastole

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what does C = LV Contraction indicate or lead to?
back: MV bulging → systole

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what should be recalled about Three main risks to remember?
back: Arrhythmias (most common), PA rupture (most feared), infection

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what does Most common: arrhythmia indicate or lead to?
back: Classically RBBB but LBBB can occur too

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what should be recalled about Rarely?
back: Ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what should be recalled about Most feared complication?
back: Pulmonary artery rupture

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what does Incidence: 0.02 - 2% indicate or lead to?
back: Mortality rate of 50%

front: What are the risk factors of Complications?
back: Hypothermia (↑ catheter stiffness), anticoagulation, age, pulmonary HTN

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what should be recalled about Hallmark?
back: Hemoptysis or hemothorax

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what should be recalled about Diagnosis?
back: CXR has poor sensitivity, may need pulmonary angiography

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what does Infection indicate or lead to?
back: Right-sided endocarditis

front: In Pulmonary Artery Catheterization, what value(s) complete this note: “Risk same as central line insertion if heparin-coated (___x increased risk if not heparin-coated)”?

front: In Gas Cylinders, what should be recalled about Nitrous Oxide?

front: In Ischemic Optic Neuropathy (ION), what does No optic disc edema at onset of symptoms indicate or lead to?

front: In Magnesium in Pregnancy, what does Standard treatment for preeclampsia indicate or lead to?

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Clinical?

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Associated symptoms?
back: Nausea, vomiting, neck pain, dizziness, tinnitus, diplopia, hearing loss

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what does Cause: CSF leak indicate or lead to?

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what value(s) complete this note: “Spontaneous resolution usually occurs within ___ days in the majority (___) of cases”?
back: Spontaneous resolution usually occurs within 7 days in the majority (72%) of cases

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Age?
back: More frequent in younger patients

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Gender?
back: More frequent in female patients

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Pregnancy?
back: More frequent in pregnancy

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Obesity?
back: Less frequent in obese patients

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Needle size?
back: More frequent with larger needle (smaller gauge)

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Needle bevel?
back: Less frequent when bevel is placed parallel to the longitudinal axis of spine

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Needle type?
back: Less frequent with pencil point (compared to beveled, cutting needles)

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Dural puncture?
back: More frequent with more punctures

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Experience of provider?
back: Less frequent with more experienced providers

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Loss of resistance technique?

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what should be recalled about Conservative management?

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what does Epidural blood patch: most effective treatment indicate or lead to?

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what value(s) complete this note: “Perform ___ hours after dural puncture and after symptoms development (not prophylactically)”?
back: Perform 24 hours after dural puncture and after symptoms development (not prophylactically)

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what value(s) complete this note: “Symptom relief within ___ hrs in ___ of patients”?
back: Symptom relief within 24 hrs in 90% of patients

front: In Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH), what value(s) complete this note: “___ of patients who fail an initial blood patch respond to a second patch (___h after first patch)”?
back: 90% of patients who fail an initial blood patch respond to a second patch (24-48h after first patch)

back: Noncardiovascular medical disease (renal, immunologic, hematologic, etc.) - 14.5%

front: In Pregnancy and Maternal Mortality (CDC 2011-2013 Data), what value(s) complete this note: “Infection or sepsis, ___”?
back: Infection or sepsis, 12.7%

front: In Pregnancy and Maternal Mortality (CDC 2011-2013 Data), what value(s) complete this note: “Hemorrhage, ___”?
back: Hemorrhage, 11.4%

front: In Pregnancy and Maternal Mortality (CDC 2011-2013 Data), what value(s) complete this note: “Cardiomyopathy, ___”?
back: Cardiomyopathy, 11.0%

front: In Pregnancy and Maternal Mortality (CDC 2011-2013 Data), what value(s) complete this note: “Thrombotic pulmonary embolism, ___”?
back: Thrombotic pulmonary embolism, 9.2%

front: In Pregnancy and Maternal Mortality (CDC 2011-2013 Data), what value(s) complete this note: “Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, ___”?
back: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, 7.4%

front: In Pregnancy and Maternal Mortality (CDC 2011-2013 Data), what value(s) complete this note: “Cerebrovascular accidents, ___”?
back: Cerebrovascular accidents, 6.6%

back: Amniotic fluid embolism, 5.5%

front: In Pregnancy and Maternal Mortality (CDC 2011-2013 Data), what value(s) complete this note: “Anesthesia complications, ___.”?
back: Anesthesia complications, 0.2%.

front: In Physiologic Changes in Active Labor, what value(s) complete this note: “Cardiac output increases (___ of pre-pregnancy values)”?
back: Cardiac output increases (100% of pre-pregnancy values)

front: In Physiologic Changes in Active Labor, what does Minute ventilation increases indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperventilation may cause left-shift of oxyhemoglobin curve

front: In Management on Neonatal Bradycardia, what does HR < 100 bpm indicate or lead to?

front: In Management on Neonatal Bradycardia, what does Most common cause of bradycardia is hypoxia indicate or lead to?

front: In Management on Neonatal Bradycardia, what does HR < 60 bpm (for > 30s despite ventilation) indicate or lead to?

front: In Amniotic Fluid Embolism, what does Sudden, peripartum cardiogenic shock + pulmonary edema + coagulopathy indicate or lead to?

front: In Amniotic Fluid Embolism, what should be recalled about Clinical presentation?

front: In Amniotic Fluid Embolism, what does Second stage: left heart dysfunction indicate or lead to?

front: In Amniotic Fluid Embolism, what does Maternal coagulopathy (unclear etiology): consumptive coagulopathy (DIC) indicate or lead to?

front: In Amniotic Fluid Embolism, what does Treatment: supportive indicate or lead to?

front: In Amniotic Fluid Embolism, what should be recalled about Labs?
back: CBC, fibrin degradation products, PT/INR, aPTT, PA catheter aspirate stain for fetal squamous cells

front: In Uterine Blood Flow (UBF), what does No autoregulation mechanism indicate or lead to?

front: In Uterine Blood Flow (UBF), what does Uterine contractions indicate or lead to?

front: In Uterine Blood Flow (UBF), what does Systemic hypotension indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased uterine arterial pressure → decreased UBF

front: In Uterine Blood Flow (UBF), what value(s) complete this note: “Aortocaval compression, hypovolemia, regional blockade, hemorrhage, Mg ___+ , oxytocin”?
back: Aortocaval compression, hypovolemia, regional blockade, hemorrhage, Mg 2+ , oxytocin

front: In Uterine Blood Flow (UBF), what does Uterine vasoconstriction indicate or lead to?

front: In Uterine Blood Flow (UBF), what value(s) complete this note: “Can be caused by ɑ-agonists, severe hypocapnia (P CO < ___ mmHg), preeclampsia”?

front: In Uterine Blood Flow (UBF), what does Tocolytics indicate or lead to?

front: In Phenylephrine, what does High dose: increased afterload indicate or lead to?

front: In Phenylephrine, what does Arterial and venous constriction indicate or lead to?

front: What is the onset in Phenylephrine?

front: What is the duration in Phenylephrine?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what value(s) complete this note: “Converted to morphine by CYP2D6”?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what does Many polymorphisms with varying response to codeine indicate or lead to?
back: Avoid in children

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what should be recalled about For example?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what should be recalled about CYP2D6 inhibitors?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what should be recalled about Preferred?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what should be recalled about Second-line?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what should be recalled about Third-line?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what should be recalled about Avoid?

front: In Post-Tonsillectomy Pain Control, what does Bleeding (8%) indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what should be recalled about Two isomers?

front: In Ketamine, what does Onset: rapid (due to high lipid solubility and low [13%] protein binding) indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what should be recalled about Wears off?

front: In Ketamine, what should be recalled about Metabolism?

front: In Ketamine, what does Cardiovascular: sympathomimetic indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does In catecholamine depleted states indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does Increases CMRO and CBF indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does Increased EEG activity (theta waves and pseudo-seizure activity) indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does Acts at phencyclidine site of NMDA-R indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does Unpleasant psychological reactions indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does Minimal effects indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does Bronchodilation (indirect mechanisms) indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what should be recalled about Mechanism (3)?
back: Β agonism (by catecholamines), some vagolysis, histamine antagonism

front: In Ketamine, what does Increased salivation indicate or lead to?
back: Minimized by antisialogogue/anticholinergic like glycopyrrolate

front: In Ketamine, what does Pheochromocytoma indicate or lead to?

front: In Ketamine, what does Chronically Ill patients indicate or lead to?

front: What is the elderly age (mac increases after birth, in neonates, and then decreases - exception in Factors Affecting MAC?
back: Sevoflurane)

front: In Factors Affecting MAC, what does Hypothermia indicate or lead to?
back: Already sleepy

front: In Factors Affecting MAC, what does Anemia indicate or lead to?
back: Already sleepy

front: In Factors Affecting MAC, what does Hypercarbia/hypoxemia indicate or lead to?
back: Already sleepy

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Doxorubicin: intercalates DNA indicate or lead to?

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Cardiotoxicity indicate or lead to?
back: Heart failure → avoid excess fluid administration

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what value(s) complete this note: “Cardiomyopathy can also seen with ___-FU and cyclophosphamide”?
back: Cardiomyopathy can also seen with 5-FU and cyclophosphamide

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Bleomycin: glycopeptides that complex with iron indicate or lead to?

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Pulmonary toxicity indicate or lead to?

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Cyclophosphamide indicate or lead to?

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Hemorrhagic cystitis indicate or lead to?
back: Give with mesna to decrease side effect

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does ✪ Pseudocholinesterase inhibitor indicate or lead to?
back: Prolonged succinylcholine activity

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Methotrexate indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Myelosuppressant indicate or lead to?
back: Give leucovorin “rescue” → use strict aseptic technique

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Vincristine indicate or lead to?
back: Binds tubulin → inhibits cell division

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Neurotoxicity indicate or lead to?
back: “stocking and glove” peripheral neuropathy

front: In Antineoplastic agents, what does Cyclosporine and tacrolimus (Calcineurin inhibitors) indicate or lead to?
back: Nephrotoxic

front: In Cerebral Aneurysm Clipping, what does Avoid increases in transmural pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Increased risk of aneurysm rupture

front: In Cerebral Aneurysm Clipping, what should be recalled about Adequate brain relaxation + reduce intracranial volume?
back: Hyperventilation, mannitol

front: In Cerebral Aneurysm Clipping, what does “Adenosine arrest” indicate or lead to?
back: Temporary circulation arrest for 30-45 seconds to facilitate clipping

front: In Cerebral Aneurysm Clipping, what value(s) complete this note: “Ventilation with ___ oxygen to improve delivery”?
back: Ventilation with 100% oxygen to improve delivery

front: In Cerebral Aneurysm Clipping, what value(s) complete this note: “Cooling to ___°C”?
back: Cooling to 33°C

front: In Cerebral Aneurysm Clipping, what does Induced coma indicate or lead to?
back: Decrease cerebral metabolic requirements → propofol or thiopental

front: In Nonshivering Thermogenesis, what value(s) complete this note: “Most important in neonates and infants (importance decreases with age - around ___ months)”?
back: Most important in neonates and infants (importance decreases with age - around 3 months)

front: In Nonshivering Thermogenesis, what does Uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation indicate or lead to?
back: Heat generated instead of ATP

front: In Nonshivering Thermogenesis, what should be recalled about Triggered by?
back: Norepinephrine, thyroxine and glucocorticoids

front: In Nonshivering Thermogenesis, what does Sympathetic stimulation indicate or lead to?
back: Norepinephrine (but not epinephrine) → brown fat → lipolysis → free fatty acids → substrates for heat generation

front: In Nonshivering Thermogenesis, what should be recalled about Inhibited by?
back: Inhalational anesthetics and β-blockers (inhibits sympathetic stimulation)

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does Tracheal compression from hematoma formation indicate or lead to?
back: Remove sutures first → manage airway

front: In Thyroidectomy, what value(s) complete this note: “Usually happens within ___ hours postoperatively”?
back: Usually happens within 24 hours postoperatively

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does PTH indicate or lead to?
back: Increases Ca 2+ absorption from bone, renal and GI (Vitamin D activation to 1,25-OH)

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does Parathyroid damaged during surgery indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased Ca 2+ → hypocalcemia 24-94h later

front: How does Major complications present?
back: Circumoral paresthesias, tetany, carpopedal spasm, laryngospasm

front: How is Major complications management handled?
back: Vitamin D (1,25-OH aka calcitriol) and calcium supplementation

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does Bilateral RLN damage indicate or lead to?
back: Unopposed tension of cricothyroid muscle (innervated by superior laryngeal nerve) → adducted vocal cords → stridor → acute respiratory distress → hypoxic respiratory drive → hyperventilation → initial respiratory alkalosis

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does Then patient starts to tire out indicate or lead to?
back: Respiratory acidosis

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does Neural mapping: RLN stimulated indicate or lead to?
back: Response monitored by special EMG tracheal tube with sensing pads where the vocal cords lie

front: In Thyroidectomy, what should be recalled about Direct visualization?
back: By the surgeon or by using a fiberoptic scope through an LMA

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does MEPs: stimulate motor cortex indicate or lead to?
back: Monitor peripheral nerve response → cannot monitor RLN

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does Unilateral RLN damage indicate or lead to?
back: Unopposed tension of ipsilateral cricothyroid muscle → difficulty with phonation, but no respiratory distress

front: In Thyroidectomy, what does Bilateral external superior laryngeal nerve (SLN) damage indicate or lead to?
back: Innervates cricothyroid muscle → no respiratory distress → hoarseness and tiring of voice

front: In Postintubation Croup, what does Causes: subglottic injury and edema indicate or lead to?
back: Dexamethasone up to 10 mg/kg can prevent this

front: In Postintubation Croup, what should be recalled about Associated with?
back: Large ETT, over inflated cuffs, or traumatic intubation, Trisomy 21 (subglottic stenosis)

front: In Postintubation Croup, what should be recalled about Mild?
back: Cool, humidified mist

front: In Postintubation Croup, what does Moderate-severe: nebulized racemic epinephrine over 5-10 minutes indicate or lead to?
back: 4-5 hours of monitoring for rebound effects, dexamethasone IV

front: In Postintubation Croup, what does If refractory, may need emergency airway indicate or lead to?
back: In pediatric patients transtracheal jet ventilation is preferred over cricothyroidotomy

front: How does Hyperparathyroidism present?
back: “stones, bones, moans and psychiatric overtones”

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what should be recalled about Causes?
back: Singular parathyroid adenoma, hyperplasia, malignancy, or chronic renal insufficiency

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does Renal insufficiency indicate or lead to?
back: Chronically ↓ Vit D (→ ↓ Ca 2+ absorption) + ↑ PO → secondary ↑ PTH

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does Increases osteoclast activity indicate or lead to?
back: Increased Ca 2+

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does There is also PO - release from bone indicate or lead to?
back: PTH counteracts this by renal excretion of PO -

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does Increases renal tubular calcium reabsorption indicate or lead to?
back: Increased Ca 2+

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does Increases activation of Vitamin D indicate or lead to?
back: 1,25-OH-Vitamin D → increased GI Ca 2+ absorption

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does Acid-base disorders (↓ HCO - , ↑ Cl - indicate or lead to?
back: Acidosis)

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does High PTH indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibits renal bicarbonate reabsorption → metabolic acidosis

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what does PTH inhibits chloride excretion indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperchloremic acidosis → normal anion gap

front: In Hyperparathyroidism, what should be recalled about Example?
back: PH 7.3, P CO 38 (low-ish), Bicarbonate 20 (low), Anion Gap 12 (normal)

front: In Capnography, what does Mainstream sampling: infrared sensor in main breathing circuit indicate or lead to?
back: No delay

front: In Capnography, what does Sidestream sampling: 6ft tube indicate or lead to?
back: Infrared sensor → 1-4 second delay

front: In Capnography, what value(s) complete this note: “P CO is normally ___ mmHg less than P CO due to alveolar dead space (not anatomic dead space)”?
back: P CO is normally 5 mmHg less than P CO due to alveolar dead space (not anatomic dead space)

front: In Capnography, what does A-B indicate or lead to?
back: Phase 1 → beginning of expiration → expired CO is filling anatomic dead space

front: In Capnography, what does B-C indicate or lead to?
back: Phase 2 → sharp upstroke → exhaled alveolar CO reaches expiratory limb

front: In Capnography, what does C-D indicate or lead to?
back: Phase 3 → expiratory alveolar plateau → continued CO exhalation

front: In Capnography, what does D indicate or lead to?
back: End-tidal CO

front: In Capnography, what does D-E indicate or lead to?
back: Phase 0 → sharp downstroke → inspiration

front: In Capnography, what does Increased upslope of phase III (plateau) Bready,Nunn's indicate or lead to?
back: Bronchospasm or obstruction

front: In Capnography, what should be recalled about Note?
back: Some sources 1 say phase II is prolonged, and phase III is shortened

front: In Capnography, what should be recalled about Gravenstein?
back: Phase II slope is decreased and phase III slope is increased

front: In Capnography, what does Cardiogenic oscillations indicate or lead to?
back: At the end of exhalation

front: In Capnography, what does Clefts during phase III indicate or lead to?
back: Spontaneous breathing efforts during controlled mechanical ventilation

front: In Capnography, what does Rebreathing of CO indicate or lead to?
back: Faulty expiratory valve or exhausted absorber system (inspiratory CO > 0)

front: In Capnography, what does Faulty inspiratory valve indicate or lead to?
back: Slower downslope, extends into inhalation phase as CO is rebreathed

front: In Capnography, what does Two peaks in phase III indicate or lead to?
back: Single-lung transplant in COPD

front: In Capnography, what does Faulty expiratory valve indicate or lead to?
back: Elevated F I CO baseline from rebreathing CO from expiratory circuit

front: In Capnography, what does Sudden shortening of phase III indicate or lead to?
back: Abrupt onset of ruptured/leaking tube cuff

front: In Capnography, what does Dual plateau in phase III indicate or lead to?
back: Leak in sidestream sample line

front: In Bland-Altman Plot, what should be recalled about In contrast to?
back: Pearson coefficient which measures correlation (r, r 2 , and P-value)

front: In Bland-Altman Plot, what does Can measure correlation between two different variables indicate or lead to?
back: Bland-Altman measures agreement between the same variables

front: In Bland-Altman Plot, what should be recalled about Values obtained?
back: Bias and range of agreement

front: In Bland-Altman Plot, what should be recalled about Bias?
back: Deviation of the new test measurements from the gold-standard measurements

front: In Bland-Altman Plot, what should be recalled about Calculated as?
back: Test - Test (i.e., if Test is larger, bias will be negative)

front: In Bland-Altman Plot, what should be recalled about Range of agreement?
back: Limits within which 95% of the differences reside (2 standard deviations)

front: In Bland-Altman Plot, what should be recalled about Must be interpreted clinically (e.g. BP range 10 is fine, but [K + ] range 10 is not acceptable) Source?
back: Bach, Aaron JE, et al. PloS one 10.2 (2015): e0117907.

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what value(s) complete this note: “Cardiac defects (___ incidence)”?
back: Cardiac defects (40-50% incidence)

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what value(s) complete this note: “___ incidence of ASD/VSD”?
back: 40% incidence of ASD/VSD

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what value(s) complete this note: “___ incidence of endocardial cushion defects”?
back: 50% incidence of endocardial cushion defects

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what does Atlantoaxial instability: careful with neck flexion or extension indicate or lead to?
back: Some reports of subluxation

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what does Smaller trachea (may need smaller ETT), subglottic stenosis indicate or lead to?
back: Higher risk postoperative stridor

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what does Adenotonsillar hypertrophy indicate or lead to?
back: OSA

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what should be recalled about Respiratory?
back: Frequent infections, OSA, pulmonary hypertension

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what does Cognitively challenged indicate or lead to?
back: May require more sedation or parental assistance during induction

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what should be recalled about Ophthalmic?
back: Cataracts, narrow-angle glaucoma

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what should be recalled about Endocrine?
back: Thyroid hypofunction, diabetes

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what should be recalled about Heme?
back: Polycythemia (may need phlebotomy), leukemia develops in 0.7% of children

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what should be recalled about GI/GU?
back: Duodenal atresia, umbilical hernia, renal malformations, undescended testis, hypospadias

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what does Radial artery abnormalities (i.e., single median artery) are common indicate or lead to?
back: Vascular access can be difficult

front: In Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21), what does Hypotonia indicate or lead to?
back: May require less muscle relaxation

front: In Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS), what should be recalled about Triad?
back: Intrapulmonary vascular dilatations (IPVD), increased A-a gradient, end-stage liver disease

front: In Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS), what does Pathophysiology: excess vasodilators (NO) indicate or lead to?
back: IPVD formation → V/Q mismatch (increased perfusion relative to ventilation → shunt physiology) → hypoxia

front: In Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS), what does Orthodeoxia: standing indicate or lead to?
back: Worsened V/Q mismatch (gravity → more perfusion to the base of the lungs → already has low ventilation, now more perfusion → more shunt → hypoxia on standing)

front: In Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS), what should be recalled about Diagnosis?
back: Room-air ABG with hypoxia + increased A-a gradient + contrast-enhanced echo with IPVD

front: In Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS), what value(s) complete this note: “Strong predictor of perioperative mortality after liver transplant (PaO < ___ on room air)”?
back: Strong predictor of perioperative mortality after liver transplant (PaO < 50 on room air)

front: In Pulmonary Metabolism of Hormones, what does Converts angiotensin I indicate or lead to?
back: Angiotensin II (active)

front: In Pulmonary Metabolism of Hormones, what does Converts (inactivates) bradykinin indicate or lead to?
back: Inactive form

front: In Pulmonary Metabolism of Hormones, what should be recalled about COMT?
back: Breakdown of norepinephrine

front: In Pulmonary Metabolism of Hormones, what should be recalled about MAOI?
back: Breakdown of serotonin and norepinephrine

front: In Pulmonary Metabolism of Hormones, what should be recalled about Hormones not metabolized by the lungs?
back: Dopamine, epinephrine, histamine

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what value(s) complete this note: “Definition of AKI (___; Acute Kidney Injury Network)”?
back: Definition of AKI (2004; Acute Kidney Injury Network)

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what value(s) complete this note: “Increase in creatinine of ≥ ___ mg/dL or ≥___ (___x) from baseline, or”?
back: Increase in creatinine of ≥ 0.3 mg/dL or ≥50% (1.5x) from baseline, or

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what should be recalled about Oliguria?
back: < 0.5 mL/kg/h for >6 h

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what should be recalled about Three causes of azotemia?
back: Prerenal, intrinsic/renal, postrenal

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does Postrenal azotemia: outflow obstruction indicate or lead to?
back: Diagnosed by imaging/exam (labs variable) → reversible

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does Prerenal azotemia: from hypoperfusion of the kidney indicate or lead to?
back: If untreated, progresses to AKI

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does FeNa < 1% & U < 20 indicate or lead to?
back: Kidneys able to reabsorb Na to try and maintain volume/perfusion

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does U > 500 indicate or lead to?
back: Urine is concentrated (volume is reabsorbed)

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what should be recalled about Causes?
back: Low BP (CO, volume) or high renal vascular resistance

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does Intrinsic/renal azotemia: kidneys unable to concentrate urine indicate or lead to?
In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what should be recalled about Most common causes of AKI?
back: Prerenal azotemia and ATN

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what should be recalled about Gold-standard for distinction?
back: Fluid responsiveness

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what should be recalled about Prerenal azotemia?
back: Responds (normal Cr) to fluid repletion within 1-3 days

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what should be recalled about ATN?
back: Important to avoid excess fluid administration

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does < 1% indicate or lead to?
back: Prerenal azotemia: kidney trying to retain Na + and volume

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does > 1% indicate or lead to?
back: Renal azotemia (e.g. ATN) → tubules unable to retain Na + → more Na + excreted

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does ATN indicate or lead to?
back: Tubules unable to retain Na + → > 40 mEq

front: In Azotemia and Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), what does Prerenal disease indicate or lead to?
back: Kidneys retaining Na + → < 20 mEq

front: In Sensitivity and Specificity, what does SnNOut indicate or lead to?
back: Sensitive test → Negative → ruled Out

front: In Sensitivity and Specificity, what does SpPIn indicate or lead to?
back: Specific test → Positive → ruled In

front: In Meperidine, what does Synthetic opioid indicate or lead to?
back: Acts on MOP → analgesia and ↓ postoperative shivering

front: In Meperidine, what does Causes histamine release (like morphine) indicate or lead to?
back: Hypotension

front: In Meperidine, what does Atropine-line structure indicate or lead to?
back: Anticholinergic effects → tachycardia

front: In Meperidine, what does Weak local anesthetic properties indicate or lead to?
back: Myocardial depressant (resistant to naloxone)

front: In Meperidine, what does Metabolism: liver indicate or lead to?
back: Normeperidine → further metabolism → elimination (kidneys and liver)

front: In Meperidine, what does Normeperidine: minimal analgesic, but CNS stimulant indicate or lead to?
back: Seizures

front: What is the short half-life of meperidine in Meperidine?
back: 3 hours

front: In Meperidine, what does Normeperidine: 15 hours indicate or lead to?
back: Sticks around much longer → avoid long-term meperidine use

front: In Meperidine, what does Not recommended for PCA indicate or lead to?
back: Potential for neurotoxicity

front: In Meperidine, what does In renal and liver failure indicate or lead to?
back: Prolonged half-lives of both meperidine and normeperidine

front: In Meperidine, what distinction applies to Causes increased biliary pressures (like other opioids)?
back: Cannot be reversed by naloxone

front: In Meperidine, what does Avoid with MAOI indicate or lead to?
back: Meperidine inhibits 5-HT reuptake → serotonin syndrome: fever, rigidity, seizures

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what does Activated Clotting Time (ACT) process: whole blood indicate or lead to?
back: Add activator → measure clot time

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what should be recalled about Normal?
back: Around 120 seconds

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what should be recalled about Adequate anticoagulation for bypass?
back: 400-480 seconds

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what should be recalled about Limitations?
back: 10% variability, less reliable with hypothermia and hemodilution, prolonged at >25°C

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what should be recalled about Affected by?
back: Low platelets

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what does Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT): citrated (stored) blood indicate or lead to?
back: Add reagent (containing Ca 2+ and phospholipid) → add activator of “intrinsic” or contact pathway (kaolin, celite) → measure clot time

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what should be recalled about Not affected by low platelets (unlike ACT)?
back: Phospholipid (acts like platelets) is in the aPTT reagent

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what should be recalled about Limitation?
back: Not useful for high levels of heparin used in bypass (ACT is better)

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what should be recalled about Anti-Xa?
back: Not useful for bypass ⇒ used for monitoring LMWH activity

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what does UFH: potentiates AT3 activity indicate or lead to?
back: Inactivates several clotting factors → track with aPTT

front: In Measuring Heparin Anticoagulation on Bypass, what does LMWH: potentiates AT3 activity mostly against Xa indicate or lead to?
back: Track with anti-Xa activity

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what does Autoimmune attack of myelin indicate or lead to?
back: Inflammation + reactive gliosis + axonal degeneration → multifocal areas of demyelination in the brain and spinal cord (peripheral nerves are not affected)

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what should be recalled about Clinical course?
back: Very variable (subacute with relapse, chronic, progressive, remission, exacerbations, etc.)

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what should be recalled about Diagnosis?
back: Clinical signs + radiographic evidence ± CSF oligoclonal Ig abnormalities

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what should be recalled about Therapies (management)?
back: Corticosteroids (acute), IFN-β (relapsing-remitting), glatiramer, azathioprine

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what should be recalled about Pre-op?
back: Thorough evaluation and documentation of existing neurological deficits

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what does Avoid succinylcholine indicate or lead to?
back: May cause hyperkalemia

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what distinction applies to Nondepolarizing NMBAs are safe?
back: Patients may exhibit varying sensitivity

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what distinction applies to Potential risk of increased LA toxicity from exposure to demyelinated nerves?
back: Benefits of neuraxial techniques probably outweigh risks

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what should be recalled about Avoid hyperthermia?
back: Even 1°C increase in temperature can cause exacerbation

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what should be recalled about Autonomic instability?
back: Patients may have this as part of their MS symptoms

front: In Multiple Sclerosis (MS), what value(s) complete this note: “Regardless of anesthetic technique, ___ of women have postpartum exacerbation”?
back: Regardless of anesthetic technique, 20-30% of women have postpartum exacerbation

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what value(s) complete this note: “Oxygen Content C a O = (___ x Hb x S a O ) + (___ x P a O )”?
back: Oxygen Content C a O = (1.39 x Hb x S a O ) + (0.003 x P a O )

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what value(s) complete this note: “Oxygen carried by hemoglobin = ___ x Hb x S a O”?
back: Oxygen carried by hemoglobin = 1.39 x Hb x S a O

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what does Oxygen dissolved in the blood = 0.003 x P a O ⇒ hyperbaric oxygen therapy increases this component (e.g. at 3 atm indicate or lead to?
back: Can meet tissue oxygen needs without any hemoglobin oxygen)

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Gas-bubble disease?
back: Air embolism, decompression sickness

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Poisoning, envenomation?
back: CO, CN, CCl , H S, brown recluse spider bites

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Infections?
back: Soft tissue necrotizing infections, chronic osteomyelitis, intracranial abscess, etc.

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Acute Ischemia?
back: Crush injury, compromised skin flaps, central retinal artery/vein occlusion

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Chronic ischemia?
back: Ischemic ulcers, radiation necrosis

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Acute hypoxia?
back: During lung lavage, significant blood loss if transfusion not possible

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Thermal injuries?
back: Burns

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Henry’s Law?
back: At a constant temperature, amount of gas dissolved in a liquid is equal to its partial pressure

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Boyle Law?
back: At a constant temperature for a gas, volume is inversely proportional to pressure

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what does Most common side effect is difficulty with middle ear pressure equalization indicate or lead to?
back: Ear pain, edema, bleeding, and rarely tympanic membrane rupture.

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what does First signs: pulmonary indicate or lead to?
back: Tracheobronchial irritation (cough) and chest pain

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what does Treatment indicate or lead to?
back: Immediate reduction of the inspired PO2 until the seizure stops

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what should be recalled about Pulmonary barotrauma?
back: Most likely to occur during decompression

front: In Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT), what does Areas of regional hypoventilation become overpressurized indicate or lead to?
back: Alveolar rupture

front: In Ventilatory Drive, what does Located in brain parenchyma indicate or lead to?
back: Sensitive to CSF [H + ] → increases minute ventilation

front: In Ventilatory Drive, what does Located in carotid and aortic bodies indicate or lead to?
back: Sensitive to P a O below 60-65 mmHg

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about Classic approach?
back: Acid-base status calculated based on [H + ] and [HCO - ]

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does Stewart method indicate or lead to?
back: Three independent variables: SID, A , P a CO

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about P a CO?
back: Differentiates respiratory alkalosis and acidosis

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does A : sum of weak acids and conjugate bases (AH + A - ) indicate or lead to?
back: Phosphates and proteins

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about Strong-Ion Difference (SID)?
back: Sum of all strong cations (positive) minus strong anions (negative)

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about SID simplified as?
back: (Na + + K + ) - (Cl - + lactate - )

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does Acidosis: hypoventilation indicate or lead to?
back: Increased P a CO

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does Alkalosis: hyperventilation indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased P a CO

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does Abnormal SID (normal SID = 40) ⇒ ↓ SID indicate or lead to?
back: Acidosis

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does Water excess indicate or lead to?
In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does Other ions indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ [UMA - ] (unmeasured anions)

front: In Stewart Method of Acid-Base Disorders, what does Increased [albumin], [P ] indicate or lead to?
back: Metabolic acidosis

front: In Inferential Statistics, what should be recalled about Null hypothesis (H )?
back: No difference between the variables in question (e.g. treatment vs. placebo)

front: In Inferential Statistics, what should be recalled about Alternative hypothesis (H )?
back: Difference exists (e.g. treatment is better than placebo)

front: In Inferential Statistics, what does Type 1: H incorrectly rejected (you find treatment works, when it really doesn’t) indicate or lead to?
back: More serious

front: In Inferential Statistics, what does Probability of making a Type I error = ɑ ⇒ usually 0.05 (5%) indicate or lead to?
back: Represents false-positives

front: In Inferential Statistics, what should be recalled about Type 2?
back: H incorrectly accepted (you find treatment doesn’t work, when it really does)

front: In Inferential Statistics, what value(s) complete this note: “Power (ability to detect significant difference) = ___-β ⇒ ___ ways to ↑ power ⇒ ↓ type ___ error”?
back: Power (ability to detect significant difference) = 1-β ⇒ 4 ways to ↑ power ⇒ ↓ type 2 error

front: In Inferential Statistics, what does Increase ɑ indicate or lead to?
back: But this increases probability type 1 error → not a good option

front: In Inferential Statistics, what does Increase sample size indicate or lead to?
back: Most easy to control in study design

front: In Inferential Statistics, what does Make the difference between variables greater indicate or lead to?
back: Clinical relevance Reality: difference exists (H ) Reality: no difference (H ) Study: difference exists (H rejected) True-positive (Power = 1-β) False-positive (Type I error; ɑ) Study: no difference (H accepted) False-negative (Type II error; β) True-negative

front: In Inferential Statistics, what does Increasing ɑ indicate or lead to?
back: Increased power (1-β) → lower chance of Type II error (false-negative)

front: In Inferential Statistics, what does Increasing β indicate or lead to?
back: Decrease power → less chance of finding a true difference (true-positive)

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what should be recalled about Normal ICP?
back: 5-13 mmHg

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what should be recalled about Three components involved in ICP?
back: Brain parenchyma (80%), blood (10%), and CSF (10%)

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what should be recalled about Common monitoring sites?
back: Intraventricular, intraparenchymal, subarachnoid, subdural, epidural

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does Intraventricular indicate or lead to?
back: Gold-standard, most reliable, allows CSF drainage, but more complications

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does P1 indicate or lead to?
back: “percussion wave” → arterial pulsation

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does P2 indicate or lead to?
back: “tidal wave” → intracranial compliance

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does P3 indicate or lead to?
back: “dicrotic wave” → aortic valve closure

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what should be recalled about Normal?
In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what value(s) complete this note: “Decreased intracranial compliance ⇒ P2 > P1 and P3”?
back: Decreased intracranial compliance ⇒ P2 > P1 and P3

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does Three types of waves in ICP monitoring: A, B and C indicate or lead to?
back: Only A is clinically significant

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does A = plateau waves indicate or lead to?
back: Sudden, rapid ICP elevation to > 50 mmHg for 5-20 minutes

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does Decreased CPP (low MAP) indicate or lead to?
back: Vasodilation ⇒

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does Rise in P a CO indicate or lead to?
back: Vasodilation ⇒

front: In Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring, what does ⇒ increased CBF and CBV indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased intracranial compliance → increased ICP

front: How is Three types of waves in ICP monitoring: A, B and C → only A is clinically significant treatment handled?
back: Try to address the root cause (increase MAP, hyperventilate) and treat ICP

front: In Factor V Leiden, what does Hereditary procoagulant disorder indicate or lead to?
back: Resistance to activated protein C (APC)

front: In Factor V Leiden, what does APC ⇒ natural anticoagulant indicate or lead to?
back: Cleaves and inactivates factor V and factor VIII

front: In Factor V Leiden, what does Clinical manifestations indicate or lead to?
back: Highly variable

front: In Factor V Leiden, what should be recalled about Less commonly?
back: Venous thrombosis (DVT, PE)

front: In Factor V Leiden, what should be recalled about Initial management?
back: Anticoagulation for 3-6 months (INR goal 2-3)

front: In Factor V Leiden, what does >1 episodes or high-risk thrombosis (e.g. PE or cerebral) indicate or lead to?
back: Lifelong anticoagulation (INR goal 2-3)

front: In Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), what value(s) complete this note: “Typically occurs in type ___ diabetics when they are under stress or deficient in insulin”?
back: Typically occurs in type 1 diabetics when they are under stress or deficient in insulin

front: In Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), what does Low insulin indicate or lead to?
back: Excess glucagon → gluconeogenesis → hyperglycemia

front: In Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), what does Ketoacidemia indicate or lead to?
back: Excess β-hydroxybutyrate (measure this to track ketoacidemia)

front: In Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), what should be recalled about First step?
back: Aggressive fluid replacement to correct hypovolemia and hyperosmolality

front: In Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), what does Then, IV insulin indicate or lead to?
back: Correct hyperglycemia ⇒ if too rapid (>100 mg/dL/hr) → cerebral edema

front: In Local Anesthetic Allergy, what should be recalled about Two classes of local anesthetics (LA)?
back: Amides and esters

front: In Local Anesthetic Allergy, what does Esters are derivatives of PABA indicate or lead to?
back: More likely to be allergenic

front: In Local Anesthetic Allergy, what does Methylparaben, a preservative used in local anesthetics, is metabolized to PABA indicate or lead to?
back: Can cause allergy

front: In Local Anesthetic Allergy, what should be recalled about In recent literature?
back: More reports of amide allergy because of increased preference for them and increase of PABA-free skin products ⇒ however, the likelihood of allergic reaction to esters is higher

front: In Liver Ischemia-Reperfusion Injury, what does Pre-anhepatic stage: incision indicate or lead to?
back: Cross-clamping of major vessels of liver

front: In Liver Ischemia-Reperfusion Injury, what does Anhepatic stage: cross-clamping indicate or lead to?
back: Anastomosis → reperfusion

front: In Liver Ischemia-Reperfusion Injury, what does Neo-hepatic stage: reperfusion indicate or lead to?
back: Hepatic a., biliary duct anastomosis → end of surgery

front: In Liver Ischemia-Reperfusion Injury, what does Ischemia indicate or lead to?
back: Hepatocyte necrosis/apoptosis → Depletion of ATP and glycogen → Na/K pump failure → alteration of ion gradients → cellular edema → buildup of ischemic byproducts

front: In Liver Ischemia-Reperfusion Injury, what does ischemic byproducts released into circulation indicate or lead to?
back: Hypotension, arrhythmias

topic: Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis
front: In Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis, what does For each 1°C drop in temperature indicate or lead to?
back: 0.017 increase in pH (hypothermia → alkalosis)

topic: Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis
front: In Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis, what does E.g. patient’s temperature is 27°C indicate or lead to?
back: ABG shows pH 7.25 @ 37°C → pH is actually 7.42 @ 27°C

topic: Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis
front: In Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis, what does As temperature increases indicate or lead to?
back: Solubility of a gas decreases (inversely proportional) → more gas in gaseous phase → partial pressure increases

topic: Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis
front: In Temperature and Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Analysis, what does E.g. Hypothermic patient at 27°C indicate or lead to?
back: P a CO measured to be 55 mmHg @ 37°C → real P a CO (at 27°C) will be lower than 55 mmHg

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what value(s) complete this note: “___ are benign, ___ have hypertension, ___ originate in one adrenal medulla”?
back: 90% are benign, 90% have hypertension, 90% originate in one adrenal medulla

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what should be recalled about Clinical triad?
back: Diaphoresis + tachycardia + headache

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what does Main problem: hypertension indicate or lead to?
back: Cardiac (MI, CHF) and neurological sequelae (stroke)

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what should be recalled about MEN 2A?
back: Medullary thyroid cancer + pheochromocytoma + parathyroid tumors

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what should be recalled about MEN 2B?
back: Medullary thyroid cancer + pheochromocytoma + neurofibromas

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what should be recalled about Von-Hippel Lindau?
back: CNS hemangioblastomas + pheochromocytoma (10-25%)

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what value(s) complete this note: “ɑ-antagonist (phenoxybenzamine, phentolamine, terazosin, etc.) ___ days prior to surgery”?
back: Ɑ-antagonist (phenoxybenzamine, phentolamine, terazosin, etc.) 10-14 days prior to surgery

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what does Phenoxybenzamine can potentially cause hypertension and tachycardia (against treatment goals): ɑ -antagonism indicate or lead to?
back: Increased norepi levels → tachycardia and HTN

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what does Typically hypovolemic from long-standing hypertension indicate or lead to?
back: Volume repletion

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what value(s) complete this note: “Add beta-blockers only after ɑ-blockade is achieved (at least ___ hours)”?
back: Add beta-blockers only after ɑ-blockade is achieved (at least 48 hours)

front: In Pheochromocytoma, what does Otherwise indicate or lead to?
back: Unopposed ɑ-agonism → hypertensive crisis

front: In Milrinone, what does Mechanism: selective PDE inhibitor indicate or lead to?
back: Impaired cAMP breakdown → increased cAMP

front: In Milrinone, what does Cardiac muscle indicate or lead to?
back: Increase Ca 2+ influx → increased inotropy (contractility)

front: In Milrinone, what does Cardiac muscle indicate or lead to?
back: Increase lusitropy (relaxation)

front: In Milrinone, what does Smooth muscle indicate or lead to?
back: Vasodilation → decreased PVR and pulmonary vasodilation

front: In Milrinone, what should be recalled about CV?
back: Hypertension (13%), hypotension (20%), bradycardia (13%), tachycardia (8%), new afib (5%)

front: In Milrinone, what should be recalled about Main limitations of short-term use?
back: Risk of hypotension and ventricular dysrhythmias

front: In Milrinone, what does Excretion: urine (unconjugated form) indicate or lead to?
back: Adjusted dose in renal failure (lower doses)

front: In Milrinone, what should be recalled about + + ⇒ Figure?
back: Decreased afterload + increased inotropy + increased lusitropy ⇒ Milrinone PV loop

front: In Milrinone, what does Positive lusitropy: Reduction in slope of the diastolic filling phase and rightward shift indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased LVEDP’ → Increased CPP (CPP = AoDP - LVEDP)

front: In Milrinone, what does Increased contractility (positive inotropy) indicate or lead to?
back: Increased SV

front: In Milrinone, what does AoSP’ decreased due to vasodilation indicate or lead to?
back: Increased SV

front: In Milrinone, what does Cilostazol: PDE inhibitor indicate or lead to?
back: Arterial vasodilation → used to treat peripheral artery disease (claudication)

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) indicate or lead to?
back: Converted to salicylic acid → absorbed from the gut

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Interrupts oxidative phosphorylation indicate or lead to?
back: Buildup of lactate → metabolic acidosis

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Directly stimulates respiratory center indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperventilation → respiratory alkalosis

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does In children, acute toxicity can lead to lethargy indicate or lead to?
back: Blunts respiration → mostly acidosis

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what should be recalled about Sample blood gas?
In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Respiratory alkalosis indicate or lead to?
back: Low pCO (25)

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Metabolic acidosis indicate or lead to?
back: Low HCO (16)

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Mixed acidosis + alkalosis indicate or lead to?
back: Close to normal pH (7.43)

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what should be recalled about Clinical manifestations?
back: Headache, tinnitus (85%), nausea, vomiting, vertigo, diarrhea, tachycardia

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what should be recalled about Severe intoxication?
back: Lethargy, pulmonary edema, altered mental status, seizures, coma, GI bleeding

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Dextrose indicate or lead to?
back: Avoid CSF hypoglycemia

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does IV fluids indicate or lead to?
back: Replace losses from tachypnea and vomiting

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Raises pH indicate or lead to?
back: Decreases tissue distribution of salicylate

front: In Salicylate Poisoning, what does Raises urinary pH indicate or lead to?
back: Increases rate of salicylate clearance

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what does Atrial pacing (left image): pacing spike indicate or lead to?
back: Single P wave → normal PR and QRS (assuming no other defects)

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what does Ventricular pacing: single pacing spike indicate or lead to?
back: Wide QRS complex

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what does Most common lead location: RV apex indicate or lead to?
back: Looks like LBBB because RV activated before LV

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what does Dual chamber sequential AV pacing (right image): spike indicate or lead to?
back: P-wave → spike → wide QRS

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what should be recalled about Biventricular pacing (cardiac resynchronization therapy)?
back: Leads in RA, RV and coronary sinus

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what should be recalled about ECG findings?
back: Complex

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what does When ventricle is stimulated indicate or lead to?
back: Wide QRS complex

front: In Pacemakers and ECG changes, what does When RV is stimulated indicate or lead to?
back: LBBB pattern (RV activated before LV)

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what should be recalled about Three main forms?
back: Bicarbonate (90%), dissolved CO (5%), and carbamino compounds (5%)

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what does Bicarbonate indicate or lead to?
back: RBCs and vascular endothelium have carbonic anhydrase which converts CO to bicarbonate (with carbonic acid as a transient intermediate) → thus, CO is stored in the blood as bicarbonate ✪

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what should be recalled about Carbamino compounds (small amount)?
back: Hemoglobin proteins and plasma proteins

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what should be recalled about Dissolved CO?
back: CO is 10x more soluble than O

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what should be recalled about Haldane effect?
back: When O binds hemoglobin, CO is released

front: What is the mechanism of Haldane effect: when O binds hemoglobin, CO is released?
back: Two ways

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what does O binds hemoglobin indicate or lead to?
back: Conformational change → less affinity for CO → less carbamino compounds

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what does O binds hemoglobin indicate or lead to?
back: Conformational change → H + is released (deoxyhemoglobin acts as a buffer by binding H + ) → H + combines with HCO - (which was acting as CO storage) → converted to H O + CO → released into the alveoli

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what does COPD and supplemental oxygen: can worsen hypercarbia indicate or lead to?
back: Two mechanisms

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what does Impaired hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction indicate or lead to?
back: More perfusion to poorly ventilated areas (shunt) → diminished capacity to eliminate CO

front: In Carbon Dioxide Transport, what should be recalled about Haldane effect?
back: More O leads to increased CO released from hemoglobin and bicarbonate store

front: In Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE), what value(s) complete this note: “___ of patients develop DVT during surgery ⇒”?
back: 30% of patients develop DVT during surgery ⇒

front: In Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE), what value(s) complete this note: “___ will have complete resolution”?
back: 33% will have complete resolution

front: In Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE), what value(s) complete this note: “___ will have no extension”?
back: 40% will have no extension

front: In Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE), what value(s) complete this note: “___ will have proximal clots or PE”?
back: 25% will have proximal clots or PE

front: In Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE), what should be recalled about Pulmonary embolism?
back: Obstruction of pulmonary artery or its branches by thrombus, tumor, air or fat

front: In Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE), what value(s) complete this note: “Generally occurs ___ days postoperatively (but can happen up to ___ weeks)”?
back: Generally occurs 3-7 days postoperatively (but can happen up to 2 weeks)

front: How does Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE) present?
back: Dyspnea (73%), pleuritic chest pain (66%), cough (37%), hemoptysis

front: How does Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE) present?
back: Tachypnea (70%), crackles (50%), tachycardia (30%)

front: In Postoperative Pulmonary Embolism (PE), what value(s) complete this note: “Symptoms generally occur when ___ of the pulmonary arterial bed is occluded”?
back: Symptoms generally occur when 30-50% of the pulmonary arterial bed is occluded

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Trisomy 21 indicate or lead to?
back: Subglottic stenosis, macroglossia, floppy soft palate, enlarged tonsils

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what should be recalled about Treacher-Collins, Goldenhar, Pierre-Robin sequence?
back: Micrognathia

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Ankylosing spondylitis indicate or lead to?
