back: Chronic cervical and lumbar spine inflammation → early morning stiffness and low back pain → can lead to fusion of the spine

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Cervical stiffness + TMJ stiffness indicate or lead to?
back: Difficult ventilation and intubation

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Lumbar stiffness indicate or lead to?
back: Difficult neuraxial placement → increased risk of epidural hematoma and total spinal

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what value(s) complete this note: “Usually associated with HLA-B2___ (Reiter’s, ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s, psoriasis)”?
back: Usually associated with HLA-B27 (Reiter’s, ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s, psoriasis)

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Treatment: NSAIDs indicate or lead to?
back: Can lead to platelet dysfunction and bleeding

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Hypothyroidism indicate or lead to?
back: Myxedema → airway swelling

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Thyroid goiters indicate or lead to?
back: Laryngeal obstruction and deviation → stridor and respiratory compromise

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what should be recalled about Flow-volume loop?
back: Fixed upper-airway obstruction pattern (inspiratory and expiratory flattening)

front: In Co-existing Disease and Difficult Airways, what does Diabetic stiff joint syndrome indicate or lead to?
back: “prayer sign” → difficulty laryngoscopy due to impaired neck movement

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what does Heparin binds platelets indicate or lead to?
back: Exposes platelet factor 4 (PF4) → anti-PF4 antibodies formed

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what value(s) complete this note: “___ T’s of HIT”?
back: 5 T’s of HIT

front: What is the recommended timing for 5 T’s of HIT?
back: 4-10 days after heparin initiation

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what should be recalled about Thrombocytopenia?
back: Platelet count drops > 50%

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what should be recalled about Type of heparin?
back: UFH causes HIT 10x more than LMWH

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what does UFH indicate or lead to?
back: More IgG mediated

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what does LMWH indicate or lead to?
back: More IgM and IgA mediated

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what should be recalled about Thrombosis?
back: Prothrombotic state

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what should be recalled about Type of patient?
back: Surgical > medical

front: In Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT), what does Serotonin Release Assay (SRA): highly specific for HIT indicate or lead to?
back: Heparin added to platelets labeled with radioactive serotonin → positive if serotonin is released

front: In Microlaryngeal Tracheal Tube (MLT), what should be recalled about When performing ENT procedures in adults, need to use smaller ID tubes?
back: Regular smaller ID ETTs (pediatric sizes) will be too short for the adult trachea, and the cuff will be too small

front: In Microlaryngeal Tracheal Tube (MLT), what distinction applies to MLTs have small ID’s (4-6)?
back: Are longer, and have larger cuffs for adult size tracheas

front: In Glucagon, what does One of the counter-regulatory hormones (along with cortisol and catecholamines) indicate or lead to?
back: Counteracts insulin

front: In Glucagon, what does Function: stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis indicate or lead to?
back: Raises glucose levels

front: In Glucagon, what does Mechanism: ↑ cAMP indicate or lead to?
back: Cardiac inotropic and chronotropic effects (not blocked by β-blockers)

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Direct effects?
back: Stimulates hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Indirect effects?
back: Stimulates lipolysis (for gluconeogenesis), inhibits glycolysis and glycogenesis

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Peripheral vascular system?
back: Vasodilator via effects on local vascular tone

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Cardiac?
back: Increases cardiac output and heart rate, possibly via direct effects on the heart

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Renal?
back: Increased renal blood flow, GFR, and urinary electrolyte excretion, gluconeogenesis

front: In Glucagon, what does Glucagon levels increase (relative to insulin) after most major surgery indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperglycemia

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Carbohydrate load 2h before surgery attempts to reduce glucagon?
back: Insulin ratio

front: In Glucagon, what does In sepsis, this mechanism fails indicate or lead to?
back: Hypoglycemia can be seen (associated with very poor outcomes)

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Increases hepatic artery blood flow?
back: Vasodilation and blocks the effects of physiologic vasoconstrictors

front: What is the usual dose in Glucagon?
back: 1 mg IV/IM/SQ

front: In Glucagon, what should be recalled about Hyperkalemia?
back: Increases extracellular shift of K + (opposite of insulin)

front: What are the risk factors of Complications?
back: Overinflation, long surgery (> 2-4 hrs), nitrous oxide, cervical joint disease, lidocaine lubrication (avoid this), difficult insertion

front: In Laryngeal Mask Airway, what should be recalled about Increased risk of aspiration?
back: Prolonged mask ventilation, obesity, pregnancy, upper GI bleed

front: In Laryngeal Mask Airway, what should be recalled about Need for high airway pressures ( > 20 cmH O)?
back: Laparoscopy

front: In Prostaglandin E (PGE ), what does Mechanism: Binds prostanoid receptors in ductus arteriosus indicate or lead to?
back: Direct-acting vasodilator

front: In Prostaglandin E (PGE ), what should be recalled about Side effects?
back: Apnea, hypotension, fever, flushing, bradycardia, gastric outlet obstruction, CNS irritability

front: In Prostaglandin E (PGE ), what should be recalled about Clinical use?
back: Congenital heart disease (usually cyanotic) that requires ductus to remain open

front: In PACU and Discharge Criteria, what is associated with phase 1: typical pacu?
back: Monitoring: (i) hemodynamics and (ii) sedation

front: In PACU and Discharge Criteria, what does Modified Aldrete Score (1-10) indicate or lead to?
back: COBRA: Consciousness, Oxygenation, BP, Respiration, Activity

front: In PACU and Discharge Criteria, what value(s) complete this note: “≥ ___ ⇒ ready for Phase ___ (from either Phase ___ or fast-tracked directly from OR)”?
back: ≥ 9 ⇒ ready for Phase 2 (from either Phase 1 or fast-tracked directly from OR)

front: In PACU and Discharge Criteria, what is associated with phase 2: patient regains cognitive function?
back: Preparing patient for discharge to home → discharge

front: In PACU and Discharge Criteria, what should be recalled about Discharge criteria?
back: Pain controlled, no nausea/vomiting, alert, oriented, stable vitals

front: In PACU and Discharge Criteria, what does Voiding after surgery (ambulatory) indicate or lead to?
back: Not a requirement in low-risk patients

front: In PACU and Discharge Criteria, what does Fast-tracking: bypass Phase 1 directly to Phase 2 indicate or lead to?
back: Faster discharge, more comfortable, cheaper

front: In Head-Down (Trendelenburg) Positioning, what does Cephalad displacement of diaphragm indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased ERV and FRC + decreased compliance

front: In Head-Down (Trendelenburg) Positioning, what does Not much difference in FRC supine (0°) to -30° indicate or lead to?
back: Significant after -30°

front: In Head-Down (Trendelenburg) Positioning, what does Carina moves up indicate or lead to?
back: Endotracheal tube moves deeper → possible endobronchial intubation

front: In Head-Down (Trendelenburg) Positioning, what does Cardiac: Increased venous return (1 L bolus) indicate or lead to?
back: Transiently increased cardiac index

front: In Head-Down (Trendelenburg) Positioning, what does However, this is not good for treating hypotension indicate or lead to?
back: Use straight leg raise instead

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Regional pain indicate or lead to?
back: Meaning: not in a specific nerve territory or dermatome

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Burning spontaneous pain in affected extremity indicate or lead to?
back: Disproportionate to initial inciting event

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Hyperalgesia (normally painful stimuli indicate or lead to?
back: Excess pain), allodynia (non-painful stimuli → pain)

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Early stages, affected limb is warmer indicate or lead to?
back: Mechanical hyperalgesia occurs frequently

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Sometimes, affected limb is cold indicate or lead to?
back: Poor prognosis → more dystonia, sensory loss

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Sweating abnormalities indicate or lead to?
back: Very common → hyperhidrosis > hypohidrosis

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Trophic changes: abnormal nail growth, increased or decreased hair growth, fibrosis, thin glossy skin, and osteoporosis indicate or lead to?
back: Especially in chronic stage

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what value(s) complete this note: “In about ___ of cases, dystonia of the affected hand or foot develops”?
back: In about 10% of cases, dystonia of the affected hand or foot develops

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what does Muscle atrophy indicate or lead to?
back: Usually seen after 6 months

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what should be recalled about Progression?
back: Variable progression over time

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what value(s) complete this note: “CRPS type ___ (reflex sympathetic dystrophy) develops after minor injuries or fractures of a limb”?
back: CRPS type 1 (reflex sympathetic dystrophy) develops after minor injuries or fractures of a limb

front: In Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS), what value(s) complete this note: “CRPS type ___ (causalgia) develops after injury to a major peripheral nerve”?
back: CRPS type 2 (causalgia) develops after injury to a major peripheral nerve

front: How is Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS) treatment handled?
back: Physical therapy (1st line), diagnostic/therapeutic autonomic blocks, oral analgesics, TCAs, gabapentin, somatic blocks, spinal cord stimulators and intrathecal medications

front: In Somatosensory Evoked Potentials (SSEP), what does Pathway: peripheral nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Ipsilateral dorsal root ganglia → ipsilateral posterior spinal cord (dorsal column) → decussation at cervicomedullary junction → contralateral medial lemniscus (brainstem) → contralateral thalamus (VPL nucleus) → contralateral sensory cortex

front: In Somatosensory Evoked Potentials (SSEP), what should be recalled about Good for?
back: Intracranial aneurysm repair, carotid surgery, laminectomy, spinal fusion

front: In Somatosensory Evoked Potentials (SSEP), what does Not a good monitor for anterior spinal cord (motor pathways) indicate or lead to?
back: Supplied by anterior spinal artery (ASA)

front: In Somatosensory Evoked Potentials (SSEP), what does Theoretically, SSEP can measure some ASA compromise because the lateral funiculus pathway (sensory pathway) is supplied by ASA indicate or lead to?
back: But this has not been shown to be reliable

front: In Somatosensory Evoked Potentials (SSEP), what should be recalled about Other blood supplies evaluated by SSEP?
back: ICA, MCA, and ACA (all of these are cortical supply)

front: In Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential, what does Pathway: auditory stimulus at middle ear indicate or lead to?
back: Auditory nerve → brainstem → midbrain

front: In Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential, what should be recalled about Most sensitive?
back: Visual evoked potentials

front: In Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential, what does Most resistant to most sensitive: BAEP indicate or lead to?
back: SSEP → MEP → VEP

front: In Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential, what does MNEMONIC: BAEP indicate or lead to?
back: Barely affected, SSEP → Somewhat affected, MEP → Mostly affected, VEP → Very affected

front: In Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential, what should be recalled about Transient?
back: From surgical manipulation or irrigation

front: In Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential, what should be recalled about Permanent?
back: From CN 8 transection

front: In Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential, what should be recalled about Contralateral changes?
back: Changes in global physiology and blood supply (e.g. hypoperfusion, edema)

front: In Markers of Hepatic Function, what does Best: PT/INR indicate or lead to?
back: Because of short half-life of Factor VII → 4-6 hours

front: In Markers of Hepatic Function, what should be recalled about Part of the MELD score for liver failure mortality (5 factors?
back: Cr, INR, bilirubin, Na, and dialysis)

front: In Markers of Hepatic Function, what should be recalled about Liver produces all Vitamin K dependent factors?
In Markers of Hepatic Function, what should be recalled about PT/INR reflects?
In Markers of Hepatic Function, what does Albumin: half-life of 3 weeks indicate or lead to?
back: Not as good for acute failure detection

front: In Markers of Hepatic Function, what should be recalled about AST/ALT?
back: More useful in acute hepatitis; may be normal in chronic hepatic failure

front: In Infective Endocarditis Prophylaxis Guidelines (2014), what should be recalled about History?
back: Previous infective endocarditis

front: In Infective Endocarditis Prophylaxis Guidelines (2014), what value(s) complete this note: “Repaired CHD with prosthetic materials/device(s) placed in first ___ months”?
back: Repaired CHD with prosthetic materials/device(s) placed in first 6 months

front: In Infective Endocarditis Prophylaxis Guidelines (2014), what should be recalled about Not recommended for?
back: GI/GU procedures, UTI, heart transplant with no valve issues, MVP alone, HOCM, CAD, CABG, Unrepaired VSD or PDA, acquired valvular dysfunction (e.g. MR, AS, AR)

front: In Infective Endocarditis Prophylaxis Guidelines (2014), what does Oral: amoxicillin indicate or lead to?
back: If penicillin allergy: clindamycin, azithromycin

front: In Infective Endocarditis Prophylaxis Guidelines (2014), what does IV: ampicillin, cefazolin, or ceftriaxone indicate or lead to?
back: If penicillin allergy: clindamycin

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what does During CPB, hypothermia can be induced indicate or lead to?
back: Deep hypothermic circulatory arrest → decreases CMRO

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what does Hypothermia indicate or lead to?
back: Increased gas solubility → less PaCO (gaseous phase) → “alkaline drift”

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what should be recalled about Two methods of managing acid-base status?
back: PH-stat and alpha-stat

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what should be recalled about pH-stat?
back: Corrects alkaline drift to maintain normal pH

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what should be recalled about Alpha-stat?
back: No correction of alkaline drift

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what does Cerebral vasodilation indicate or lead to?
back: Increased speed of homogenous cerebral cooling

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what does Reduced CMRO (from hypothermia) indicate or lead to?
back: But increased CBF (from vasodilation)

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what does Counteracting left-shift of O -dissociation curve by alkalosis indicate or lead to?
back: Improved oxygen delivery

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Acid-Base Management, what does Vasodilation indicate or lead to?
back: Increased delivery of embolic load to the brain

front: In Geriatric Pulmonary Changes, what does Loss of stiffness of the airways and elasticity of the lung indicate or lead to?
back: Airway collapses more easily

front: In Geriatric Pulmonary Changes, what should be recalled about Closing capacity?
back: Volume remaining when the small (non-cartilaginous) airways start to collapse

front: In Geriatric Pulmonary Changes, what does Increases with age indicate or lead to?
back: More airway collapse with increasing age

front: In Geriatric Pulmonary Changes, what value(s) complete this note: “Can surpass TV at age >___ (airway starts to collapse with normal breathing…)”?
back: Can surpass TV at age >70 (airway starts to collapse with normal breathing…)

front: In Geriatric Pulmonary Changes, what does Chest wall stiffens indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased TLC

front: In Geriatric Pulmonary Changes, what does Diaphragm flattens indicate or lead to?
back: Higher RV ☆ and higher FRC ☆ → lower IC

front: In Pain on Injection, what does Propofol indicate or lead to?
back: Possibly due to bradykinin release → decrease with lidocaine and AC injection

front: In Pain on Injection, what does Etomidate indicate or lead to?
back: Prepared with PEG → pain more common than propofol

front: In Pain on Injection, what does Rocuronium indicate or lead to?
back: Acidic

front: In Pain on Injection, what should be recalled about No pain with injection of?
back: Opioids, ketamine, midazolam, fospropofol (prodrug), dexmedetomidine

front: In Pneumocephalus, what does After posterior fossa craniotomy indicate or lead to?
back: Some degree of pneumocephalus always happens

front: In Pneumocephalus, what does Avoid nitrous oxide after surgical closure, it can cause expansion of pneumocephalus indicate or lead to?
back: Clinically significant tension pneumocephalus → mental status not recovering in PACU → get a STAT CT scan (will show Mt. Fuji sign)

front: In Pneumocephalus, what should be recalled about It occurs even without nitrous oxide use?
back: Air enters the cranium when the patient is in a head-up position at a time when the volume of the intracranial contents has been reduced because of some combination of hypocapnia, good venous drainage, osmotic diuresis, and CSF loss from the operative field

front: In Bronchopleural Fistula (BPF), what should be recalled about Clinical diagnosis?
back: Sudden onset of dyspnea, subcutaneous emphysema, contralateral deviation of the trachea, and a decrease of fluid level on serial radiographs of the chest (see figure below)

front: In Bronchopleural Fistula (BPF), what should be recalled about In lobectomy patients?
back: Persistent air leak, purulent drainage, and purulent sputum

front: In Bronchopleural Fistula (BPF), what should be recalled about Anesthetic management of bronchopleural fistula?
back: Lung isolation before positive-pressure ventilation or repositioning the patient

front: In Bronchopleural Fistula (BPF), what should be recalled about Other absolute indications?
back: Isolation for infection/hemorrhage, alveolar proteinosis

front: In CYP2D6, what does Codeine (no analgesia) indicate or lead to?
back: Morphine

front: In CYP2D6, what does Oxycodone (no analgesia) indicate or lead to?
back: Oxymorphone

front: In CYP2D6, what does Hydrocodone indicate or lead to?
back: Hydromorphone (can be converted, but this is not the major analgesic pathway)

front: In CYP2D6, what does Hydrocodone itself provides analgesia indicate or lead to?
back: Similar analgesia with varied CYP2D6 activity

front: In CYP2D6, what does 7-10% of Caucasians have inactive homozygous CYP2D6 indicate or lead to?
back: Codeine doesn’t work

front: In CYP2D6, what does In patients with hyperactive CYP2D6 indicate or lead to?
back: Too much morphine from codeine → overdose

front: In CYP2D6, what does Inhibitors of CYP2D6: SSRIs and quinidine indicate or lead to?
back: Increased codeine/oxycodone requirement

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Peripheral nerve damage indicate or lead to?
back: Signal through afferent nerve fibers (Aδ, C-fibers)

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what should be recalled about Activators?
back: Protons, sympathetic amines, ATP, glutamate, neuropeptides (CGrP, substance P), nerve growth factor, prostanoids, bradykinin, proinflammatory cytokines

front: What is the mechanism of Excitatory Mechanisms?
back: Opening (gating) of cation channels in the distal neuronal membrane

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Gating ⇒ inward Na + and Ca 2+ into the peripheral nociception terminal indicate or lead to?
back: Voltage-gated Na + channels are activated → action potential is propagated

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Gating can refer to opening or closing indicate or lead to?
back: In this case, the gate opens

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what should be recalled about Examples of nociceptive cation channels?
back: Capsaicin-, proton-, and heat-sensitive transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1), ATP-gated P2X receptor

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Signal travels via nerve fiber indicate or lead to?
back: To dorsal root ganglia (DRG) → dorsal horn of the spinal cord

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does In the dorsal horn: excitatory transmitter release onto indicate or lead to?
back: Second-order spinal neuron

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Substance P indicate or lead to?
back: NK receptor agonist

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Glutamate indicate or lead to?
back: NMDA receptor agonist

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Neurotrophic factors indicate or lead to?
back: Tyrosine Kinase receptor agonist

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Dull, poorly-localized: paleospinothalamic tract indicate or lead to?
back: Thalamus → cingulate cortex

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Sharp, localized: neospinothalamic tract indicate or lead to?
back: Thalamus → postcentral gyrus

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Ascending spinothalamic tracts indicate or lead to?
back: Periaqueductal grey → descending inhibitory pathways

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what should be recalled about Peripherally?
back: Upregulation of endogenous opioid release (from leukocytes) and opioid receptors

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Spinal cord indicate or lead to?
back: Interneurons → release of inhibitory mediators like opioids, GABA, glycine

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Presynaptic receptors on central nociceptor terminals indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ excitatory transmitter release

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Opioids indicate or lead to?
back: Presynaptic receptor → inhibits adenylyl cyclase → ↓ cAMP → ↓ Ca 2+

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Opening of postsynaptic K + (opioids) or Cl - (GABA) channels indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperpolarize cell (K + leaves or Cl - enters) → inhibitory potentials in dorsal horn neurons

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Powerful descending inhibitory pathways from the brainstem (periaqueductal gray indicate or lead to?
back: Rostral ventromedial medulla → dorsolateral funiculus → dorsal horn)

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Periaqueductal gray indicate or lead to?
back: Opioid (e.g. enkephalin) suppression of pain signals

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Locus coeruleus indicate or lead to?
back: Noradrenergic suppression of pain signals

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Dorsal raphe nucleus indicate or lead to?
back: Serotonergic suppression of pain signals

front: In Chronic Pain: Physiology, what does Complex interaction of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters with cognitive, emotional, and environmental factors indicate or lead to?
back: Central perception of pain

front: In Peripheral Nerve Structure and Injections, what does Multiple fascicles bundled indicate or lead to?
back: Peripheral nerve

front: In Peripheral Nerve Structure and Injections, what should be recalled about Inner epineurium?
back: Holds fascicles together

front: In Peripheral Nerve Structure and Injections, what should be recalled about Outer epineurium?
back: Encases peripheral nerve

front: In Peripheral Nerve Structure and Injections, what should be recalled about Most blocks?
back: Between adventitia and epineurium

front: In Acute dystonic reactions (ADRs), what should be recalled about Involuntary muscle contractions?
back: Repetitive or twisting movements and abnormal posturing

front: In Acute dystonic reactions (ADRs), what should be recalled about Pathophysiology?
back: Altered dopaminergic-cholinergic balance in extrapyramidal system (basal ganglia)

front: In Acute dystonic reactions (ADRs), what should be recalled about Causes?
back: Antipsychotic (neuroleptic) drugs or dopamine antagonists (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)

front: How is Acute dystonic reactions (ADRs) treatment handled?
back: Anticholinergics like benztropine, trihexyphenidyl or atropine

front: In Acute dystonic reactions (ADRs), what should be recalled about Second line?
back: Diphenhydramine (antihistamine + anticholinergic), benzodiazepines, dopamine agonists

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what should be recalled about Seen during?
back: Sitting posterior fossa craniotomy, total hip replacement, cesarean section, etc.

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what does 100 mL can cause RV outflow obstruction indicate or lead to?
back: > 300mL can be fatal (cardiovascular collapse)

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what does PA obstruction indicate or lead to?
back: RV outflow obstruction → pulmonary hypertension, etc.

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what does Paradoxical embolus (ASD) indicate or lead to?
back: MI, stroke, etc.

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what should be recalled about Most sensitive?
back: Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) but invasive

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what should be recalled about Most sensitive non-invasive?
back: Precordial Doppler ultrasound with transducer placed over RA

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what does Others (most indicate or lead to?
back: Least sensitive): PAC, TCD, ETN , ETCO , SaO , esophageal stethoscope, ECG

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what should be recalled about Stop entrainment of air?
back: Flood surgical field with saline

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what does Apply 15 sec jugular venous pressure to cause back bleed indicate or lead to?
back: Identify site of air entry

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what does High FiO indicate or lead to?
back: Stop nitrous oxide if venous air embolism is detected

front: In Venous Air Embolism (VAE), what should be recalled about “Durant position” (left lateral decubitus)?
back: Not that effective in recent studies

front: In Lithotomy Position, what should be recalled about Former most common (till 1991)?
back: Common peroneal nerve motor injury

front: What are the risk factors of Potential injuries?
back: Candy cane stirrups, low BMI, recent cigarette smoking, long surgery

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Obturator nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Medial thigh

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Lateral thigh

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Tibial nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Lateral leg

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Peroneal nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Anterior leg

front: In Lithotomy Position, what should be recalled about Risk factor?
back: Surgery > 2 hours

front: In Lithotomy Position, what should be recalled about Lower extremity compartment syndrome?
back: Decreased perfusion due to increase in height from heart, and increased leg compartment pressures (due to unexplained reasons)

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Crush injuries to the fingers indicate or lead to?
back: Make sure they are well off to the side on padded armboard

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Increased venous return indicate or lead to?
back: Transient ↑ CO, cerebral venous and intracranial pressure

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Cephalad diaphragm displacement indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ lung compliance, V

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Increased abdominal pressure (obesity, pregnancy, mass) indicate or lead to?
back: Obstruct venous return to the heart

front: In Lithotomy Position, what does Normal lordotic curvature of the lumbar spine is lost indicate or lead to?
back: Can worsen chronic lower back pain

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about Normal values?
In Acid-Base Disorders, what does E.g. pH 6 = 10 -6 [H + ] ⇒ 90% reduction in [H + ] ⇒ 10 -7 indicate or lead to?
back: PH of 7

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what does Respiratory acidosis indicate or lead to?
back: Metabolic compensation (slow)

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what does Acute: 0.2 mEq/L rise in HCO per 1 mmHg rise in PaCO indicate or lead to?
back: PH decrease of 0.05

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about Chronic?
back: 0.4 mEq/L rise in HCO per 1 mmHg rise in PaCO (double the efficiency of compensation)

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what does Respiratory alkalosis indicate or lead to?
back: Metabolic compensation

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what does Acute: 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO indicate or lead to?
back: 2 mEq/L decrease in HCO and 0.1 increase in pH (values are double those of acute respiratory acidosis)

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what does Metabolic alkalosis indicate or lead to?
back: Respiratory compensation → hypoventilation

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what does 1 mEq/L rise in HCO indicate or lead to?
back: 0.5 mmHg rise in PaCO

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what does Metabolic acidosis indicate or lead to?
back: Respiratory compensation → hyperventilation

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about Winter’s formula?
In Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about Awake patient?
back: Metabolic acidosis + compensatory respiratory alkalosis

front: In Acid-Base Disorders, what should be recalled about Unconscious patient?
back: Metabolic acidosis (no compensation)

front: In Tourniquet, what should be recalled about Upper extremity?
back: 50 mmHg above SBP

front: In Tourniquet, what should be recalled about Lower extremity?
back: 100 mmHg above SBP

front: In Tourniquet, what should be recalled about Reason?
back: Minimize blood loss, ensure adequate surgical visualization, avoid overinflation

front: In Tourniquet, what should be recalled about Risks of overinflation?
back: Arterial vasospasm, venous thrombosis, nerve injury

front: In Pediatric Fluid Resuscitation, what value(s) complete this note: “Urine output <___ ml/kg/hr, Urine specific gravity >___”?
back: Urine output <0.5 ml/kg/hr, Urine specific gravity >1.030

front: In Pediatric Fluid Resuscitation, what does Initial resuscitation: 20-30 mL/kg of normal saline indicate or lead to?
back: 10 mL/kg albumin if unresponsive

front: In Pediatric Fluid Resuscitation, what does Avoid dextrose and potassium as resuscitation fluid indicate or lead to?
back: They are maintenance fluids

front: In Pediatric Fluid Resuscitation, what does In oliguria: decreased potassium excretion indicate or lead to?
back: Potassium administration can lead to hyperkalemia

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what does Pericardial effusion indicate or lead to?
back: Pericardial pressure on all heart chambers → impaired diastolic filling → equalization of diastolic pressures (RAP, RVEDP, diastolic PA, PCWP, LVEDP)

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what does Beck triad indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ arterial pressure (hypotension), ↑ venous pressure (JVD), muffled heart sounds

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what should be recalled about Pulsus Paradoxus?
back: Exaggerated decrease in SBP on inspiration

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what does Normal: Inspiration indicate or lead to?
back: Negative intrathoracic pressure → increased venous return to right heart (free wall of RV distends out towards pericardium) → decreased filling of left heart (due to ventricular interdependence) → decreased SV → SBP decreases (less than 10 mmHg normally)

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what does Cardiac tamponade: Inspiration indicate or lead to?
back: Negative intrathoracic pressure → increased venous return to right heart (free wall of RV cannot distend out towards pericardium → septum bulges into LV → smaller LV) → super decreased filling of left heart → SBP decreases more than 10 mmHg

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what does Management: “fast, full and tight” indicate or lead to?
back: Induce with etomidate or ketamine → keep sympathetic tone

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what should be recalled about Fast?
back: CO is HR dependent

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what should be recalled about Full?
back: SV is fixed and dependent on adequate preload

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what should be recalled about Tight?
back: Keep SVR high

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what should be recalled about X-ray?
back: Will show widened mediastinum after 200 mL of fluid has accumulated

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what should be recalled about ECG?
back: Low voltage in all leads, electrical alternans (due to swinging of the heart)

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what should be recalled about Right heart catheterization?
back: Attenuated Y-descent, equilibration of CVP, PAP, PCWP

front: In Cardiac Tamponade, what value(s) complete this note: “Can detect pericardial fluid (as little as ___ mL can be detected)”?
back: Can detect pericardial fluid (as little as 20 mL can be detected)

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what does Intestinal contents in thorax indicate or lead to?
back: 80% are left-sided → respiratory distress + sunken abdomen

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what value(s) complete this note: “Among left-sided hernias, ___ are Bochdalek-type (posterolateral)”?
back: Among left-sided hernias, 90% are Bochdalek-type (posterolateral)

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what does Diaphragm forms between 7-10 weeks of gestation indicate or lead to?
back: Failure of fusion → CDH

front: How is Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH) management handled?
back: Open abdominal surgery in 24-48 hours after birth (pulmonary resistance decreases)

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what does Avoid bag-mask ventilation indicate or lead to?
back: Gastric insufflation can worsen status → tracheal intubation preferred

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what does “Gentle ventilation” indicate or lead to?
back: Spontaneous, PIP < 25 cmH O, permissive hypercapnia, SaO 85-95%

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what does Spontaneous ventilation indicate or lead to?
back: Helps maintain FRC

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what does PaCO indicate or lead to?
back: Maintain between 45-60 mmHg (permissive hypercapnia)

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what does Avoid high peak pressures indicate or lead to?
back: Can cause pneumothorax of contralateral lung

front: In Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH), what should be recalled about Avoid?
back: High FiO , muscle relaxation, hyperventilation

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what should be recalled about Clinical?
back: First-born, white males at 3-5 weeks of age with nonbilious vomiting, dehydration and lethargy

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does Vomiting indicate or lead to?
back: Loss of H + + dehydration → hypokalemic hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does Treatment: medical emergency (not surgical emergency) indicate or lead to?
back: Dehydration, alkalosis, low K + and low Cl -

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does Dehydration indicate or lead to?
back: Resuscitate with 3 L/m 2 /day of D5NS

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does Hypokalemia indicate or lead to?
back: After urine output is normal → add 40 mEq/L of K + to maintenance fluid

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does Chloride is low indicate or lead to?
back: Normal Cl - represents adequate resuscitation

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does U is initially low due to dehydration indicate or lead to?
back: U > 20 mEq/L = adequate resuscitation

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does OG tube to empty stomach prior to induction indicate or lead to?
back: Insert and leave NG tube in for surgery

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what should be recalled about Postoperative pain?
back: Rectal acetaminophen > opioids (increased sensitivity at this age)

front: In Pyloric Stenosis, what does Hypoglycemia can occur postoperatively indicate or lead to?
back: Monitor carefully

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what should be recalled about Basic pulmonary function tests measure three sets of data?
back: Flow, volume, and gas exchange

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does Gas flow rates indicate or lead to?
back: To assess airway narrowing

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does Lung volumes indicate or lead to?
back: To assess lung tissue loss or change induced by chest wall muscle disease

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does Gas Flow Rates indicate or lead to?
back: Evaluate FEV and FVC → FEV is decreased in both obstructive and restrictive

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what should be recalled about Restrictive Lung Disease?
back: Low FEV , Low FVC but normal FEV /FVC ratio

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what should be recalled about Obstructive Lung Disease?
back: Normal FVC, but decreased FEV /FVC ratio

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does Bronchodilator therapy: improvement of >15% in FEV indicate or lead to?
back: Positive for reversibility

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what should be recalled about Basically?
back: Not as useful in healthy lungs or severe COPD

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does ppoFEV >40% indicate or lead to?
back: Low risk of postoperative pulmonary complications

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does ppoFEV <30% indicate or lead to?
back: High risk

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does VO > 15 mL/kg/min indicate or lead to?
back: Low risk

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what value(s) complete this note: “ppoD >___”?
back: PpoD >40%

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does If PFTs reveal questionable tolerance for pneumonectomy indicate or lead to?
back: Split-function testing (figure out how much each lung is contributing to ventilation and perfusion)

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does Xenon indicate or lead to?
back: Used to assess ventilation in each lung

front: In Pulmonary Function Testing, what does Technetium indicate or lead to?
back: Used to assess perfusion in each lung

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what does Mechanism: opens K + channels in atria and SA node indicate or lead to?
back: K + out hyperpolarization → bradycardia

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what does Very short duration of action: rapid active transport into RBC and endothelial cells indicate or lead to?
back: Rapidly metabolized

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what should be recalled about Treatment for?
back: AVNRT, AVRT and atrial tachycardia when hemodynamically stable

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what should be recalled about Doesn’t work for?
back: Atrial fibrillation (most common SVT), atrial flutter (2nd most common SVT)

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what distinction applies to Slows the rate?
back: Does not terminate the arrhythmia

front: What are the adverse effects of Adenosine for SVT?
back: Hypotension, bronchospasm, facial flushing, dyspnea and chest discomfort

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what should be recalled about Contraindications?
back: Asthma, sick sinus syndrome, 2nd/3rd-degree heart block (unless pacemaker present)

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what should be recalled about Antagonized by?
back: Caffeine and theophylline

front: In Adenosine for SVT, what should be recalled about Potentiated by?
back: Dipyridamole and carbamazepine, post cardiac transplant (denervated heart) require only one-third to one-fifth the dose

front: In Postherpetic Neuralgia (PHN), what does Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV, HHV-3) indicate or lead to?
back: Lies dormant in dorsal root ganglia (DRG) after initial infection (chickenpox in children) → immune compromise → reactivation → herpes zoster (“shingles”)

front: What are the risk factors of Postherpetic Neuralgia (PHN)?
back: Age, severity of herpes zoster pain, Location (V , brachial plexus), immunosuppression

front: In Postherpetic Neuralgia (PHN), what should be recalled about Most commonly affected?
back: Thoracic > V (ophthalmic) > V (maxillary) > cervical > sacral

front: How is Acute herpes zoster management handled?
back: Antivirals, steroids, sympathetic blocks

front: In Postherpetic Neuralgia (PHN), what should be recalled about Pathophysiology?
back: Damage to dorsal root ganglion from inflammation during active infection

front: How is Acute herpes zoster management handled?
back: Pain relief with TCAs, anticonvulsants, lidocaine patches, capsaicin, opioids

front: In SIADH, what value(s) complete this note: “Inappropriately concentrated urine (P < ___, U > ___, U high > ___)”?
back: Inappropriately concentrated urine (P < 280, U > 100, U high > 20)

front: In SIADH, what should be recalled about Other lab findings?
back: Low BUN, low uric acid, FeNa > 1%

front: In SIADH, what should be recalled about Primary therapy for SIADH?
back: Water restriction

front: In SIADH, what should be recalled about CNS?
back: Acute ↑ ICP, trauma, tumors, meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, Guillain-Barre (50%!)

front: In SIADH, what should be recalled about Pulmonary?
back: TB, pneumonia, asthma, hypoxemia, hypercarbia, and positive-pressure ventilation

front: In SIADH, what should be recalled about Malignancies?
back: ADH-like compounds

front: In SIADH, what should be recalled about Endocrine?
back: Adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about Shock?
back: Profound hypotension resulting in hypoperfusion (oxygen supply doesn’t meet demand)

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about Four types?
back: Hypovolemic, obstructive, cardiogenic, distributive

front: In Classification of Shock, what does Hypovolemic indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ CO, DO , CVP, PCWP → ↑ SVR (compensatory) ⇒ e.g. hemorrhage → give volume

front: In Classification of Shock, what does Obstructive indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ CO due to obstruction → ↑ CVP, ↑/- PCWP, ↑ mPAP due to pump failure → ↑/- SVR (compensatory) ⇒ e.g. PE, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade → give inotropes

front: In Classification of Shock, what does Cardiogenic indicate or lead to?
back: Pump failure → ↓ CO → ↑ CVP, ↑ PCWP, ↑ mPAP due to pump failure → ↑ SVR (compensation) ⇒

front: In Classification of Shock, what does g. MI, arrhythmia indicate or lead to?
back: Give inotropes (norepinephrine probably first line)

front: In Classification of Shock, what does Distributive (sepsis) indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ SVR → ↓ venous return → ↓ PCWP, mPAP, CVP → ↑ CO → give pressors and volume

front: In Classification of Shock, what value(s) complete this note: “Normal cardiac pressure values (Miller ___e Table ___)”?
back: Normal cardiac pressure values (Miller 8e Table 45-2)

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about CVP?
back: 4-12 mmHg

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about RA?
back: 2-7 mmHg

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about PA diastolic?
back: 4-12 mmHg

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about PA systolic?
back: 15-30 mmHg

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about PCWP?
back: 4-12 mmHg

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about CI?
In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about SVR?
back: 800-1200 dynes⋅sec/cm 5

front: In Classification of Shock, what should be recalled about PVR?
back: <250 dynes⋅sec/cm 5

front: In Colloids, what value(s) complete this note: “Available in ___ and ___ concentrations”?
back: Available in 5% and 25% concentrations

front: In Colloids, what does Heat-stable tightly wound protein molecule indicate or lead to?
back: Heated to 60°C for 10h to prevent infections

front: In Colloids, what value(s) complete this note: “Contains ___ mEq of sodium (caprylate and acetyltryptophanate, not chloride) Grifols Albutein ___”?
back: Contains 130-160 mEq of sodium (caprylate and acetyltryptophanate, not chloride) Grifols Albutein 5%

front: In Colloids, what value(s) complete this note: “Hetastarch ___ and pentastarch ___”?
back: Hetastarch 6% and pentastarch 10%

front: In Colloids, what does Do not interfere with blood typing or cross matching indicate or lead to?
back: But interfere with coagulation

front: In Colloids, what should be recalled about Hetastarch?
back: 25.5 hours

front: In Colloids, what does Pentastarch: 2.5 hours indicate or lead to?
back: Higher maximum dose than hetastarch

front: In Colloids, what should be recalled about Elimination?
back: Primarily renal

front: In Colloids, what should be recalled about Dilutional coagulopathy?
back: ↑ PT, aPTT, and bleeding times when given at large doses

front: In One-Lung Ventilation (OLV): Hypoxia → Management, what value(s) complete this note: “Increase FiO to ___ (except in patients who received bleomycin)”?
back: Increase FiO to 100% (except in patients who received bleomycin)

front: In One-Lung Ventilation (OLV): Hypoxia → Management, what should be recalled about Ensure adequate cardiac output (CO)?
back: Inotropes, vasopressors, volume, decrease volatiles < 1 MAC

front: In One-Lung Ventilation (OLV): Hypoxia → Management, what does Recruitment maneuvers in the ventilated lung indicate or lead to?
back: Transiently makes hypoxemia worse

front: In One-Lung Ventilation (OLV): Hypoxia → Management, what does PEEP to the ventilated lung (except in patients with emphysematous pathology indicate or lead to?
back: Raises end-expiratory volume to FRC → lowers pulmonary vascular resistance → improved V/Q (perfusion to ventilated lung)

front: In One-Lung Ventilation (OLV): Hypoxia → Management, what does Right lung is larger and better perfused: right-sided thoracotomy indicate or lead to?
back: Desaturation more likely on OLV

front: In One-Lung Ventilation (OLV): Hypoxia → Management, what does Two-Lung Oxygenation: better PaO levels during TLV in the lateral position indicate or lead to?
back: Better OLV oxygenation

front: In One-Lung Ventilation (OLV): Hypoxia → Management, what does Better spirometric lung function preoperatively indicate or lead to?
back: More likely to desaturate during OLV (inversely related)

front: In Echothiophate, what does Echothiophate eye drops indicate or lead to?
back: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor → ↑ACh → miosis → for refractory glaucoma

front: In Echothiophate, what does Use for > 1 month indicate or lead to?
back: Up to 95% decrease in function of plasma butyrylcholinesterase → prolonged SCh

front: In Echothiophate, what value(s) complete this note: “Normal activity returns within ___ weeks after discontinuation”?
back: Normal activity returns within 4-6 weeks after discontinuation

front: In Alfentanil, what does Lipid solubility indicate or lead to?
back: Crosses BBB more easily

front: In Alfentanil, what does Fentanyl has a longer elimination half-life than morphine, but shorter duration due to the higher lipid solubility of fentanyl indicate or lead to?
back: Faster onset and shorter duration of fentanyl

front: In Alfentanil, what does pKa indicate or lead to?
back: If a molecule exists in the nonionized form, it is more likely to cross BBB → faster onset

front: In Alfentanil, what does Alfentanil: lowest pKa among opioid indicate or lead to?
back: Fastest onset of action, even though it is not the most lipid soluble

front: In Ipratropium, what does MOA: non-selective antimuscarinic agent indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ cGMP → bronchodilation

front: In Ipratropium, what does Quaternary-amine indicate or lead to?
back: Does not cross membranes easily → minimal central effects

front: In Ipratropium, what does Slow-acting: 30-60 minutes indicate or lead to?
back: Lasts 6-8 hours

front: In Ipratropium, what does For asthma: not as effective as albuterol, but lasts longer indicate or lead to?
back: Albuterol also has faster onset

front: In Ipratropium, what should be recalled about Tiotropium?
back: Slightly more selective for M blockade (more than M blockade)

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what does Clopidogrel indicate or lead to?
back: Hold for 7 days (or 5-6 days and normal platelet function tests)

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what does Ticlopidine indicate or lead to?
back: Hold for 14 days

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what does Fondaparinux indicate or lead to?
back: Insufficient data for ASRA recommendations, int’l recommendations: 36-42 hours

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what does Warfarin indicate or lead to?
back: Hold for 4-5 days and ensure normal INR

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what distinction applies to INR decreases in first 1-3 days?
back: Patient is actually still anticoagulated

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what does Herbs and supplements: garlic, ginkgo, ginseng indicate or lead to?
back: No mandatory discontinuation necessary

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what does GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors: abciximab indicate or lead to?
back: 24-48 hours, Eptifibatide and tirofiban: 4-8 hours

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what does Heparin: IV indicate or lead to?
back: 2-4 hours and normal aPTT; SQ: no recommendations

front: In Regional Anesthesia and Anticoagulation Therapy, what should be recalled about Enoxaparin?
back: 24 hours for anticoagulant dose, 12 hours for prophylactic dose

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what does Normal respiration: at end expiration (FRC) indicate or lead to?
back: “PEEP” = 0

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what should be recalled about Dynamic hyperinflation?
back: Occurs when a new breath begins before the lung has emptied to FRC

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what should be recalled about Dynamic hyperinflation with expiratory flow limitation?
back: COPD, asthma ⇒ external PEEP can help keep the airway open during expiration, bronchodilators

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what should be recalled about Dynamic hyperinflation without expiratory flow limitation?
back: High volumes, short expiratory time, or extrinsic expiratory flow limitation (tracheal tube clogged, expiratory valve malfunction) ⇒ reduce volumes, increase expiratory time, relieve blockage

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what should be recalled about Exaggerated expiratory activity without dynamic hyperinflation?
back: Strong expiratory muscle activity at the end of expiration

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what does Diagnosis: hard to measure automatically indicate or lead to?
back: Two methods available

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what does Watch flow-time curve indicate or lead to?
back: Persistent end-expiratory flow = auto-PEEP

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what does Expiratory hold maneuver indicate or lead to?
back: Occlude expiratory port at end expiration → auto-PEEP will show up on pressure curve

front: In Intrinsic (Auto) PEEP, what does Severe cases: hypotension, tachycardia, and high airway pressures indicate or lead to?
back: Disconnect patient from ventilator and manually decompress the thorax

front: In Propofol: Metabolism, what does Very high hepatic extraction ratio indicate or lead to?
back: Pretty much 100% of the propofol that enters the liver is cleared

front: In Propofol: Metabolism, what does Enters through the hepatic artery or portal vein indicate or lead to?
back: Close to 0% leaves via the hepatic vein

front: In Propofol: Metabolism, what does Unaffected by hepatic inducers indicate or lead to?
back: Liver already clears so efficiently that it doesn’t matter

front: In Propofol: Metabolism, what does Relatively unaffected by liver dysfunction indicate or lead to?
back: Even a half functional liver can clear 100% of propofol

front: In Propofol: Metabolism, what does However, it is sensitive to hepatic blood flow indicate or lead to?
back: 30% of cardiac output (1.5 L/min)

front: In Propofol: Metabolism, what does Clearance of propofol is 2 L/min indicate or lead to?
back: There is some extrahepatic (mostly renal) clearance

front: In Propofol: Metabolism, what does Propofol redistributes into other tissue indicate or lead to?
back: That’s why it sticks around longer even though liver clears it

front: In Celiac Plexus Block, what does Innervates all abdominal viscera except the left colon and pelvic viscera indicate or lead to?
back: Generally performed for upper abdominal malignancy pain (e.g. pancreatic cancer)

front: In Celiac Plexus Block, what does Block performed at T12-L1 using fluoroscopy or ultrasound guidance indicate or lead to?
back: Near the diaphragm and aorta

front: In Celiac Plexus Block, what should be recalled about Two main approaches (shown in image on right)?
back: Retrocrural (blue) and anterocrural (green)

front: In Celiac Plexus Block, what should be recalled about Diaphragm-related?
back: Reactive pleurisy, hiccups, pneumothorax

front: In Celiac Plexus Block, what should be recalled about Aorta-related?
back: Retroperitoneal hematoma, hematuria, abdominal aortic dissection

front: In Celiac Plexus Block, what should be recalled about Sympatholysis?
back: Orthostatic hypotension (most common), transient diarrhea (65%)

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what value(s) complete this note: “Occurs in up to ___ of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)”?
back: Occurs in up to 60% of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what does Grading of SAH: Hunt and Hess vs. Fisher indicate or lead to?
back: To predict likelihood of vasospasm

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what should be recalled about Hunt and Hess?
back: 6 grades, uses imaging and clinical symptoms

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what should be recalled about Fisher?
back: Four grades, uses imaging only

front: What is the recommended timing for Occurs in up to 60% of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)?
back: Between 2 days and 2 weeks

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what value(s) complete this note: “Hemorrhage is the feared complication within the first ___ hours”?
back: Hemorrhage is the feared complication within the first 48 hours

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what should be recalled about Prophylaxis?
back: Nimodipine (calcium-channel blocker)

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what does Treatment: “Triple H therapy” indicate or lead to?
back: Hypervolemia, hypertension and hemodilution

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what does Hypervolemia indicate or lead to?
back: Keeps vessel dilated

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what should be recalled about Hypertension (mnemonic?
back: Keeps vessel taut and less likely to spasm)

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what does Hemodilution indicate or lead to?
back: Blood flows easily and causes less irritation to trigger spasm

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what does Labetalol is not useful indicate or lead to?
back: Can actually make ischemia worse through hypotension

front: In Cerebral Vasospasm, what should be recalled about Refractory cases?
back: Chemical angioplasty with intraarterial injection of vasodilators can help

front: What is the onset in Fentanyl Patch?
back: 6-8 hours

front: In Fentanyl Patch, what should be recalled about Peak?
back: 30 hours

front: What is the duration of patch in Fentanyl Patch?
back: 72 hours

front: What is the duration of effect in Fentanyl Patch?
back: Takes 24 hours after patch removal for plasma fentanyl to ↓ 50%

front: In Fentanyl Patch, what does Preoperative: keep fentanyl patch on indicate or lead to?
back: Maintain baseline fentanyl and analgesia level

front: In Fentanyl Patch, what should be recalled about Intraoperative considerations?
back: Physiologic changes during general anesthesia can increase absorption from patch (increased peripheral blood flow, increased temperature from warming device)

front: In Degradation of Inhalation Gases, what value(s) complete this note: “Hepatic metabolism mostly by CYP2E1”?
back: Hepatic metabolism mostly by CYP2E1

front: In Degradation of Inhalation Gases, what does Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) indicate or lead to?
back: Can act as a hapten → antibody formation → reaction to subsequent des/iso

front: In Degradation of Inhalation Gases, what does Desflurane: resistant to degradation by soda lime indicate or lead to?
back: 0.02% metabolized → trifluoroacetic acid

front: In Degradation of Inhalation Gases, what does Isoflurane: resistant to degradation by soda lime indicate or lead to?
back: 0.2% metabolized → trifluoroacetic acid

front: In Degradation of Inhalation Gases, what does Sevoflurane indicate or lead to?
back: Compound A in soda lime → 3% metabolized → hexafluoroisopropanol → no immune reaction (unlike TFA from des/iso)

front: In Degradation of Inhalation Gases, what does All anesthetics agents oxidized to fluoride ions in the bloodstream indicate or lead to?
back: Not clinically significant amounts → sevo does it the most

front: In Degradation of Inhalation Gases, what should be recalled about ✪ Overall?
back: Des forms CO, des/iso form TFA, sevo forms compound A and fluoride ions

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what value(s) complete this note: “Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) = MAP − ICP ⇒ ___ mmHg”?
back: Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) = MAP − ICP ⇒ 80 - 100 mmHg

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what should be recalled about Current recommendations?
back: Keep CPP 50-70 mmHg and ICP < 20 mmHg for patients with severe TBI

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Hyperventilation indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ CBF 1-2 mL/100g/min per 1 mmHg change in PaCO → ↓ ICP

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does However, in systemic hypotension indicate or lead to?
back: Increased risk of neurological ischemia

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what value(s) complete this note: “Hyperventilation to maintain PaCO between ___ mmHg (do not go below ___)”?
back: Hyperventilation to maintain PaCO between 25-30 mmHg (do not go below 25)

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Avoid hyperventilation in first 24 hours after TBI indicate or lead to?
back: But can be used as a temporizing measure

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Osmotic diuretic therapy: mannitol 0.25-1.0 g/kg body weight indicate or lead to?
back: Draws extravascular fluid from brain into vascular system → brisk diuresis → monitor osmolarity and electrolytes (particularly potassium)

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Do not use in CHF indicate or lead to?
back: Rapid expansion of intravascular volume → cardiac decompensation

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Do not use in renal failure indicate or lead to?
back: Risk of electrolyte derangements

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Rapid administration indicate or lead to?
back: Cerebral vasodilation → engorgement of the brain → ↑ ICP

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what value(s) complete this note: “Give slowly over ___ minutes”?
back: Give slowly over 10-15 minutes

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Barbiturate coma indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased CMRO , CBF, and cerebral oxygen demand → decrease ICP

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does Side effect: hypotension indicate or lead to?
back: Limit its use in the hemodynamically unstable patient

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what value(s) complete this note: “Vasopressors may be used in order to maintain CPP ___ mmHg”?
back: Vasopressors may be used in order to maintain CPP 50-70 mmHg

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what value(s) complete this note: “Elevation of the head to ___° with maintenance of neutral head position to facilitate venous drainage”?
back: Elevation of the head to 30° with maintenance of neutral head position to facilitate venous drainage

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does CSF drainage (intraventricular catheter) indicate or lead to?
back: Fastest method

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what should be recalled about Side-note?
back: Aneurysm rupture is predicted by transmural pressure (TMP) = MAP - ICP

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what does In this situation, draining CSF indicate or lead to?
back: Decreasing ICP → increased TMP → rupture!

front: In Increased ICP: Treatment, what value(s) complete this note: “Hypertonic saline (___) especially beneficial in TBI”?
back: Hypertonic saline (2-3%) especially beneficial in TBI

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what should be recalled about Indications?
back: Bowel obstruction, short bowel syndrome, active GI bleeding

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what should be recalled about Infection?
back: Most common risk

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what does Thrombophlebitis: common with peripheral TPN indicate or lead to?
back: Peripheral veins cannot tolerate >12.5% dextrose

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what does Cholecystitis: no CCK production indicate or lead to?
back: Cholestasis

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what does Hypophosphatemia: TPN indicate or lead to?
back: Glucose load → insulin release → cellular uptake of glucose and phosphate (“refeeding syndrome”; also seen in alcoholics and malnourished)

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what does Hypokalemia: similar to above indicate or lead to?
back: Insulin → cellular uptake of K +

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what does Glucose load indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperglycemia → acute discontinuation → hypoglycemia

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what does Hypercarbia: overfeeding/high carbohydrate load indicate or lead to?
back: Increased CO production (RQ = 1) → makes vent weaning harder because patient needs high MV to expire excess CO

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what should be recalled about Protein/fat breakdown for energy?
In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what should be recalled about Carbohydrate breakdown for energy?
back: RQ = 1

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what should be recalled about Carbohydrate breakdown for energy + lipogenesis?
back: RQ > 1

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what should be recalled about Hepatic steatosis?
back: Excess sugar stored as adipose in liver

front: In Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN), what value(s) complete this note: “Delayed postoperative jaundice (>___ weeks after surgery)”?
back: Delayed postoperative jaundice (>3 weeks after surgery)

front: In Heparin Resistance, what should be recalled about Definition?
back: ACT < 480 seconds after 500 units/kg heparin OR ACT < 400 during bypass with heparin

front: In Heparin Resistance, what does Heparin indicate or lead to?
back: Binds and enhances antithrombin III (which is an anticoagulant)

front: In Heparin Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “Normally AT3 inactivates IIa (thrombin), VII, IX, X, XI, and XII”?
back: Normally AT3 inactivates IIa (thrombin), VII, IX, X, XI, and XII

front: In Heparin Resistance, what does AT3 deficiency can be acquired or hereditary indicate or lead to?
back: Results in heparin resistance

front: In Heparin Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “AT3 level < ___ of normal”?
back: AT3 level < 60% of normal

front: In Heparin Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “Platelets > ___K”?
back: Platelets > 300K

front: In Heparin Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “Age > ___”?
back: Age > 65

front: In Heparin Resistance, what should be recalled about Refractory or emergency cases?
back: FFP (has AT3), AT3 concentrates or recombinant AT3

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Femoral nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Arises from L2-L4 → largest nerve of the lumbar plexus

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Course: beneath inguinal ligament on top of the iliopsoas muscle indicate or lead to?
back: Lateral to the femoral artery → beneath fascia lata and fascia iliaca

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Innervation: sensation to anterior thigh indicate or lead to?
back: Branches into saphenous nerve → sensation to medial leg

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does If sartorius twitches indicate or lead to?
back: Indicates stimulation of the anterior branch of the femoral nerve → advance needle and direct laterally → look for quadriceps (patellar) twitch

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Sciatic Nerve: contains two trunks in one sheath indicate or lead to?
back: Bifurcates high in the popliteal fossa

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Lateral trunk (L4-S2) indicate or lead to?
back: Common peroneal (fibular) nerve → divides at the knee

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Superficial branch?
back: Mixed sensory and motor

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Motor?
back: Eversion

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Sensory?
back: Lateral lower leg and most of the dorsum of foot

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Deep branch?
back: Mixed sensory and motor

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Motor?
back: Dorsiflexion

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Sensory?
back: Small patch of skin between first and second toes

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Isolated nerve injury: trauma to lateral knee indicate or lead to?
back: Foot drop

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Medial trunk (L4-S3) indicate or lead to?
back: Tibial (sural) nerve

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Motor?
back: Foot plantar flexion

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Sensation?
back: Posterolateral lower leg and lateral foot

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what should be recalled about Obturator nerve?
back: Medial thigh and posterior knee

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Mnemonic: ALABAMa from superficial to deep (longus indicate or lead to?
back: Brevis → magnus)

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Lateral thigh

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Adductor canal indicate or lead to?
back: Femoral nerve → distal femur and thigh, medial calf, medial lower leg distal to knee

topic: Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity
front: In Sensory Distribution of the Lower Extremity, what does Popliteal fossa indicate or lead to?
back: Sciatic nerve → everything distal to the knee except medial lower leg (femoral)

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Hemolytic transfusion reactions?
back: Incompatibility of antibodies to ABO, Rh, others (e.g. Kell, Kidd, Duffy, etc.)

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Types?
back: Acute vs. Delayed and Intravascular vs. Extravascular

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Acute?
back: Typically occurs during or shortly after incompatible RBCs transfusion

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Mechanism: recipient IgM antibodies indicate or lead to?
back: Complement activation → hemolysis

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Delayed: hemolysis 3–10 days after transfusion indicate or lead to?
back: Less likely to present as clinical emergency

front: What is the mechanism of Transfusion Reactions?
back: Recipient IgG antibodies

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Intravascular: full complement activation indicate or lead to?
back: IgG mediated → more acute clinical findings

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Extravascular: no/partial complement activation indicate or lead to?
back: IgG mediated → less acute clinical findings

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Clinical findings?
back: Fever, chills, chest/bank/flank pain, hypotension, nausea, flushing, DIC and ARF

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Look for these instead?
back: Fever, hypotension, hemoglobinuria, diffuse bleeding (DIC)

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Lab findings?
back: Massive release of hemoglobin →

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Haptoglobin: normally binds free hemoglobin indicate or lead to?
back: Haptoglobin decreases with hemolysis (because the haptoglobin binds up all the released hemoglobin)

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Free hemoglobin indicate or lead to?
back: Metabolized to bilirubin → bilirubin increases (both direct and indirect)

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Anaphylactic reactions: binding of IgE indicate or lead to?
back: Usually in patients with IgA deficiency → exposure to donor IgA

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Bronchospasm, edema, redness, and hypotension indicate or lead to?
back: Urgent treatment with epinephrine, fluids, corticosteroids and antihistamines

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does 1°C within four hours of a blood transfusion indicate or lead to?
back: Defervescence in 48 hours

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Bacterial contamination of blood products indicate or lead to?
back: Highest in platelets → rapidly experience: fever, chills, tachycardia, dyspnea, emesis, and shock. May develop DIC and ARF

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI): noncardiogenic pulmonary edema indicate or lead to?
back: Supportive treatment

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Immune-related indicate or lead to?
back: 6 to 12 hours after transfusion

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Highest risk with FFP indicate or lead to?
back: Especially from multiparous women

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Urticarial reactions indicate or lead to?
back: Mast cell degranulation → do not stop transfusion → give antihistamines

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Citrate indicate or lead to?
back: Prevents blood product clotting (mostly in FFP) → decreases ionized calcium by chelation → hypocalcemia: hypotension, narrow pulse pressure, prolonged QT, wide QRS, flattened T waves

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Citrate is metabolized to bicarbonate indicate or lead to?
back: MTP can cause metabolic alkalosis

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Causes of mortality?
back: TRALI > hemolytic reactions (non-ABO IgG > ABO IgM), sepsis

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what should be recalled about Noted in kidney transplant patients?
back: Those who got transfusions had better allograft survival

front: In Transfusion Reactions, what does Mechanism: soluble leukocyte mediators from blood transfusion indicate or lead to?
back: Immunosuppression

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Decreased venous return indicate or lead to?
back: Reflex tachycardia

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Decreased RBF, GFR, UOP indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ renin-angiotensin → vasopressin → ↑ SVR, ↑ MAP

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Activation of the sympathetic response indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ SVR, ↑ MAP

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Decreased total hepatic blood flow and bowel circulation indicate or lead to?
back: May be counterbalanced by the direct splanchnic vasodilation caused by hypercapnia

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does CO absorption indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ PCO ↑ E CO → some vasodilation (direct effect of CO ) → but sympathetic response from hypercarbia (indirect effect of CO ) and increased IAP dominate → ↑ SVR ↑ MAP

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what value(s) complete this note: “CO levels reach maximum level at ___ minutes of mechanical ventilation”?
back: CO levels reach maximum level at 40 minutes of mechanical ventilation

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Upward movement of diaphragm indicate or lead to?
back: Inadvertent endobronchial intubation

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Basal atelectasis indicate or lead to?
back: But V/Q matching remains pretty good (HPV) → +/- PaO changes

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Increased intrathoracic pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Increased CVP → decreased cerebral venous drainage → increased ICP (in healthy patients, CPP is maintained)

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what should be recalled about Morbid obesity?
back: Hemodynamic changes are similar to those observed in the nonobese

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what should be recalled about Elderly?
back: Significantly increased SVR and decreased cardiac index (CI)

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Less surgical trauma indicate or lead to?
back: Less pain → faster recovery

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what value(s) complete this note: “Keep pressure between ___ mmHg ⇒ higher pressures can drop venous return (decreased preload) and decreased end organ perfusion (increased afterload)”?
back: Keep pressure between 12-15 mmHg ⇒ higher pressures can drop venous return (decreased preload) and decreased end organ perfusion (increased afterload)

front: In Laparoscopic Surgery, what does Congenital diaphragmatic hernia indicate or lead to?
back: Laparoscopic surgery has a high failure rate → open preferred

front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what does Instability of cervical spine (C1/2 anterior subluxation), TMJ arthritis indicate or lead to?
back: Difficulty intubating

front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what value(s) complete this note: “Axis (C1) held in place by odontoid process (of C2) by transverse axial ligament (of C1)”?
back: Axis (C1) held in place by odontoid process (of C2) by transverse axial ligament (of C1)

front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what does Cricoarytenoid arthritis indicate or lead to?
back: Hoarseness/ Inspiratory stridor → smaller ETT may be necessary

front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what does Debilitation leading to decreased exercise tolerance indicate or lead to?
back: Cardiac risk harder to stratify

front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what does Chronic steroid use indicate or lead to?
back: Perioperative corticosteroid supplementation

front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what should be recalled about Pulmonary?
back: Pleural effusions (most common), nodules and fibrosis (restrictive lung disease)

front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what should be recalled about Cardiac?
back: Constrictive pericarditis, possible cardiac tamponade

front: In Vagal Reflexes, what should be recalled about Oculocardiac?
back: Trigeminal nerve (V branch)

front: In Vagal Reflexes, what does Baroreceptor: carotid sinus indicate or lead to?
back: Glossopharyngeal nerve

front: In Vagal Reflexes, what does Bezold-Jarisch reflex: noxious stimuli detected by left ventricle indicate or lead to?
back: Vagus nerve (afferent) → vagus nerve (efferent) → hypotension, bradycardia, coronary artery dilation

front: In Vagal Reflexes, what does Can be protective when MI triggers this indicate or lead to?
back: Increased myocardial supply, decreased demand

front: In Vagal Reflexes, what does Counterproductive when triggered by decreased venous return (e.g. venous pooling of blood in beach chair position indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased preload → Bezold-Jarisch → hypotension, bradycardia)

front: In Vagal Reflexes, what does Postulated mechanism: decreased preload indicate or lead to?
back: Hypercontractility of the heart → noxious LV stimulus

front: In Preoperative Cardiac Evaluation: Indications, what value(s) complete this note: “Acute MI < ___ days”?
back: Acute MI < 7 days

front: In Preoperative Cardiac Evaluation: Indications, what value(s) complete this note: “Recent MI < ___ days”?
back: Recent MI < 30 days

front: In Preoperative Cardiac Evaluation: Indications, what value(s) complete this note: “Severe aortic stenosis (max velocity > ___, mean pressure > ___, area < ___ cm ___ ) or symptomatic”?
back: Severe aortic stenosis (max velocity > 4, mean pressure > 40, area < 1 cm 2 ) or symptomatic

front: In Preoperative Cardiac Evaluation: Indications, what value(s) complete this note: “SVT (including afib) with HR > ___ at rest”?
back: SVT (including afib) with HR > 100 at rest

front: In High FiO2: Adverse Pulmonary Effects, what does Blunts hypoxic vasoconstriction indicate or lead to?
back: More perfusion than ventilation → shunt physiology

front: In High FiO2: Adverse Pulmonary Effects, what does Relative decrease in nitrogen content in alveoli indicate or lead to?
back: Once oxygen is absorbed → microatelectasis (nitrogen would have not been absorbed and could have “kept alveoli open”

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Open and semi-open breathing systems indicate or lead to?
back: Eliminate CO by venting exhaled gases to atmosphere

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Semi-closed and closed breathing systems indicate or lead to?
back: Eliminate CO by chemical neutralization

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Plain calcium hydroxide indicate or lead to?
back: Very little absorbance of CO → catalysts need to be added
