front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does NaOH and KOH used as catalysts indicate or lead to?
back: Leads to formation of bad stuff: heat, compound A, CO, and TFA

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what should be recalled about Traditional Absorbents?
back: Barium hydroxide (no longer manufactured) and soda lime

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Barium hydroxide: 80% calcium hydroxide, 20% barium hydroxide indicate or lead to?
back: Low water content → less absorptive capacity than soda lime

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what should be recalled about Soda lime?
back: 80% calcium hydroxide, 15% water, 4% NaOH, 1% KOH

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Newer Absorbents: Sodasorb, Medisorb, and Drägersorb 800 Plus indicate or lead to?
back: Use less NaOH and KOH

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Newest Absorbents indicate or lead to?
back: No NaOH or KOH → no risk of Compound A, TFA, or CO formation!

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what should be recalled about Amsorb Plus?
back: Uses calcium chloride, calcium sulfate and polyvinylpyrrolidone instead

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Litholyme: uses LiCl instead indicate or lead to?
back: Less heat generated + can use low gas flows to conserve sevoflurane

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does Ethyl violet indicate or lead to?
back: PH indicator → colorless when fresh → purple when the pH falls below 10.3

front: In Carbon Dioxide Absorbents, what does But an issue arises when absorbent is not replaced indicate or lead to?
back: NaOH/KOH regenerate → gradually increasing the pH above the threshold for color change → absorbent may be exhausted but will no longer be violet!

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Hypoosmolar effect of irrigating fluids (particularly distilled water) indicate or lead to?
back: Fluid moves intravascularly (absorption)

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Historically, fluids hypoosmolar solutions of glycine, sorbitol, or glucose indicate or lead to?
back: Non-conductive (non-electrolyte containing) → can be used with monopolar resectoscopes

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Now, bipolar resectoscopes used indicate or lead to?
back: Electrolyte-containing crystalloids used → less TURP syndrome

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what should be recalled about Hydrostatic pressure?
back: Height between the patient and irrigating fluid

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what should be recalled about Classic triad?
back: Altered mental status + bradycardia + hypertension (↑ SBP, ↑ DBP, ↑ pulse pressure)

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Cardiovascular: fluid overload indicate or lead to?
back: Hypertension → reflex bradycardia

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what should be recalled about ECG?
back: Wide QRS, elevated ST, ventricular arrhythmias

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Respiratory: fluid overload indicate or lead to?
back: Pulmonary edema → tachypnea, dyspnea, desaturation

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what should be recalled about Neurological?
back: Nausea, restlessness, blindness (glycine), confusion, coma, seizures

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Hematological: hypoosmolarity indicate or lead to?
back: Hemolysis

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Renal: hyperoxaluria (metabolite of glycine) indicate or lead to?
back: Renal failure

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Metabolic: deamination of glycine indicate or lead to?
back: Glyoxylic acid + ammonia → acidosis

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what value(s) complete this note: “Suspend irrigating fluid < ___ cm above patient”?
back: Suspend irrigating fluid < 30 cm above patient

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what value(s) complete this note: “Limit resection time < ___ hour”?
back: Limit resection time < 1 hour

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Use vasopressors to treat hypotension indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased pressure gradient for absorption

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Symptomatic with Na < 120 indicate or lead to?
back: 3% hypertonic saline (not normal saline)

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what value(s) complete this note: “Sodium deficit = (___ - serum Na) * (weight * ___)”?
back: Sodium deficit = (140 - serum Na) * (weight * 0.6)

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what does Replace 50% Na deficit in 24h indicate or lead to?
back: Too rapid correction → central pontine myelinolysis

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what value(s) complete this note: “Do not exceed ___ mL/hr”?
back: Do not exceed 100 mL/hr

topic: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome
front: In Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) Syndrome, what value(s) complete this note: “Stop treatment once Na > ___ or mental status returns to baseline”?
back: Stop treatment once Na > 120 or mental status returns to baseline

front: In PEEP Physiology, what does Major effect indicate or lead to?
back: Increase FRC above closing capacity → improve lung compliance & improve V/Q

front: In PEEP Physiology, what does Decrease in intrapulmonary shunting indicate or lead to?
back: Improves arterial oxygenation

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what does High osmolarity indicate or lead to?
back: Draws fluid intravascular → hypervolemia → increased preload

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what does Transiently decreases serum ionized calcium indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased left ventricular contractility

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what does Leftward shift of oxygen-hemoglobin curve indicate or lead to?
back: Less oxygen delivery → more lactate

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what does Bicarbonate binds H + (what we’re trying to get rid of in acidosis) indicate or lead to?
back: Forms carbonic acid → dissociates into H O + CO → if CO is not rapidly eliminated by lungs → increased PCO

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what does Theoretical reason for why it was formerly recommended in CPR: acidosis lowers fibrillation threshold and decreases response to catecholamines indicate or lead to?
back: Increasing pH with bicarbonate may increase response to epinephrine

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what does Worsens oxygen delivery (bicarb indicate or lead to?
back: Alkalosis → left-shifted oxyHb curve)

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what should be recalled about Overdoses?
back: TCAs, phenobarbital, salicylates

front: In Sodium Bicarbonate, what value(s) complete this note: “Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <___ and serum bicarbonate <___ mEq/L)”?
back: Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1 and serum bicarbonate <6 mEq/L)

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does Clinical course: GI/respiratory illness indicate or lead to?
back: Lower extremity paresthesia → ascending weakness

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what should be recalled about Serious symptoms?
back: Bilateral facial paralysis (bulbar), dyspnea (intercostals), dysphagia

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does Acute Inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculopathy indicate or lead to?
back: Immune reaction usually after infection

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what should be recalled about Autonomic dysfunction?
back: Labile BP, orthostatic hypotension, diaphoresis, tachycardia, conduction defects

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does Immobility indicate or lead to?
back: High risk of DVTs

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does Associated with SIADH in 50% of cases indicate or lead to?
back: Hyponatremia

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does FVC < 20 mL/kg indicate or lead to?
back: ICU monitoring

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does FVC < 15 mL/kg indicate or lead to?
back: Intubation and mechanical ventilation

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does Avoid succinylcholine indicate or lead to?
back: Risk of hyperkalemia from extrajunctional receptors

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what does Avoid NMBA’s if possible indicate or lead to?
back: Both increased and decreased sensitivity reported

front: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Acute Idiopathic Polyneuritis), what should be recalled about Diagnosis?
back: Clinical symptoms + increased protein in CSF (normal cell counts)

front: In Angiotensin, what does Macula densa (in juxtaglomerular apparatus) senses decreased renal perfusion indicate or lead to?
back: Renin cleaves angiotensinogen (in the blood) → angiotensin I → lungs → ACE → angiotensin II

front: In Angiotensin, what value(s) complete this note: “___ minutes till significant levels of angiotensin II are present”?
back: 20 minutes till significant levels of angiotensin II are present

front: In Angiotensin, what should be recalled about Direct effect?
back: Arteriolar vasoconstriction (throughout body and kidney efferent > afferent)

front: In Angiotensin, what does Indirect effect: stimulates aldosterone secretion indicate or lead to?
back: Increased Na+ reabsorption → more volume

front: In Angiotensin, what does Induction indicate or lead to?
back: Hypotension → activation of renin-angiotensin system → return to stable blood pressure in 20 minutes (because this is the time it takes for angiotensin II levels to ramp up)

front: In Angiotensin, what does Patients on ACEI/ARB indicate or lead to?
back: No angiotensin II effects → more pronounced or refractory hypotension

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what should be recalled about The alveolar anesthetic concentration (P or F )?
back: We care about concentration in the alveoli (not blood)

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what should be recalled about Critically important factor in anesthetic uptake and distribution?
back: In equilibrium with the brain

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Gradient between P and P indicate or lead to?
back: Higher = more anesthetic delivered → faster rate

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Increasing ventilation indicate or lead to?
back: Increases/maintains P (which decreases from uptake) → faster rate (this effect is less significant for insoluble anesthetics)

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does One-lung ventilation (mainstem intubation) indicate or lead to?
back: Right-left shunt → slower induction → more significant for insoluble anesthetics → absorbed anesthetic is diluted → less gets to the brain

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Cardiac output indicate or lead to?
back: Higher = more anesthetic cleared from the alveoli → slower rate of F rise

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Solubility in blood indicate or lead to?
back: Higher solubility (higher blood:gas coefficient) → more gas moves from alveoli to blood → slower rate of F rise

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Key point: the greater the uptake from alveoli to blood indicate or lead to?
back: Greater the difference between F and F (F is smaller) → since P is in equilibrium with F → slower induction

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Adult vs. Pediatric indicate or lead to?
back: Pediatric patients have faster rise in F /F

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Pediatric patients have higher MV relative to FRC indicate or lead to?
back: Faster rise in F /F

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Pediatric patients have lower blood:gas partition coefficients indicate or lead to?
back: Anesthetic gases are less soluble in pediatric blood than in adult blood → faster onset in pediatric patients

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Pediatric patients have decreased tissue:blood partition coefficients indicate or lead to?
back: More anesthetic stays in blood and doesn’t get stuck in random tissue (fat, etc.) → more anesthetic gets to the brain

front: In Uptake of Inhaled Anesthetics, what does Pediatric patients have higher distribution of cardiac output to vessel-rich groups (brain) because of lower muscle mass indicate or lead to?
back: Faster induction in kids

topic: Concentrating Effect and Second Gas Effect
front: In Concentrating Effect and Second Gas Effect, what does Even though there was 50% absorption in each case (A vs B), when the starting concentration was higher (B), there was less decrease in concentration (-45% in A vs -34% in B) indicate or lead to?
back: Faster induction

topic: Concentrating Effect and Second Gas Effect
front: In Concentrating Effect and Second Gas Effect, what should be recalled about Second Gas Effect?
back: Nitrous oxide increases the uptake of other agents (similar mechanisms as above)

front: In Sacroiliac Pain, what does SI pain: low back pain (LBP) that is dull and constant indicate or lead to?
back: Exacerbated by standing from sitting position

front: In Sacroiliac Pain, what should be recalled about Provocation tests?
back: Compression and distraction, Gaenslen test (significant hip flexion), posterior pressure

front: In Sacroiliac Pain, what does 2+ positive tests indicate or lead to?
back: 88% sensitive for SI dysfunction

front: In Sacroiliac Pain, what should be recalled about ☆ Differentiate from other causes of low back pain?
back: Discogenic pain, facet pain, and spinal stenosis

front: In Sacroiliac Pain, what should be recalled about Discogenic pain?
back: Worse with spine flexion and increased abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze)

front: In Sacroiliac Pain, what should be recalled about Spinal stenosis?
back: Worse with spine extension, radicular symptoms

front: In Sacroiliac Pain, what should be recalled about Facet pain?
back: Non-radiating, relieved by lying down, not worsened by flexion or extension

front: In Phantom Limb Pain, what does Occurs in the majority (60-80%) of patients after amputations indicate or lead to?
back: 5-15% have severe pain

front: What is the onset in Phantom Limb Pain?
back: The first few weeks following amputation (but can be delayed for years)

front: In Phantom Limb Pain, what should be recalled about Some things that may prevent it?
back: Epidurals, peripheral nerve blocks/catheters

front: In Phantom Limb Pain, what does Medications: TCAs, gabapentin, pregabalin, anticonvulsants, ketamine, memantine indicate or lead to?
back: Last-line: opioids

front: In Phantom Limb Pain, what should be recalled about Non-pharmacological therapies?
back: PT, massage, TENS, acupuncture, biofeedback (mirror), spinal cord stimulation, deep brain stimulation, sympathetic blockade

front: In Vascular Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “SVR = ___ x (MAP – CVP) / CO”?
back: SVR = 80 x (MAP – CVP) / CO

front: In Vascular Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “Normal SVR = ___ dynes.s.cm -___”?
back: Normal SVR = 900 – 1500 dynes.s.cm -5

front: In Vascular Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “PVR = ___ x (mPAP – PCWP) / CO”?
back: PVR = 80 x (mPAP – PCWP) / CO

front: In Vascular Resistance, what value(s) complete this note: “Normal PVR = ___ dynes.s.cm -___”?
back: Normal PVR = 50 – 150 dynes.s.cm -5

front: In Vascular Resistance, what should be recalled about Causes of increased pulmonary vascular resistance?
back: SNS stimulation, catecholamine release, angiotensin, serotonin, hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidemia, N O

front: In Vascular Resistance, what should be recalled about Pulmonary artery hypertension?
back: Congenital shunts (ASD, VSD, PDA), portal hypertension, collagen vascular disease, HIV, cocaine

front: In Vascular Resistance, what should be recalled about Pulmonary venous hypertension?
back: CHF, mitral valve disease, pulmonary veno occlusive disease, left atrial myxoma, extrinsic compression

front: In Vascular Resistance, what should be recalled about Chronic Hypoxia?
back: COPD, ILD, hypoventilation, OSA, cystic fibrosis

front: In Vascular Resistance, what should be recalled about Chronic Thrombosis?
back: PE, tumor emboli

front: In Vascular Resistance, what should be recalled about Pulmonary Vasculature?
back: Sarcoidosis, vasculitis

front: In Autonomic Dysreflexia, what value(s) complete this note: “Occurs with spinal cord injury (SCI) above T7 (splanchnic outflow of the SNS)”?
back: Occurs with spinal cord injury (SCI) above T7 (splanchnic outflow of the SNS)

front: How is Management prevention handled?
back: Neuraxial anesthesia or general anesthesia (sedation not as useful)

front: In Autonomic Dysreflexia, what should be recalled about Hypertension?
back: Drugs with short-half life (sodium nitroprusside), longer-acting agents if persistent

front: In Autonomic Dysreflexia, what does Avoid beta-blockers indicate or lead to?
back: Can worsen reflexive bradycardia

front: In Intraoperative Hypothermia: Consequences, what value(s) complete this note: “Adverse effects of even mild hypothermia (___°C)”?
back: Adverse effects of even mild hypothermia (1-2°C)

front: In Intraoperative Hypothermia: Consequences, what does Poor coagulation indicate or lead to?
back: Increases blood loss and the need for allogeneic transfusions by 20%

front: In Intraoperative Hypothermia: Consequences, what should be recalled about Drug effects?
back: MAC ↓ 15% for every 1°C, prolongation of vecuronium, atracurium by up to 60%

front: In Intraoperative Hypothermia: Consequences, what should be recalled about Thermoregulation center?
back: Preoptic nuclei in the anterior hypothalamus

front: In Brachial Plexus Blocks, what does Interscalene block can miss: C8-T1 indicate or lead to?
back: Medial hand (ulnar sparing)

front: In Brachial Plexus Blocks, what does Axillary block can miss: musculocutaneous nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Lateral forearm

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what should be recalled about 6 nerves?
back: Supratrochlear, supraorbital, zygomaticotemporal, auriculotemporal, lesser & greater occipital

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what does Supraorbital nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Sensation to forehead, anterior scalp, top of the head

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what does Technique: palpate the supraorbital notch below the eyebrow indicate or lead to?
back: Injecting local just superior

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what does Supratrochlear nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Sensation to forehead and anterior scalp

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what should be recalled about Technique?
back: Blocked just medial to the supraorbital site above the eyebrow line

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what does Zygomaticotemporal nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Passes through the temporalis muscle → innervates the temporal area and a small area of the lateral forehead

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what should be recalled about Technique?
back: Blocked at the lateral border of the supraorbital margin

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what does Auriculotemporal nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Innervation to part of the auricle and scalp just above the auricle

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what value(s) complete this note: “Lies deep to the superficial temporal artery (___ cm anterior to the auricle)”?
back: Lies deep to the superficial temporal artery (1 cm anterior to the auricle)

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what does Lesser and greater occipital nerves indicate or lead to?
back: Posterior part of the scalp and top of the head

front: In Awake Craniotomy: Scalp Block, what should be recalled about Greater occipital nerve?
back: Innervates skin of the posterior scalp

topic: Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine
front: In Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine, what value(s) complete this note: “Hemoglobin A1c (≥ ___ ⇒ diabetes)”?
back: Hemoglobin A1c (≥ 6.5% ⇒ diabetes)

topic: Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine
front: In Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine, what value(s) complete this note: “Reflects average glycemic control value over ___ days”?
back: Reflects average glycemic control value over 120 days

topic: Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine
front: In Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine, what value(s) complete this note: “Target hemoglobin A1c ___ for elective surgery”?
back: Target hemoglobin A1c 6-8% for elective surgery

topic: Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine
front: In Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine, what does Measures glycosylation of plasma proteins indicate or lead to?
back: Better in RBC disease states

topic: Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine
front: In Diabetes Testing: Hemoglobin A1c vs. Fructosamine, what value(s) complete this note: “Reflects average glycemia control value over ___ weeks (shorter than A1c)”?
back: Reflects average glycemia control value over 1-2 weeks (shorter than A1c)

front: In Scotty Dog, what does Ear indicate or lead to?
back: Superior articular process

front: In Scotty Dog, what does Eye indicate or lead to?
back: Pedicle

front: In Scotty Dog, what does Nose indicate or lead to?
back: Transverse process

front: In Scotty Dog, what does Neck indicate or lead to?
back: Pars interarticularis

front: In Scotty Dog, what does Body/tail indicate or lead to?
back: Spinous process and lamina

front: In Scotty Dog, what does Foreleg indicate or lead to?
back: Inferior articular process

front: In Scotty Dog, what does Hind Leg indicate or lead to?
back: Opposite inferior articular process

front: In One-Lung Ventilation: Indications, what should be recalled about Protective isolation of each lung to prevent contamination of the healthy lung?
back: Infection (e.g. abscess, infected cyst), massive hemorrhage.

front: In One-Lung Ventilation: Indications, what should be recalled about Control of distribution of ventilation to only one lung?
back: Bronchopleural fistula, bronchopleural cutaneous fistula, unilateral cyst or bullae, major bronchial disruption, or trauma

front: In One-Lung Ventilation: Indications, what should be recalled about Surgical exposure (high priority)?
back: Thoracic aortic aneurysm, pneumonectomy, lung volume reduction, minimally invasive cardiac surgery, upper lobectomy

front: In One-Lung Ventilation: Indications, what should be recalled about Surgical exposure (low priority)?
back: Esophageal surgery, middle and lower lobectomy, mediastinal mass

front: In Autonomic Sympathetic Innervation: Upper Extremity, what does “Thoracolumbar origin” of SNS in general (T1-L3) indicate or lead to?
back: Ventral horn of spinal cord → preganglionic fibers → white communicating rami → paravertebral ganglia →

front: In Autonomic Sympathetic Innervation: Upper Extremity, what should be recalled about Upper extremity SNS innervation?
back: Fibers originate from the T1-T4/T5

front: In Autonomic Sympathetic Innervation: Upper Extremity, what does Stellate ganglion (cervicothoracic ganglion indicate or lead to?
back: Fusion of inferior cervical and first thoracic ganglia)

front: In Autonomic Sympathetic Innervation: Upper Extremity, what does Within these ganglia: preganglionic fibers indicate or lead to?
back: ACh → nicotinic receptors → postganglionic fibers travel with afferent pain fibers to the head, neck, upper extremities, heart, and lungs

front: In Autonomic Sympathetic Innervation: Upper Extremity, what does MI pain, neck pain, arm pain indicate or lead to?
back: Due to widespread sympathetic distribution of fibers

front: In Autonomic Sympathetic Innervation: Upper Extremity, what does Stellate ganglion block indicate or lead to?
back: Treats sympathetically mediated pain (e.g. CRPS of upper extremity)

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what should be recalled about Progression of systemic toxicity (Source?
back: NYSORA)

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what does CNS depression indicate or lead to?
back: Restlessness, disorientation, drowsiness

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what should be recalled about Paresthesias?
back: Mouth and tongue

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what does CNS excitation indicate or lead to?
back: Seizures

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what does Epinephrine: not adding it indicate or lead to?
back: More absorption → more toxicity

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what should be recalled about Location (vascularity)?
back: ITIC PEB-BALLS: IV > tracheal > intercostal > caudal > paracervical > epidural > brachial plexus > Bier block (“IV regional anesthesia”) > axillary > lower limb > subcutaneous

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what should be recalled about Supportive care?
back: 100% oxygen, benzodiazepines for seizures, ACLS (avoid CCB, BB, vasopressin)

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what value(s) complete this note: “ACLS dose of epinephrine should be decreased to ___ mcg/kg (vs. ___ mg in regular ACLS dosing)”?
back: ACLS dose of epinephrine should be decreased to 1 mcg/kg (vs. 1 mg in regular ACLS dosing)

front: In Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST), what does Lipid Emulsion (20%): bolus 1.5 mL/kg over a minute indicate or lead to?
back: Infuse 0.25 mL/kg/min

front: In Sugammadex, what does Modified gamma cyclodextrin indicate or lead to?
back: Binds and encapsulates rocuronium and vecuronium

front: In Sugammadex, what does NMBA-sugammadex complex is renally eliminated indicate or lead to?
back: Not recommended for CrCl < 30

front: In Sugammadex, what value(s) complete this note: “Anaphylaxis (___)”?
back: Anaphylaxis (0.1-3%)

front: In Sugammadex, what does Hypersensitivity reactions (nausea, pruritus, and/or urticaria) indicate or lead to?
back: 7-9% and dose-dependent

front: In Sugammadex, what does Bradycardia (1-5%, dose-dependent) indicate or lead to?
back: Treat with anticholinergic agents

front: In Sugammadex, what value(s) complete this note: “Transient increase in PT/INR and aPTT by ___ for an hour (clinical significance uncertain)”?
back: Transient increase in PT/INR and aPTT by 25% for an hour (clinical significance uncertain)

front: In Sugammadex, what should be recalled about Interferes with other steroidal drugs?
back: Hormonal contraceptives, toremifene (SERM for breast cancer)

front: In Sugammadex, what value(s) complete this note: “May interfere with serum progesterone assay lab test for ___ minutes after ___ mg/kg dose”?
back: May interfere with serum progesterone assay lab test for 30 minutes after 16 mg/kg dose

front: In Sugammadex, what should be recalled about 2 mg/kg?
back: Moderate blockade (at least 2 twitches on Train-of-Four)

front: In Sugammadex, what should be recalled about 4 mg/kg?
back: Deep blockade (PTC 1-2)

front: In Sugammadex, what should be recalled about 16 mg/kg?
back: Emergency reversal after intubation/RSII dose

front: In Sugammadex, what does Physically incompatible with verapamil, ondansetron, and ranitidine indicate or lead to?
back: Flush + avoid giving in same line

front: In Protamine, what does Protein derived from salmon sperm indicate or lead to?
back: Binds heparin → → eliminated by the reticuloendothelial system

front: In Protamine, what does Type 1: rapid protamine infusion indicate or lead to?
back: Histamine + NO → ↓ SVR → hypotension

front: How is Protamine reactions treatment handled?
back: Volume administration, slow protamine administration

front: In Protamine, what should be recalled about Type 2?
back: Anaphylactic (IgE) and anaphylactoid reactions

front: What are the risk factors of Antihistamine pretreatment has shown mixed results?
back: NPH insulin, fin fish allergy, prior protamine exposure

front: How is Shellfish allergy not a risk treatment handled?
back: Epinephrine

front: In Protamine, what should be recalled about Type 3?
back: Catastrophic pulmonary hypertension and RV failure

front: In Protamine, what does Pathophysiology: heparin-protamine complex indicate or lead to?
back: Triggers release of TXA → pulmonary vasoconstriction

front: How is Shellfish allergy not a risk management handled?
back: Stop protamine, inotropic agents (milrinone, isoproterenol), nitric oxide

front: In Protamine, what does No role of histamine indicate or lead to?
back: Antihistamines are ineffective

front: In Thermal Neutral Zone, what should be recalled about Definition?
back: Range of environmental temperatures within which metabolic rate is minimal and normal body temperature is maintained with vasomotor tone alone

front: In Thermal Neutral Zone, what should be recalled about Unclothed full term neonate?
In Thermal Neutral Zone, what should be recalled about Unclothed adult?
In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what should be recalled about Definition?
back: Volume of gas remaining in the lungs after normal tidal volume expiration

front: In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what value(s) complete this note: “FRC = ERV + RV ⇒ average FRC = ___ mL/kg”?
back: FRC = ERV + RV ⇒ average FRC = 30 mL/kg

front: In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what value(s) complete this note: “Males > Females (by ___)”?
back: Males > Females (by 10%)

front: In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what does Highest when upright indicate or lead to?
back: Decreases 10% as your move to supine/prone

front: In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what value(s) complete this note: “Greatest decrease occurs when going from ___° to ___° (supine)”?
back: Greatest decrease occurs when going from 60° to 0° (supine)

front: In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what value(s) complete this note: “No significant change going from ___° to -___° (Trendelenburg)”?
back: No significant change going from 0° to -30° (Trendelenburg)

front: In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what does Beyond -30° Trendelenburg indicate or lead to?
back: Steep Trendelenburg → decreased FRC

front: In Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), what should be recalled about Age?
back: FRC increases with age (along with RV) due to increased lung compliance and loss of elasticity

topic: Chronic Pain Medications that Lower Seizure Threshold
front: What is the mechanism of Tramadol?
back: Weak MOP agonist, inhibits reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine

topic: Chronic Pain Medications that Lower Seizure Threshold
front: In Chronic Pain Medications that Lower Seizure Threshold, what should be recalled about Meperidine?
back: Metabolite normeperidine causes CNS excitation which leads to seizures

front: In Liver Disease Scores, what does Two scores: Child-Pugh (old) and MELD (new) indicate or lead to?
back: Used to predict prognosis in liver failure

front: In Liver Disease Scores, what does Child-Pugh indicate or lead to?
back: 3 labs (PT/INR, bilirubin, albumin), 2 clinical symptoms (ascites, encephalopathy)

front: In Liver Disease Scores, what does MELD indicate or lead to?
back: 2 liver factors (INR, bilirubin) + 3 kidney factors (Cr, Na + , dialysis)*

front: In Liver Disease Scores, what value(s) complete this note: “*Serum Na + was newly added in Jan ___”?
back: *Serum Na + was newly added in Jan 2016

front: In EMLA Cream, what does Prilocaine indicate or lead to?
back: Biotransformed to aminophenols → oxidize Hb to metHb

front: In EMLA Cream, what does Dermal analgesia timeline: satisfactory in 1h indicate or lead to?
back: Peak in 2-3 h → persists 1-2 h after removal

front: In EMLA Cream, what value(s) complete this note: “___h for IV/venipuncture; ___h for skin graft harvesting”?
back: 1h for IV/venipuncture; 2h for skin graft harvesting

front: In EMLA Cream, what does Concomitant Class III antiarrhythmics indicate or lead to?
back: Close surveillance and ECG monitoring

front: In EMLA Cream, what value(s) complete this note: “Infants (< ___mo) receiving treatment with methemoglobin-inducing agents”?
back: Infants (< 12mo) receiving treatment with methemoglobin-inducing agents

front: In ASA Physical Status (PS) Classification, what value(s) complete this note: “Obesity (___ < BMI < ___)”?
back: Obesity (30 < BMI < 40)

front: In ASA Physical Status (PS) Classification, what value(s) complete this note: “___+ moderate-severe diseases”?
back: 1+ moderate-severe diseases

front: In ASA Physical Status (PS) Classification, what value(s) complete this note: “Morbid obesity (BMI ≥ ___)”?
back: Morbid obesity (BMI ≥ 40)

front: In ASA Physical Status (PS) Classification, what value(s) complete this note: “Premature infant PCA < ___ weeks”?
back: Premature infant PCA < 60 weeks

front: In ASA Physical Status (PS) Classification, what value(s) complete this note: “History (>___ months) of MI, CVA, TIA, or CAD/stents”?
back: History (>3 months) of MI, CVA, TIA, or CAD/stents

front: In ASA Physical Status (PS) Classification, what value(s) complete this note: “Recent (< ___ months) MI, CVA, TIA, or CAD/stents”?
back: Recent (< 3 months) MI, CVA, TIA, or CAD/stents

front: In Multiple Myeloma, what does Bone marrow infiltration indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibits RBC production → normocytic, normochromic anemia

front: In Multiple Myeloma, what does Immunosuppression indicate or lead to?
back: Frequent infections (pyelonephritis and pneumonia)

front: In Multiple Myeloma, what does Damage from abnormal antibody production indicate or lead to?
back: Kidney damage (Bence-Jones proteins)

front: In Multiple Myeloma, what does Increased production of RANKL protein indicate or lead to?
back: Activates osteoclasts → bone destruction → bone pain, pathologic fractures, hypercalcemia

front: In Multiple Myeloma, what should be recalled about Neurologic?
back: Neuropathies, vertebral fractures, hypercalcemia (lethargy, confusion, weakness)

front: In Coronary Perfusion, what should be recalled about Two major determinants?
back: CPP and resistance

front: In Coronary Perfusion, what should be recalled about Resting coronary blood flow in the adult?
back: 250 mL/min (1 mL/min/g) or 5% of total cardiac output

front: In Coronary Perfusion, what does Subendocardial pressure > subepicardial pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Inside of the heart more susceptible to ischemia

front: In Coronary Perfusion, what does Coronary stenosis indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ autoregulation → ↓ subendocardial perfusion → ST-depression

front: In Coronary Perfusion, what should be recalled about Cardiac extraction of oxygen?
back: 75-80% (highest extraction ratio of all organs)

front: In Coronary Perfusion, what does Since extraction cannot be increased much more indicate or lead to?
back: More demand is met by higher flow

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what value(s) complete this note: “Berlin Definition (___)”?
back: Berlin Definition (2012)

front: What is the recommended timing for Berlin Definition (2012)?
back: Acute onset within 1 week of a known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory status

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what should be recalled about Chest imaging?
back: Bilateral opacities, not explained by effusion, atelectasis, or nodules

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what should be recalled about Edema origin?
back: Not explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what distinction applies to PAOP ≤ 18 mmhg is not absolutely required?
back: Rules out cardiac etiology

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what should be recalled about Oxygenation?
back: P O /F O ratio with PEEP/CPAP ≥ 5 cmH O

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what should be recalled about Mild?
In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what should be recalled about Moderate?
In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what should be recalled about Severe?
In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what value(s) complete this note: “Lung protective ventilation (V ___ mL/kg IBW, PEEP, Peak pressures < ___)”?
back: Lung protective ventilation (V 4-6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP, Peak pressures < 30)

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what does Inhaled nitric oxide indicate or lead to?
back: Improved V/Q matching

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what does iNO travels to ventilated alveoli indicate or lead to?
back: Vasodilation → more perfusion to ventilated areas

front: In Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), what does Inverse ratio ventilation (e.g. APRV or BiLevel) indicate or lead to?
back: Increased inspiratory time to improve gas exchange

front: In Achondroplasia, what distinction applies to Autosomal dominant (inheritance of 2 genes is fatal)?
back: 80% are spontaneous mutations

front: In Achondroplasia, what does Pathophysiology: abnormal cartilage formation at epiphyseal growth plates indicate or lead to?
back: Early bone fusion

front: In Achondroplasia, what value(s) complete this note: “Usually around ___ feet tall”?
back: Usually around 4 feet tall

front: In Achondroplasia, what does Atlantoaxial instability + foramen magnum stenosis indicate or lead to?
back: Risk of spinal cord damage with laryngoscopy → consider fiberoptic intubation with limited cervical movement

front: In Achondroplasia, what does Facial features (large protruding forehead, short maxilla, large mandible, flat nose, and large tongue) indicate or lead to?
back: Difficulty obtaining a good mask fit

front: In Achondroplasia, what does Significant lumbar lordosis indicate or lead to?
back: May have difficulty placing neuraxial blocks

front: In Achondroplasia, what does Mothers may have pelvic distortions indicate or lead to?
back: Frequently need cesarean delivery

front: In Achondroplasia, what should be recalled about No effect on?
back: Gastric emptying, TMJ mobility

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what value(s) complete this note: “Deoxyhemoglobin absorbs ___ nm (red)”?
back: Deoxyhemoglobin absorbs 660 nm (red)

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what value(s) complete this note: “Oxyhemoglobin absorbs ___ nm (infrared)”?
back: Oxyhemoglobin absorbs 910 nm (infrared)

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what should be recalled about S value = (AC/DC) ÷ (AC/DC) ⇒ derive SpO (S = 1?
back: 1 ratio = SpO 85%).

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what should be recalled about Fractional saturation (co-oximetry)?
back: SaO = OxyHb / (DeoxyHb + OxyHb + MetHb + COHb)

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what should be recalled about Functional saturation (pulse oximetry)?
back: SpO = OxyHb / (DeoxyHb + OxyHb)

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what value(s) complete this note: “Deoxyhemoglobin absorbs at ___ nm (red light)”?
back: Deoxyhemoglobin absorbs at 660 nm (red light)

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what does Methylene blue > indocyanine green > indigo carmine, nitrobenzene, and lymphazurin/isosulfan blue are blue-green dyes indicate or lead to?
back: Absorb light at 660 nm > than at 940 nm → falsely low reading

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what does Fluorescein indicate or lead to?
back: Yellow-orange → minimal absorbance at 660 or 940nm → minimal effect

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what should be recalled about Factors that do not affect SpO?
back: Bilirubin, HbF, HbS, SuHb, acrylic nails, fluorescein

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what does Hypothermia indicate or lead to?
back: Vasoconstriction (but not hyperthermia)

front: In Pulse Oximetry, what value(s) complete this note: “Severe anemia (Hb < ___ g/dL)”?
back: Severe anemia (Hb < 3 g/dL)

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what should be recalled about CYP2C9?
back: Warfarin, ibuprofen, diclofenac, indomethacin, phenytoin

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what value(s) complete this note: “CYP2C1___”?
back: CYP2C19

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what should be recalled about CYP2D6?
back: Variable metabolism among patients

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what does Codeine (avoid in kids) indicate or lead to?
back: Morphine

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what does Oxycodone indicate or lead to?
back: Oxymorphone

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what does CYP2E1: breakdown of inhalational anesthetics indicate or lead to?
back: TFA (des/iso) and hexafluoroisopropanol (sevo)

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what does St. John’s wort indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ CYP3A4 → increased metabolism of alfentanil, midazolam, lidocaine, oral contraceptives, antiretroviral medications, methadone

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what does Protease inhibitors (HIV) indicate or lead to?
back: CYP3A4 inhibitors

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what does Midazolam indicate or lead to?
back: Active metabolite ∝ -OH-midazolam (accounts for <10% of midazolam’s biological activity)

front: In CYP450 Enzymes, what should be recalled about CYP4F2?
back: Leukotriene breakdown

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what does Most common genetic cardiovascular disease (1/500) indicate or lead to?
back: Autosomal dominant, variable penetrance

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what does Myocardial hypertrophy indicate or lead to?
back: Complication rate related to degree of hypertrophy

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what does Systolic anterior movement (SAM) of MV indicate or lead to?
back: Mitral regurgitation

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what does Dysrhythmias indicate or lead to?
back: Main cause of sudden death in young patients

front: How is Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM) treatment handled?
back: Improve diastolic filling, reduce LV outflow obstruction, decrease myocardial ischemia

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what should be recalled about Very high risk patients?
back: Amiodarone or ICD placement

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what should be recalled about For refractory cases?
back: Prosthetic mitral valve

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what should be recalled about Anesthetic Considerations ⇒ Goal?
back: Minimize LVOT obstruction (↑ preload, ↑ afterload, ↓ contractility)

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what does Increase preload (avoid decreased preload indicate or lead to?
back: E.g. propofol)

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what does Avoid tachycardia and maintain sinus rhythm indicate or lead to?
back: Adequate ventricular filling

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what value(s) complete this note: “Careful with abdominal insufflation (insufflate slowly, do not exceed ___ mmHg)”?
back: Careful with abdominal insufflation (insufflate slowly, do not exceed 15 mmHg)

front: In Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM/HOCM), what does Increase afterload (avoid decreased afterload indicate or lead to?
back: E.g. propofol → use etomidate)

front: In Allergies to Anesthetic Drugs, what should be recalled about Most common?
back: NMBAs (succinylcholine), latex, antibiotics (penicillins and cephalosporins)

front: In Allergies to Anesthetic Drugs, what does Local Anesthetics: esters indicate or lead to?
back: Sensitized from PABA-containing cosmetics/sunscreen

front: In Allergies to Anesthetic Drugs, what does Aminosteroid NMBAs indicate or lead to?
back: Sensitized from quaternary-ammonium in cosmetics, toothpaste, detergents

front: In Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Surgery, what does Posterior vessels (RCA, PDA) are performed first indicate or lead to?
back: Avoid manipulation of LAD graft

front: In Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Surgery, what does Distal anastomosis performed first indicate or lead to?
back: Then proximal anastomosis (usually to aorta)

front: In Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Surgery, what should be recalled about Choice of bypass graft?
back: Left internal mammary artery (LIMA) is the best choice for LAD bypass

front: In Drug Metabolism, what is associated with phase i: oxidation and reduction?
back: Usually by CYP450 enzymes in the liver

front: In Drug Metabolism, what is associated with phase ii: conjugation?
back: E.g. glucuronidation

front: In Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO), what does Selective pulmonary vasodilator indicate or lead to?
back: Travels to ventilated alveoli → crosses alveolar-capillary membrane → smooth muscle of pulmonary vessels → activates guanylate cyclase → ↑ cGMP → vasodilation

front: In Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO), what does Preferential travel to ventilated alveoli indicate or lead to?
back: Improves V/Q mismatch by increasing perfusion to well-ventilated areas

front: In Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO), what does Selective pulmonary vasodilator: once in the pulmonary capillary indicate or lead to?
back: NO binds oxyHb → rapidly inactivated and metabolized by oxyHb → nitrite → metHb

front: In Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO), what does Side effects: higher oxides indicate or lead to?
back: Lung injury, pulmonary edema, chemical pneumonitis

front: In Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO), what does MetHb indicate or lead to?
back: Methemoglobinemia → left-shift of oxyhemoglobin curve + SpO false reading ~85%

front: What is the half-life of ino in Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO)?
back: 15-30 seconds (endogenous NO half-life = < 2 seconds)

front: In Caudal Anesthesia, what does Identify the sacral hiatus (formed by non-union of S4-S5 lamina) indicate or lead to?
back: Lies between the two sacral cornu → forms a triangle with the two PSIS

front: In Caudal Anesthesia, what does Sacrococcygeal ligament (extension of the ligamentum flavum) indicate or lead to?
back: Overlies sacral hiatus

front: In Caudal Anesthesia, what does In adults, this ligament becomes heavily calcified indicate or lead to?
back: Harder to do caudals in adults

front: In Caudal Anesthesia, what does In infants, dural sac ends lower at S3/S4 (vs. S1/S2 in adults) indicate or lead to?
back: Higher risk of dural puncture

front: In Caudal Anesthesia, what value(s) complete this note: “Conus medullaris ends at L3 (vs. L1/L2 in adults)”?
back: Conus medullaris ends at L3 (vs. L1/L2 in adults)

front: In Negative Pressure Leak Test, what does Negative-pressure leak test can be performed on most machines indicate or lead to?
back: Positive-pressure test only on some

front: In Negative Pressure Leak Test, what does Tests for leaks in the low-pressure circuit (components from flow control valves indicate or lead to?
back: Common gas outlet)

front: In Negative Pressure Leak Test, what should be recalled about Turn off?
back: Machine main switch, flow control valves, and vaporizers

front: In Negative Pressure Leak Test, what does Squeeze suction bulb and until fully collapsed indicate or lead to?
back: Should stay collapsed for at least 10 seconds

front: In Depolarizing Neuromuscular Blockade: Phase II, what value(s) complete this note: “Phase II blockade occurs with succinylcholine ___ mg/kg or > ___ mins infusion”?
back: Phase II blockade occurs with succinylcholine 3-5 mg/kg or > 30 mins infusion

front: How is Depolarizing Neuromuscular Blockade: Phase II treatment handled?
back: < 0.03 mg/kg of neostigmine

front: In Congenital Syndromes and Anesthetic Considerations, what does Klippel-Feil syndrome: fusion of the cervical spine indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased neck mobility

front: In Congenital Syndromes and Anesthetic Considerations, what does Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) indicate or lead to?
back: Endocardial cushion defects, hypotonia, macroglossia, subglottic stenosis, atlantoaxial instability, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA)

front: In Congenital Syndromes and Anesthetic Considerations, what should be recalled about Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome?
back: Hypoglycemia, macroglossia, organomegaly

front: In Congenital Syndromes and Anesthetic Considerations, what should be recalled about Pierre Robin sequence?
back: Micrognathia, macroglossia, severe upper airway obstruction

front: In Geriatric Cardiac Changes, what does ↑ plasma catecholamines indicate or lead to?
back: Both at rest and during stress → weaker stress response in old people

front: In Geriatric Cardiac Changes, what does Chronic desensitization indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased β-receptor sensitivity

front: In Geriatric Cardiac Changes, what does Increased vascular fibrosis indicate or lead to?
back: Hypertension, SA node dysfunction, diastolic dysfunction

front: In Geriatric Cardiac Changes, what does Early, rapid, passive filling phase indicate or lead to?
back: From pressure gradient between atrium and ventricle

front: In Geriatric Cardiac Changes, what does Slow, active phase indicate or lead to?
back: “atrial kick” → increased reliance on atrial kick in geriatric patients

front: In Amiloride, what does Potassium-sparing diuretics (along with spironolactone, triamterene) indicate or lead to?
back: Risk of hyperkalemia

front: In Amiloride, what does Mechanism: blocks the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibits sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron → loss of sodium and water from the body, but without depleting potassium

front: In Patient Controlled Analgesia (PCA), what value(s) complete this note: “Recommended for patients > ___ years of age who can cognitively understand the concept of a PCA”?
back: Recommended for patients > 6 years of age who can cognitively understand the concept of a PCA

front: In Patient Controlled Analgesia (PCA), what does Not recommended for opioid-naive patients indicate or lead to?
back: Risk of nausea, vomiting, respiratory depression

front: In Patient Controlled Analgesia (PCA), what does Opioid-tolerant patients: no strong evidence either way, but higher potential for underdosing indicate or lead to?
back: So basal rate might be useful

front: In Patient Controlled Analgesia (PCA), what should be recalled about Multimodal?
back: Strong evidence for concomitant use of acetaminophen/NSAIDs

front: In Patient Controlled Analgesia (PCA), what should be recalled about Other agents?
back: Celecoxib, ketamine, gabapentin, pregabalin, lidocaine

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what value(s) complete this note: “Peaks in ___ hours postoperatively”?
back: Peaks in 2-3 hours postoperatively

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what value(s) complete this note: “Keep MAP < ___ or SBP < ___”?
back: Keep MAP < 110 or SBP < 140-150

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what should be recalled about Hematoma formation?
back: 1-2% of patients

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what value(s) complete this note: “___ of patients will require return to the OR as a result of hematoma formation”?
back: 1% of patients will require return to the OR as a result of hematoma formation

front: What are the risk factors of Hematoma formation: 1-2% of patients?
back: Failure to reverse heparin, postoperative intubation

front: How is Hematoma formation: 1-2% of patients management handled?
back: BP control, digital compression, heparin reversal, surgical exploration

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what should be recalled about Respiratory insufficiency?
back: Recurrent laryngeal nerve or the hypoglossal nerve injury, hematoma

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Carotid sinus indicate or lead to?
back: Baroreceptor reflex → located in the adventitia of the carotid bulb of the ICA

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Afferent: pressure sensor indicate or lead to?
back: High pressure → CN 9 → brain

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Efferent: brain indicate or lead to?
back: CN 10 → bradycardia

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Carotid body indicate or lead to?
back: Chemoreceptor reflex → located at the bifurcation of the carotid (into ICA and ECA)

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Afferent: chemosensory (pH and pO ) indicate or lead to?
back: CN 9 → brain (respiratory center)

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Efferent: stimulate respiratory centers indicate or lead to?
back: Increased ventilatory drive

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Bilateral CEA indicate or lead to?
back: Loss of bilateral carotid bodies → ↓ ventilatory response to hypoxemia and respiratory acidosis

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Central chemoreceptors (sensitive to H + and pH ) become the primary sensor for maintaining ventilation indicate or lead to?
back: Respiratory depression can occur with opioid administration

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what does Problematic in COPD indicate or lead to?
back: Dependent on “hypoxic respiratory drive” (peripheral chemoreceptors sense pO )

front: In Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA): Complications, what should be recalled about Neurological dysfunction?
back: Most commonly a result of thromboembolism during CEA

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what does Fat accumulation indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ chest wall and lung compliance → ↑ work of breathing, ↓ventilatory capacity

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what does ↓ ERV indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ FRC → lung volumes below closing capacity in normal tidal ventilation → small airway closure → V/Q mismatch (shunt-like) → hypoxemia

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what should be recalled about Anesthesia and supine positioning worsen this?
back: 50% reduction in FRC occurs in the obese anesthetized patient (vs. 20% in the nonobese individual)

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what does FEV and FVC are normal indicate or lead to?
back: ERV is the most sensitive indicator of the effect of obesity on PFT

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what does Fat has ↑ metabolic requirement indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ oxygen consumption and CO production

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what should be recalled about Body responds with?
back: Increased cardiac output and alveolar ventilation

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what value(s) complete this note: “Head up positioning > ___°”?
back: Head up positioning > 30°

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what does CPAP or PEEP during preoxygenation indicate or lead to?
back: Improved FRC, decreased V/Q mismatch and atelectasis

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what value(s) complete this note: “Use of PEEP intraoperatively (___ cmH O)”?
back: Use of PEEP intraoperatively (10 cmH O)

front: In Obesity: Respiratory Physiology, what should be recalled about Not helpful?
back: Use of higher tidal volume (13-22 mL/kg)

topic: Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing
front: In Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing, what value(s) complete this note: “Infants work of breathing ___x higher than adults”?
back: Infants work of breathing 3x higher than adults

topic: Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing
front: In Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing, what does Smaller airway diameters indicate or lead to?
back: Increased airflow resistance

topic: Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing
front: In Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing, what does Increased oxygen consumption indicate or lead to?
back: 2-3x higher than adults

topic: Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing
front: In Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing, what does Highly compliant chest wall indicate or lead to?
back: Poor airway support → functional airway closure with each breath

topic: Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing
front: In Infants vs. Adults: Work of Breathing, what does Decreased diaphragmatic fatigue-resistant type I muscle fibers indicate or lead to?
back: More susceptible to fatigue

topic: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions
front: In Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions, what should be recalled about Anesthesia?
back: Administration of medication to blunt or cause the loss of pain, movements, autonomic function and memory/consciousness

topic: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions
front: In Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions, what should be recalled about Pre-anesthesia evaluation?
back: Within 48 hours immediately prior to any surgery

topic: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions
front: In Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions, what distinction applies to Some elements may be performed prior?
back: Less than 30 days prior to surgery

topic: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions
front: In Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions, what should be recalled about Post-anesthesia evaluation?
back: No later than 48 hours after surgery

topic: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions
front: In Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Definitions, what does Not possible for all patients indicate or lead to?
back: Notation stating the patient unable to participate is ok

topic: Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation
front: In Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation, what does Osmolality indicate or lead to?
back: By checking volume as a surrogate for osmolality

topic: Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation
front: In Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation, what does Volume indicate or lead to?
back: By checking osmolality to see hypovolemia

topic: Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation
front: In Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation, what should be recalled about Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS)?
back: Stimulated by systemic hypotension

topic: Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation
front: In Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation, what does Vasopressin: stored in posterior pituitary indicate or lead to?
back: Release triggered mostly by arterial hypotension

topic: Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation
front: In Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation, what does Macula densa: high Cl - ions (hyperosmolarity) indicate or lead to?
back: Interpreted as hypovolemia → renin release

topic: Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation
front: In Renal Physiology: Osmolality and Volume Regulation, what does Hypervolemia indicate or lead to?
back: ANP secretion → inhibits RAAS → increased water and sodium excretion

front: In Omphalocele vs. Gastroschisis, what does 5th week of fetal life (pretty early): abdominal contents are extruded from the body covered in a sac indicate or lead to?
back: They return back in during the 10th week → failure of proper/full return of abdominal contents ⇒ omphalocele → covered in a membrane (amnion) which prevents loss of fluid and infections

front: In Omphalocele vs. Gastroschisis, what does Late in fetal life, after abdominal contents have developed indicate or lead to?
back: Ischemia of omphalomesenteric artery → ischemia of abdominal wall → abdominal organs herniate through the abdominal wall defect ⇒ gastroschisis → no membrane (amnion) covering → highly susceptible to fluid loss and infections

front: In Omphalocele vs. Gastroschisis, what value(s) complete this note: “Rarely associated with congenital anomalies except for intestinal atresia (___ of cases)”?
back: Rarely associated with congenital anomalies except for intestinal atresia (10% of cases)

front: In Omphalocele vs. Gastroschisis, what does Large fluid losses and replacement indicate or lead to?
back: Arterial-line may be necessary

front: In Omphalocele vs. Gastroschisis, what does Ventilation can be challenging indicate or lead to?
back: Intubation and mechanical ventilation are usually needed

front: In Omphalocele vs. Gastroschisis, what value(s) complete this note: “Consider staged repair of gastroschisis if CVP increases >___ or intragastric pressure increases > ___”?
back: Consider staged repair of gastroschisis if CVP increases >4 or intragastric pressure increases > 20

front: In Perioperative Aspirin, what does Mechanism: aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibits TXA formation → inhibits platelet aggregation

front: In Perioperative Aspirin, what does Irreversible: Platelets cannot produce new COX-1 indicate or lead to?
back: Return of normal function when new platelets are formed (2-5)

front: In Perioperative Aspirin, what does Acute withdrawal of aspirin indicate or lead to?
back: Rebound hypercoagulable state

front: In Perioperative Aspirin, what should be recalled about Definitely continue for?
back: Most major vascular surgeries, CABG, CEA

front: In Ankle Block, what does Superficial peroneal indicate or lead to?
back: Branch of common peroneal → purely sensory to anterior leg and ankle

front: In Ankle Block, what does Deep peroneal indicate or lead to?
back: Branch of common peroneal → sensory innervation to first web space

front: In Ankle Block, what does Saphenous indicate or lead to?
back: Terminal branch of femoral nerve → sensory to medial leg, ankle and foot

front: In Ankle Block, what does Posterior tibial indicate or lead to?
back: Sensory to plantar foot

front: In Ankle Block, what does Sural indicate or lead to?
back: Purely sensory to dorsolateral foot

front: In Acute Normovolemic Hemodilution (ANH), what does Remove patient’s blood before surgery indicate or lead to?
back: Maintaining euvolemia with crystalloids (1:3) or colloids (1:1.5)

front: In Acute Normovolemic Hemodilution (ANH), what does At the end of surgery indicate or lead to?
back: Autologous whole blood is reinfused → replaces RBCs, platelets, factors

front: In Acute Normovolemic Hemodilution (ANH), what should be recalled about Consider for?
back: Expected EBL >50% of blood volume, blood antibodies, rare blood type, Jehovah’s Witness

front: In Acute Normovolemic Hemodilution (ANH), what should be recalled about Contraindications?
back: Anemia, cardiac disease, infection

front: In Acute Normovolemic Hemodilution (ANH), what should be recalled about Significant cardiovascular problems?
back: Uncontrolled hypertension, aortic stenosis, recent MI/CVA

front: In Buprenorphine, what does Mechanism: thebaine derivative indicate or lead to?
back: Partial MOP agonist, KOP antagonist

front: In Buprenorphine, what does High affinity for MOP indicate or lead to?
back: Prolonged clinical effects (up to 6 hrs) despite 3 hr elimination t

front: In Buprenorphine, what should be recalled about Potency?
back: 25-40x as potent as morphine

front: In Buprenorphine, what does But has a ceiling effect (respiratory > analgesia) indicate or lead to?
back: Safety against abuse, respiratory depression

front: In Buprenorphine, what should be recalled about Route of administration?
back: Sublingual (low oral bioavailability)

front: In Buprenorphine, what should be recalled about Mixed with naloxone (4?
back: 1 ratio) to prevent IV abuse: naloxone has low sublingual bioavailability

front: In Humidity, what should be recalled about Absolute humidity?
back: The actual mass of water vapor in a given volume of air

front: In Humidity, what should be recalled about Example?
back: When air is heated (and no additional water is added):

front: In Preoperative Upper Respiratory Tract Infections (URIs), what should be recalled about Increased risk of perioperative complications?
back: Cough, laryngospasm, bronchospasm, croup, pneumonia

front: In Preoperative Upper Respiratory Tract Infections (URIs), what does Some surgeries are to decrease URIs indicate or lead to?
back: Cancelling these for an existing mild URI is counterproductive

front: In Preoperative Upper Respiratory Tract Infections (URIs), what does LMA may be a better option in URI indicate or lead to?
back: Tracheal tubes can irritate the inflamed airways more than an LMA

front: In Preoperative Upper Respiratory Tract Infections (URIs), what value(s) complete this note: “Takes ___ weeks for airway to recover from viral URI”?
back: Takes 6 weeks for airway to recover from viral URI

front: In Tracheoesophageal Fistula (TEF), what does Significant ventilation problems in the neonatal period indicate or lead to?
back: Stomach may need decompression

front: In Tracheoesophageal Fistula (TEF), what does Cardiac defects (VSD, ASD, ToF, coarctation) indicate or lead to?
back: Most common association → get an echo

front: In Tracheoesophageal Fistula (TEF), what does Renal defects indicate or lead to?
back: Second most common association

front: In Tracheoesophageal Fistula (TEF), what does Gastrointestinal defects (less common than cardiac) indicate or lead to?
back: Anal atresia

front: What is the mechanism of Promethazine?
back: Anticholinergic (solitary tract nucleus), D antagonist (in CTZ), H blocker

front: In Promethazine, what should be recalled about Anticholinergic?
back: Dry mouth, urinary retention, blurred vision

front: In Promethazine, what should be recalled about Antihistamine?
back: Sedation (like diphenhydramine)

front: In Promethazine, what should be recalled about Antidopaminergic?
back: Akathisia (agitated/restless), dyskinesia (abnormal/involuntary movements)

front: In Promethazine, what should be recalled about Clinical use?
back: Rescue therapy for PONV (when ondansetron was already given prophylactically)

front: In Trigeminal Neuralgia, what should be recalled about Clinical?
back: Sharp, shooting, lightning like or electrical sensation that typically lasts seconds to minutes in the distribution of one or more branches of the trigeminal nerve (V -V > V > V )

front: In Trigeminal Neuralgia, what should be recalled about Triggers?
back: Upper lip, nose, chewing, brushing teeth, breeze

front: In Trigeminal Neuralgia, what should be recalled about First-line treatment?
back: Carbamazepine (sodium channel inhibitor)

front: In Trigeminal Neuralgia, what does Significant drug-drug interactions indicate or lead to?
back: Induces hepatic microsomal enzymes

front: In Trigeminal Neuralgia, what does Black box warnings: aplastic anemia and agranulocytosis indicate or lead to?
back: Monitor CBC

front: In Trigeminal Neuralgia, what should be recalled about Other treatments?
back: Oxcarbazepine (fewer side effects, no CBC), lamotrigine, baclofen, gabapentin, microvascular decompression, percutaneous neurolytic procedures

front: In Trigeminal Neuralgia, what should be recalled about Imaging?
back: MRI of the brain with gadolinium with special attention to Meckel's cave (gasserian ganglion)

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what does Obese patients indicate or lead to?
back: Increased butyrylcholinesterase activity → relative resistance to succinylcholine

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what does Hydromorphone indicate or lead to?
back: 3-glucuronide metabolite: accumulates in renal failure → neuroexcitation & cognitive impairment

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what does Dehydration will increase the strong ion deficit (SID) indicate or lead to?
back: Acidosis

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what does Child functioning at a high cognitive level indicate or lead to?
back: More likely to have increased preoperative anxiety

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what value(s) complete this note: “Of residents with a substance use disorder who continue training, at least ___ will eventually relapse”?
back: Of residents with a substance use disorder who continue training, at least 40% will eventually relapse

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what does Lorazepam indicate or lead to?
back: Glucuronidation in the liver

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what value(s) complete this note: “Prazosin is a selective alpha-___ receptor antagonist”?
back: Prazosin is a selective alpha-1 receptor antagonist

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what should be recalled about ASA Guidelines?
back: Backup power source should be available during delivery of an office-based anesthetic

front: In ITE 2015: Missed Questions, what does In COPD indicate or lead to?
back: Oxygen administration → hypercapnia primarily due to V/Q mismatch

front: In Anterior Mediastinal Mass, what should be recalled about Two most feared complications?
back: Complete airway obstruction and cardiovascular collapse from compression

front: In Anterior Mediastinal Mass, what does More likely in children indicate or lead to?
back: They have more compressible cartilaginous airway structures

front: In Anterior Mediastinal Mass, what does Relaxation of bronchial smooth muscle indicate or lead to?
back: Greater compression of structures

front: In Anterior Mediastinal Mass, what does Paralysis indicate or lead to?
back: Eliminates caudal movement of diaphragm

front: In Burns: Fluid Resuscitation, what should be recalled about Parkland formula?
back: 4 mL x TBSA Burned (%) x Weight (kg) = volume to resuscitate over first 24h

front: In Burns: Fluid Resuscitation, what does First half over 8 hours indicate or lead to?
back: Second half over 16 hours

front: In Burns: Pharmacological Sequelae, what does Increased free fraction of opioids indicate or lead to?
back: Normally decreases opioid requirements

front: In Burns: Pharmacological Sequelae, what does Increased free fraction of benzodiazepines indicate or lead to?
back: Lower doses needed

front: In Burns: Pharmacological Sequelae, what should be recalled about Local anesthetics and β-blockers?
back: Higher doses needed

front: In Burns: Pharmacological Sequelae, what does Rapid proliferation of extrajunctional ACh-R indicate or lead to?
back: Avoid succinylcholine after 24 hours

front: In Burns: Pharmacological Sequelae, what value(s) complete this note: “Contraindicated up to ___ year after burns”?
back: Contraindicated up to 1 year after burns

front: In Burns: Pharmacological Sequelae, what does Resistance to nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade indicate or lead to?
back: Starts 1 week after burn → peaks at 5-6 weeks

topic: Body Weight to Use for Drug Calculations
front: In Body Weight to Use for Drug Calculations, what should be recalled about Ideal body weight (IBW)?
back: Rocuronium, vecuronium

topic: Body Weight to Use for Drug Calculations
front: In Body Weight to Use for Drug Calculations, what should be recalled about Lean body weight (LBW)?
back: Induction dose of propofol, fentanyl, thiopental

topic: Body Weight to Use for Drug Calculations
front: In Body Weight to Use for Drug Calculations, what should be recalled about Total (actual) body weight (TBW)?
back: Succinylcholine, maintenance infusion dose of propofol

front: In Drug Fever, what should be recalled about Antibiotics?
back: Amphotericin, cephalosporins, penicillins, rifampin, streptokinase, and vancomycin

front: In Drug Fever, what should be recalled about Antiarrhythmics?
back: Procainamide, quinidine

front: In Drug Fever, what should be recalled about Anticonvulsants?
back: Phenytoin, carbamazepine

front: In Drug Fever, what should be recalled about Others?
back: Cimetidine, hydralazine

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what does Increased BBB permeability indicate or lead to?
back: More susceptible to inflammatory mediators and neurotoxins

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what does Brain mass starts decreasing at age 50 indicate or lead to?
back: Nonuniform → more white matter lost than gray matter

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what does Most loss in prefrontal cortex indicate or lead to?
back: Gyral atrophy, widened sulci, larger ventricles, more subdural space

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what should be recalled about Regional reduction in neurotransmitters and their receptors?
back: ACh, dopamine, 5-HT

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what does However, overall CNS activity reduced indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased ventilatory response to hypoxia and hypercarbia

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what does Loss of myelinated fibers in the spinal cord, decreased CSF volume, smaller epidural space, increased permeability of the dura indicate or lead to?
back: Increased spread of local anesthetics

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what should be recalled about Increased sensitivity to?
back: Propofol, benzodiazepines, opioids, volatile agents (MAC)

front: In Aging: Nervous System Changes, what should be recalled about No change in sensitivity to?
back: Etomidate, thiopental, NMBAs

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what value(s) complete this note: “TEE has poor sensitivity (___) for direct visualization of PE”?
back: TEE has poor sensitivity (50%) for direct visualization of PE

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what value(s) complete this note: “Increased pulmonary vascular resistance (PAT < ___ msec)”?
back: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance (PAT < 80 msec)

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what should be recalled about RV enlargement (RV?
back: LV end-diastolic area >0.6)

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what value(s) complete this note: “Tricuspid regurgitation (max flow velocity >___ m/sec)”?
back: Tricuspid regurgitation (max flow velocity >2.7 m/sec)

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what should be recalled about Most common sign?
back: Tachypnea

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what should be recalled about Most common symptom?
back: Dyspnea

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what should be recalled about Modified Wells criteria for PE pretest probability?
back: HR, prior DVT, prior PE, history of cancer, immobilization, recent surgery, hemoptysis, likelihood of PE diagnosis

front: In Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis with TEE, what should be recalled about Imaging?
back: CTPA, V/Q scan, pulmonary angiography

front: In Anemia: CV Compensation, what should be recalled about Three main mechanisms?
back: Increased CO, increased Hb O unloading, redistribution to brain and heart

front: In Anemia: CV Compensation, what does Decreased blood viscosity indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased resistance (afterload) → increased SV

front: In Anemia: CV Compensation, what does Venous constriction indicate or lead to?
back: Increased SV

front: In Anemia: CV Compensation, what does Increased contractility indicate or lead to?
back: Increased SV

front: In Anemia: CV Compensation, what does Increased 2,3-DPG indicate or lead to?
back: Right-shift of oxyHb curve (increased P )

front: In Anemia: CV Compensation, what does SNS stimulation indicate or lead to?
back: Vasoconstriction of splanchnic, skeletal, and cutaneous circulation

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about General?
back: Weight loss, nervousness, heat intolerance, warm/moist skin

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Cardiovascular?
back: Tachycardia, ↑ LVEF, atrial fibrillation, wide pulse pressure (high SBP + low DBP)

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what does Increased β and β adrenoceptor density (also seen in MI and chronic beta-blocker use) indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperadrenergic state despite normal catecholamine levels

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Neurologic?
back: Nervousness, tremor, hyperactive reflexes

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Gastrointestinal?
back: Diarrhea

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Musculoskeletal?
back: Muscle weakness, stiffness

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Hematologic?
back: Mild anemia, thrombocytopenia

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Preoperative preparation?
back: 7 to 14 days of propranolol and potassium iodide

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what does Beta-blockers do not prevent thyroid storm indicate or lead to?
back: Use steroids

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what does Increased incidence of myasthenia gravis and weakness indicate or lead to?
back: Careful NMBA use

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what does Undiagnosed/untreated hyperthyroid patient indicate or lead to?
back: Stress/surgery → thyroid storm

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Manifestations?
back: Hyperthermia, tachycardia, dysrhythmias, MI, CHF, agitation, confusion

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Differential?
back: MH, pheochromocytoma, light anesthesia

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Fever?
back: Acetaminophen, cooling blankets, meperidine for shivering

front: In Hyperthyroidism, what should be recalled about Heart failure?
back: Digoxin, especially in afib with RVR

front: In Ventilation Distribution, what does But there is relatively more ventilation at the apex indicate or lead to?
back: V/Q approaches infinity → dead space physiology

front: In Ventilation Distribution, what does “Zone 1” indicate or lead to?
back: Where P > P → ventilation but no perfusion → dead space

front: In Ventilation Distribution, what does Normally minimal in spontaneous breathing indicate or lead to?
back: But can be significant in low CO or high PEEP

front: In Ventilation Distribution, what does Relatively more perfusion at the base indicate or lead to?
back: V/Q approaches 0 → shunt physiology → lower PO than apex

front: In Ventilation Distribution, what does Transpulmonary pressure: greater in the nondependent alveoli at the apex indicate or lead to?
back: Higher volumes in apical alveoli

topic: Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management
front: In Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management, what should be recalled about Target?

topic: Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management
front: In Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management, what value(s) complete this note: “HbA1c should be ___ (correlates to average Glc of ___ mg/dL) before elective surgery”?
back: HbA1c should be 6-8% (correlates to average Glc of 125-180 mg/dL) before elective surgery

topic: Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management
