front: In Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management, what value(s) complete this note: “Type ___ & ___ Diabetics”?
back: Type 1 & 2 Diabetics

topic: Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management
front: In Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management, what value(s) complete this note: “Add a regular insulin sliding scale or insulin infusion (___ units/hr)”?
back: Add a regular insulin sliding scale or insulin infusion (0.5-2 units/hr)

topic: Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management
front: In Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management, what value(s) complete this note: “Add slow glucose (D5W) infusion to prevent hypoglycemia while NPO”?
back: Add slow glucose (D5W) infusion to prevent hypoglycemia while NPO

topic: Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management
front: In Perioperative Glycemic Control and Insulin Management, what should be recalled about Type 2?
back: Discontinue oral antihyperglycemics night before surgery

front: In Effects of Dopamine, what does Low doses (0.5 – 3.0 μg/kg/min) indicate or lead to?
back: DA agonist → renal vasodilator → increased diuresis and natriuresis

front: In Effects of Dopamine, what does Moderate doses (3.0 – 10 μg/kg/min) indicate or lead to?
back: +β , dopamine agonist → ↑ HR, ↑ CO, ↑ inotropy

front: In Effects of Dopamine, what does High doses (> 10 μg/kg/min): +α , β , dopamine agonist indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ PVR → ↓ CO, ↓ renal blood flow

front: In Effects of Dopamine, what does Indirect effects: metabolic precursor to norepinephrine indicate or lead to?
back: Indirect increased norepinephrine release

front: In Effects of Dopamine, what does Side note: Fenoldopam is a selective DA agonist (6-9x as potent as dopamine) and potent vasodilator indicate or lead to?
back: Used to treat severe hypertension (possible alternative to sodium nitroprusside because it has less side-effects)

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what does IV crystalloids at 30 mL/kg within first 3 hours indicate or lead to?
back: Reassessment → additional fluids

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Initial mean target MAP ___ mmHg for septic shock requiring vasopressors”?
back: Initial mean target MAP 65 mmHg for septic shock requiring vasopressors

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Start within ___ hour”?
back: Start within 1 hour

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what does Start with at least two (different classes) antibiotics indicate or lead to?
back: Narrow as culture results are available

front: What is the duration in Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines?
back: 7-10 days is appropriate for most cases (but base this on daily reassessment of response)

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what does Fluid Therapy: crystalloids are essentially preferred indicate or lead to?
back: Use fluid challenge technique

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Can add vasopressin (up to ___ units/min) or epinephrine”?
back: Can add vasopressin (up to 0.03 units/min) or epinephrine

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what should be recalled about Dobutamine?
back: Use if persistent hypoperfusion despite adequate fluids and vasopressors

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “If this is not achieved, IV hydrocortisone ___ mg/day”?
back: If this is not achieved, IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what should be recalled about Only give pRBC if Hgb < 7 (exceptions?
back: MI, acute hemorrhage, severe hypoxemia)

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “< ___K in the absence of apparent bleeding”?
back: < 10K in the absence of apparent bleeding

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “< ___K if there is risk of significant bleeding”?
back: < 20K if there is risk of significant bleeding

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Platelets > ___K for active bleeding, surgery or invasive procedure”?
back: Platelets > 50K for active bleeding, surgery or invasive procedure

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what should be recalled about Immunoglobulins?
back: Do not use IVIG

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what should be recalled about Anticoagulation?
back: Do not use antithrombin

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what should be recalled about TV?
back: 6 mL/kg of predicted body weight

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Limit plateau pressures to ___ cmH O”?
back: Limit plateau pressures to 30 cmH O

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “PaO /FIO ratio < ___ →”?
back: PaO /FIO ratio < 150 →

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Use NMBA for < ___ hrs”?
back: Use NMBA for < 48 hrs

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Do not use beta-___ agonists in the absence of bronchospasm”?
back: Do not use beta-2 agonists in the absence of bronchospasm

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Head of bed to ___ degrees to decrease aspiration risk and ventilator-associated pneumonia”?
back: Head of bed to 30-45 degrees to decrease aspiration risk and ventilator-associated pneumonia

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Target < ___ mg/dL”?
back: Target < 180 mg/dL

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what does Monitor blood glucose every 1-2 hours till insulin infusion stable indicate or lead to?
back: Then every 4 hours

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what should be recalled about Bicarbonate?
back: Do not use sodium bicarbonate in lactic acidemia with pH > 7.15

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what should be recalled about VTE prophylaxis?
back: LMWH > UFH

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what does Stress ulcer prophylaxis: only in patients with risk factors for GI bleeding indicate or lead to?
back: PPI or H blocker

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what does Early enteral feeding + IV glucose indicate or lead to?
back: Avoid TPN as much as possible

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what value(s) complete this note: “Do not use omega-___ fatty acids as an immune supplement”?
back: Do not use omega-3 fatty acids as an immune supplement

front: In Surviving Sepsis 2016 Guidelines, what does Do not routinely monitor gastric residual volumes indicate or lead to?
back: Only use in feeding intolerance + aspiration risk

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what should be recalled about Overall?
back: Decreases myocardial oxygen demand + increases myocardial oxygen supply, increased forward flow

front: What is the mechanism of Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP)?
back: Synchronized counterpulsation to assist the heart

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what does Balloon deflated immediately prior to systole indicate or lead to?
back: Vacuum effect → decreased afterload →

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what does Improved systolic unloading of LV indicate or lead to?
back: Reduced preload, LVEDV and LVEDP, and mPCWP

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what does Balloon inflated during diastole indicate or lead to?
back: Increases AoDP (“diastolic augmentation”) → increases LVCPP

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what should be recalled about Timing is very important to proper functioning?
back: ECG or arterial waveforms can be used ⇒

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what does Inflation: end of ventricular systole indicate or lead to?
back: Immediately after closure of the aortic valve

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what should be recalled about Deflation?
back: Start of ventricular systole

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what does Balloon inflated with helium indicate or lead to?
back: Low density → good turbulent flow → ensures balloon inflates/deflates rapidly

front: In Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP), what should be recalled about Contraindications?
back: Severe aortic regurgitation, irreversible cardiac or brain damage

front: In Common Drugs and ECG Changes, what should be recalled about QT Prolongation?
back: Methadone (> 120 mg/day) especially with P450 inhibitors, ondansetron, droperidol

front: In Common Drugs and ECG Changes, what does Hyperkalemia indicate or lead to?
back: QRS Prolongation: ACEI, ARB, K-sparing diuretics, tacrolimus, SCh, heparin, antifungals

front: In Common Drugs and ECG Changes, what should be recalled about PR Prolongation?
back: Digitalis toxicity (can also cause AV block), beta-blockers, CCBs

front: In Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES), what should be recalled about Classic triad?
back: Truncal petechiae + hypoxemia + neurological abnormalities

front: In Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES), what should be recalled about Hypoxemia?
back: PaO < 70 mmHg on FiO 100%

front: In Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES), what value(s) complete this note: “Fever > ___° C”?
back: Fever > 38° C

front: In Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES), what value(s) complete this note: “HR > ___”?
In Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES), what value(s) complete this note: “Respiratory rate greater than ___ breaths/minute”?
back: Respiratory rate greater than 30 breaths/minute

front: In Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES), what does Mechanism: Fat emboli indicate or lead to?
back: Obstruct capillaries to end organs (brain, heart, etc.) → hypoperfusion → inflammatory response → pulmonary endothelial injury → ARDS

front: How is Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES) management handled?
back: Supportive ± corticosteroids (equivocal evidence)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Overall indicate or lead to?
back: 1970-2010: Ulnar nerve (21%) > Brachial plexus (20%)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Recently indicate or lead to?
back: 1990-2010: Spinal cord (25%) > Brachial plexus (19%) > Ulnar (14%)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Overall indicate or lead to?
back: 1970-2010: Ulnar (32%) > Brachial plexus (27%)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Recently indicate or lead to?
back: 1990-2010: Brachial plexus (27%) > Ulnar (14%)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what should be recalled about For regional anesthesia?
back: Lumbosacral nerve roots (39%) > Spinal cord (29%)

front: What are the risk factors of Ulnar neuropathy (multifactorial and not always preventable)?
back: Older males, both very thin and obese, prolonged postoperative bed rest

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what should be recalled about Time course?
back: Generally delayed onset (3 days postoperatively)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Brachial plexus injury indicate or lead to?
back: Risk factors: abduction > 90°, lateral head rotation, shoulder brace

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Isolated median nerve injury indicate or lead to?
back: Usually after antecubital fossa IV insertion

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what should be recalled about Sciatic and common peroneal injury?
back: Usually from lithotomy position

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Nerve conduction studies indicate or lead to?
back: Both motor and sensory

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does EMG indicate or lead to?
back: Motor neuropathies → to detect:

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Abnormal spontaneous activity (fibrillations, sharp waves) indicate or lead to?
back: Develops 1-4 weeks after injury

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what value(s) complete this note: “That’s why you need a postoperative evaluation and another one ___ weeks later”?
back: That’s why you need a postoperative evaluation and another one 4 weeks later

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Increased insertional activity indicate or lead to?
back: Develops within days after injury

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what should be recalled about Types of nerve injury (in increasing order of severity) (Source?
back: Bradley’s Neurology 7e Ch 107)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Neurapraxia indicate or lead to?
back: Segmental demyelination → conduction block → intact nerve sheath (endo, peri, epi)

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Diabetes can be a risk factor for nerve entrapment indicate or lead to?
back: Neurapraxia

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Axonotmesis indicate or lead to?
back: Demyelination + axon damage → distal axonal degeneration → intact nerve sheath

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Nerve can regenerate (slowly) indicate or lead to?
back: 1 mm/day → good-moderate prognosis for recovery

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does Neurotmesis indicate or lead to?
back: Demyelination + axonal damage + damage to nerve sheath

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what does No function indicate or lead to?
back: Irreversible → requires surgical intervention

front: How is Postoperative Neuropathies prevention handled?
back: Foam/gel pads can prevent skin/soft tissue ischemia and necrosis

front: In Postoperative Neuropathies, what should be recalled about They may or may not prevent neuropathies (Barash?
back: They don’t, ASA Practice Advisory: they might)

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what should be recalled about Two main kinds of pumps?
back: Roller (Fig. A) and centrifugal (Fig. B)

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Roller head indicate or lead to?
back: Occludes (subtotally) tubing → roller heads turns →

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Generates high positive pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Forces fluid forward

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Generates high negative pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Drawing in fluid behind occlusive point

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Can entrain large volumes of air if reservoir is empty indicate or lead to?
back: Modern roller pumps are “servo-regulated” to reduce speed automatically when high pressure or air is detected

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Series of fins/plates inside a cone (impeller) indicate or lead to?
back: Spun using a motor → centrifugal force → sucks fluid in through central inlet → pumps fluid out of peripheral outlet

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Blood flow is pressure sensitive indicate or lead to?
back: Pressure needs to be monitored by a flowmeter

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Increases in distal pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Decrease flow → compensate by increasing pump speed

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what should be recalled about Advantages?
back: Smaller priming volume, lack of gravity drainage, ability of prolonged operation

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Disadvantages: blood stagnation/heating indicate or lead to?
back: Increased risk of thrombi; cavitation → air bubbles; hemolysis

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Roller pumps indicate or lead to?
back: Usually located after the oxygenator

front: In Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB): Types of Pumps, what does Centrifugal pumps indicate or lead to?
back: Normally located between venous reservoir and oxygenator

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does Single most sensitive lead: V indicate or lead to?
back: Placed in the 5th ICS at the anterior axillary line → 75% sensitive for MI

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does Two best leads for overall intraoperative monitoring: II and V indicate or lead to?
back: 80% sensitive for MI

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does II indicate or lead to?
back: For assessing rhythm

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does V indicate or lead to?
back: For detecting MI

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does V4 + V5 indicate or lead to?
back: 90% sensitive for MI

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does II + V4 + V5 indicate or lead to?
back: 96% sensitive for MI

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does Myoglobin indicate or lead to?
back: Rises earliest → sensitive but not specific (surgical trauma releases myoglobin)

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does Troponin I and T indicate or lead to?
back: Peaks in 12 hours → very sensitive and specific → indicator of poor prognosis

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does CK-MB indicate or lead to?
back: Less sensitive than troponin

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does Mainly type 2 indicate or lead to?
back: Demand ischemia → supply-demand mismatch

front: In MI: Intraoperative Diagnosis, what does Type 1 MI indicate or lead to?
back: Plaque rupture → site is nidus for thrombosis → ischemia → less common than Type 2

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does Aorta indicate or lead to?
back: Right + left coronary arteries → epicardium → endocardium → coronary sinus + anterior cardiac veins

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does RCA indicate or lead to?
back: PDA (85%) → superior-posterior interventricular septum and LV inferior wall (“right dominant”)

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does In 15% of people, LCA indicate or lead to?
back: PDA → “left-dominant”

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does Left coronary artery indicate or lead to?
back: LA + interventricular septum + LV (septal, anterior, and lateral walls)

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does LAD indicate or lead to?
back: Septum, LV anterior wall and RV apex

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does LCx indicate or lead to?
back: Lateral wall

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does SA node indicate or lead to?
back: Either RCA (60%) or the LAD (the remaining 40%)

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does AV node indicate or lead to?
back: RCA (90%) or LCx (10%)

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does Bundle of His indicate or lead to?
back: Dual blood supply → PDA and LAD

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does Anterior papillary muscle of the mitral valve indicate or lead to?
back: A dual blood supply → LAD diagonals and LCx marginals

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does Posterior papillary of the mitral valve is indicate or lead to?
back: Only by the PDA → more vulnerable to ischemia

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does Anterior wall indicate or lead to?
back: Dual supply → RCA acute marginal + LAD diagonals

front: In Coronary Artery Anatomy, what does Posterior wall indicate or lead to?
back: PDA

front: In Intraosseous Access, what does Technically easier indicate or lead to?
back: First-attempt success rate is 50% higher vs. central line placement

front: In Intraosseous Access, what does Faster indicate or lead to?
back: 4-5x faster than central line placement

front: In Intraosseous Access, what does Low infection risk indicate or lead to?
back: 0.6% incidence of osteomyelitis vs. 15% from femoral central access

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Four recommended sites?
back: Manubrium, proximal humerus, proximal and distal tibia (not mid-tibia)

front: In Intraosseous Access, what does Manubrium (sternum) indicate or lead to?
back: Arrow EZ-IO should not be placed in sternum

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Drainage?
back: Azygos and internal mammary veins

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Advantage?
back: Fastest uptake of drugs and fluids into systemic circulation (80-110s to peak concentration)

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Disadvantages?
back: Increased risk of puncture of heart or great vessels with longer needles

front: In Intraosseous Access, what does Proximal humerus indicate or lead to?
back: Preferred site

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Drainage?
back: Axillary vein

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Advantages?
back: Higher flow rate (2x) vs. tibia

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Disadvantages?
back: Lower first-attempt success rate (vs. tibia) + higher rate of needle dislodgement

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Drainage?
back: Popliteal vein

front: In Intraosseous Access, what does Advantages: highest first-attempt success rate indicate or lead to?
back: Preferred by most

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Disadvantage?
back: Lower flow rates vs proximal humerus

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Drainage?
back: Greater saphenous vein

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Advantages?
back: High first-attempt success rate

front: In Intraosseous Access, what value(s) complete this note: “Complications of IO access (<___)”?
back: Complications of IO access (<1%)

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Major?
back: Fat/marrow emboli (but no clinically significant changes found in PCO , shunting, or mortality), Fracture, Infection (but lower than central access), growth plate injury, compartment syndrome

front: In Intraosseous Access, what should be recalled about Minor?
back: Pain with infusion (alleviated by lidocaine flush), needle dislodgement (especially with humerus)

front: In Local Anesthetics: Alkalinization, what value(s) complete this note: “pH of commercial preparations of local anesthetics is acidic (pH ___ to ___)”?
back: PH of commercial preparations of local anesthetics is acidic (pH 3.9 to 6.4)

front: In Local Anesthetics: Alkalinization, what does pKa of local anesthetics ranges from 7.6 to 8.9 indicate or lead to?
back: Weak bases → proton acceptor

front: In Local Anesthetics: Alkalinization, what does Site of action is intracytoplasmic Na-channel indicate or lead to?
back: Local anesthetic must be neutral to cross cell membrane

front: In Local Anesthetics: Alkalinization, what does When pH is lower than pKa indicate or lead to?
back: Accepts proton → more of the ionized form → less likely to cross membrane

front: In Local Anesthetics: Alkalinization, what does But ropivacaine and bupivacaine are not indicate or lead to?
back: Can precipitate in 5-10 minutes

front: What is the mechanism of Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I)?
back: Inhibits ACE (lung) which converts angiotensin I to the active angiotensin II

front: In Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I), what should be recalled about Cardiovascular effects?
back: Decreased PVR, CO normal/increased, filling pressure unchanged.

front: In Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I), what should be recalled about Hormonal effects?
back: Decreased angiotensin-aldosterone, norepinephrine, plasma antidiuretic hormone

front: In Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I), what does Captopril and enalapril indicate or lead to?
back: Both are eliminated by renal excretion → adjust in renal insufficiency

front: In Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I), what does Enalapril indicate or lead to?
back: Converted by hepatic esterase → active compound enalaprilat

front: What are the adverse effects of Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I)?
back: Renal failure, cough, angioedema, hyperkalemia

front: In Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I), what should be recalled about Contraindications?
back: Pregnancy (teratogen), bilateral renal artery stenosis and history of angioedema

front: In Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I), what does Treatment of intraoperative ACE-inhibitor related hypotension: norepinephrine first line indicate or lead to?
back: Then vasopressin

front: In Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACE-I), what value(s) complete this note: “ABA Content Outline I.C.___.c.___, Barash ___e Ch ___, ABA ITE Gaps In Knowledge”?
back: ABA Content Outline I.C.3.c.7, Barash 7e Ch 15, ABA ITE Gaps In Knowledge

front: In Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE), what does Incidence: about 0.1% (1 in 1,000) indicate or lead to?
back: Much higher (4%) if laryngospasm occurs

front: In Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE), what value(s) complete this note: “___ happen postextubation, ___ happen during initial airway management”?
back: 75% happen postextubation, 25% happen during initial airway management

front: What are the risk factors of Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE)?
back: Airway obstruction, male, young, physically fit, HEENT surgery

front: In Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE), what should be recalled about Clinical signs?
back: Hypoxia, pink frothy fluid, bilateral patchy infiltrates on chest radiograph, normal hemodynamics

front: In Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE), what does Intense SNS activation indicate or lead to?
back: Hypertension → central displacement of blood volume

front: In Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE), what does Together these factors increase the transcapillary pressure gradient indicate or lead to?
back: Acute pulmonary edema

front: How is Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE) management handled?
back: Mostly supportive

front: In Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE), what does Severe NPPE indicate or lead to?
back: Intubation and mechanical ventilation with PEEP

front: In Negative-Pressure Pulmonary Edema (NPPE), what value(s) complete this note: “Recovery usually occurs in ___ hours”?
back: Recovery usually occurs in 12-48 hours

front: In Indications for One-Lung Ventilation, what should be recalled about Surgical exposure (high priority)?
back: Thoracic aortic aneurysm, pneumonectomy, lung volume reduction, minimally invasive cardiac surgery

front: In Indications for One-Lung Ventilation, what should be recalled about Surgical exposure - (low priority)?
back: Esophageal surgery, middle and lower lobectomies, mediastinal mass resection, bilateral sympathectomies

front: In Aging: Renal Changes, what should be recalled about Renal blood flow?
back: Decreases 10% per decade after 40 years of age

front: In Aging: Renal Changes, what should be recalled about GFR?
back: Decrease 8 mL/min/1.73m 2 per decade after 40 years of age (baseline 140 mL/min/1.73m 2 )

front: In Aging: Renal Changes, what should be recalled about Creatinine level?
back: Remains unchanged because the decline in GFR is accompanied by loss in muscle mass

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Advantages?
back: Location (often preferred for patients in C-collars or undergoing carotid/neck procedures), easy to maintain, superior patient tolerance

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Risks?
back: Pneumothorax, subclavian arterial puncture, hemothorax, and mediastinal hematoma

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Insertion point?
back: 1 cm inferior to the midpoint of the clavicle

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Direction?
back: Toward the sternal notch until blood return is noted, avoid going too posterior (pleura)

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Insertion point?
back: Lateral border of the sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle at its insertion point on the clavicle

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Direction?
back: Bisect the angle between the SCM and clavicle.

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Advantages?
back: Predictable anatomical landmarks, short course to the SVC/RA

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Risks?
back: Carotid artery puncture, pneumothorax (more common on the left due to the high-riding apex of the left lung), and thoracic duct cannulation (left side)

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Insertion point?
back: Lateral to the carotid pulse at a 45-degree angle

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Direction?
back: Ipsilateral nipple until blood return is noted

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Preferred in emergent situations (e.g. trauma resuscitation)?
back: Easy to place, far away from vital structures, easy to compressibility in case of a hematoma formation

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Insertion point?
back: Medial to femoral arterial pulse 1-2 cm below the inguinal ligament

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Radiographic confirmation?
back: Tip of central line should be just above the SVC/RA junction (in SVC)

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what does Tip in the RA indicate or lead to?
back: Too deep → can lead to cardiac perforation, cardiac tamponade, dysrhythmias

topic: Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access
front: In Landmarks for Central Line or Femoral Line Access, what should be recalled about Alternative landmarks?
back: Tip above pericardial reflection or within 3rd anterior intercostal space

front: In Glaucoma, what should be recalled about Definition?
back: Progressive disease resulting in destruction of the optic nerve with peripheral to central vision loss that is irreversible

front: In Glaucoma, what should be recalled about Types?
back: Open-angle vs. closed-angle (angle = angle between iris and cornea)

front: In Glaucoma, what does Normally, aqueous humor drains: posterior chamber indicate or lead to?
back: Pupil → anterior chamber → trabecular mesh

front: In Glaucoma, what does Open-angle indicate or lead to?
back: Chronic, more common, 90% → decreased drainage through trabecular mesh

front: In Glaucoma, what does Closed-angle indicate or lead to?
back: Acute → closure of angle between the iris and cornea

front: In Glaucoma, what does Generally, there is decreased aqueous humor drainage indicate or lead to?
back: Increased IOP → optic nerve damage

front: In Glaucoma, what should be recalled about Increased aqueous humor drainage?
back: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (echothiophate), miotic agents/parasympathomimetics (pilocarpine), and ɑ -agonists (e.g. brimonidine)

front: In Glaucoma, what should be recalled about Decreased aqueous humor production?
back: Ɑ-agonists, topical β-blockers, acetazolamide

front: In Glaucoma, what does Echothiophate indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibits serum cholinesterase which normally metabolizes succinylcholine and ester-type local anesthetics (procaine, chloroprocaine, and tetracaine) → avoid these

front: In Glaucoma, what does Mydriasis indicate or lead to?
back: Makes angle between the iris and cornea smaller → decreased drainage → increased IOP

front: In Glaucoma, what should be recalled about Anticholinergics?
back: Scopolamine (but atropine and glycopyrrolate do not cause eye issues)

front: In Glaucoma, what should be recalled about Sympathomimetics?
back: E.g. epinephrine

front: In Glaucoma, what should be recalled about Antihistamines?
back: E.g. diphenhydramine

front: In Glaucoma, what does Corticosteroids indicate or lead to?
back: Increase IOP

front: In CVP Waveform, what does Stiff pericardium indicate or lead to?
back: Impaired relaxation → elevated diastolic filling pressure → elevated V-wave

front: In CVP Waveform, what does When tricuspid valve opens indicate or lead to?
back: Rapid atrial emptying (RV filling) → sharp Y-descent (atrial emptying)

front: In CVP Waveform, what does Diminished RV filling in diastole (due to equalization of diastolic pressures) indicate or lead to?
back: Blunted Y-descent

front: In CVP Waveform, what does Atrial fibrillation indicate or lead to?
back: Absent atrial contraction → a-wave missing

front: In Etomidate, what does Quick onset indicate or lead to?
back: 30-60 sec → rapidly distributed

front: In Etomidate, what does Duration is short indicate or lead to?
back: Typically 3-5 minutes → due to rapid drug redistribution

front: In Etomidate, what does Metabolism: hepatic ester hydrolysis and N-dealkylation indicate or lead to?
back: Inactive metabolites

front: In Etomidate, what does Even in patients with severe liver disease indicate or lead to?
back: Normal duration of action of a bolus dose

front: In Etomidate, what value(s) complete this note: “Metabolites are primarily excreted by the kidneys (___) and bile (___)”?
back: Metabolites are primarily excreted by the kidneys (85%) and bile (13%)

front: In Etomidate, what does Renal disease indicate or lead to?
back: Prolonged elimination half time → but duration is normal (inactive metabolites)

front: In Etomidate, what value(s) complete this note: “Etomidate is highly protein-bound in plasma (___)”?
back: Etomidate is highly protein-bound in plasma (75%)

front: In Etomidate, what does Decreased plasma protein (severe liver or renal disease) indicate or lead to?
back: Can lead to exaggerated drug effect

front: In Etomidate, what does Avoid in seizure disorder indicate or lead to?
back: May activate seizure foci

front: In Etomidate, what does Useful in ECT indicate or lead to?
back: Will maximize seizure duration (methohexital does not affect duration)

front: In Etomidate, what does Avoid in porphyria indicate or lead to?
back: Can trigger acute porphyria

front: In Etomidate, what does Inhibits 11-beta-hydroxylase indicate or lead to?
back: Adrenal suppression

front: In Etomidate, what does Prepared with PEG (35%) indicate or lead to?
back: Pain on injection (more common than propofol), vein irritation, mild hemolysis

front: In Etomidate, what does Propofol, etomidate and thiopental indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased CMRO , CBF and ICP (coupling intact)

front: In Allowable Blood Loss, what should be recalled about Obese male?
back: 60 mL/kg (for obese people, decreased by ~10%)

front: In Allowable Blood Loss, what should be recalled about Adult female?
back: 60 mL/kg

front: In Allowable Blood Loss, what should be recalled about Adult male?
back: 70 mL/kg

front: In Allowable Blood Loss, what should be recalled about Child?
back: 75 mL/kg

front: In Allowable Blood Loss, what should be recalled about Infant?
back: 80 mL/kg

front: In Allowable Blood Loss, what should be recalled about Neonate?
back: 90 mL/kg

front: In Allowable Blood Loss, what should be recalled about Premature infant?
back: 100 mL/kg

front: In Corneal Abrasion, what should be recalled about Clinical Presentation?
back: Eye pain with a foreign body sensation upon awakening from surgery.

front: In Corneal Abrasion, what does Treatment: Symptoms are generally transient indicate or lead to?
back: Antibiotic ointment to prevent bacterial infection

front: In Corneal Abrasion, what should be recalled about Avoidance?
back: Early and careful taping of the eyelids after induction, awareness of dangling objects when leaning over patients, and close observation as patients awaken (may try to rub their eyes with pulse oximeter)

front: In Muscular Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Beckers?
back: Milder form of Duchenne, but cardiac involvement is more common

front: In Muscular Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy?
back: Contractures of elbows, ankles, spine, and humeropectoral weakness + potentially fatal cardiac conduction defects

front: In Muscular Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Limb-girdle Muscular Dystrophy?
back: Cardiomyopathy and atrioventricular conduction defects can occur.

front: In Muscular Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy?
back: Weakness of the facial, scapulohumeral muscles + retinal vascular disease, deafness, and neurologic dysfunction. Cardiac conduction defects and dysrhythmias may occur.

front: In Muscular Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy?
back: Ptosis and dysphagia

front: In Muscular Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Congenital Muscular Dystrophy?
back: Early onset (infancy) of hypotonia, developmental delay, feeding difficulties, and severe respiratory dysfunction but no cardiac involvement

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what does Mechanism (theoretical): inability of ATPase to return Ca 2+ to SR indicate or lead to?
back: Prolonged contraction

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Musculoskeletal?
back: Muscle weakness begins distally, progresses proximally with muscle wasting over time

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Cardiac?
back: AV conduction delay, atrial tachyarrhythmias, diastolic dysfunction, cardiomyopathy, MVP

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Pulmonary?
back: Restrictive disease, hypoxemia, diminished ventilatory response, weak cough

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Endocrine?
back: Diabetes, thyroid issues, adrenal issues, gonadal atrophy, premature balding

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what does Avoid succinylcholine indicate or lead to?
back: Patients have persistent contractures → can worse with succinylcholine → sustained contractures → impossible ventilation and intubation

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what does More sensitive to nondepolarizing muscle relaxants indicate or lead to?
back: Reversal with neostigmine may provoke myotonia → use sugammadex

front: In Myotonic Dystrophy, what should be recalled about Maintain normothermia?
back: Cold and shivering can provoke myotonia

front: In Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement, what should be recalled about Outcomes?
back: Noninferior at 1 year (survival), higher risk of stroke and vascular complications

front: In Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement, what does Femoral artery access indicate or lead to?
back: Valve deployed into aortic annulus

front: In Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement, what does Postdeployment AV node or conduction defect indicate or lead to?
back: Continue transvenous pacing

front: In Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement, what does Neurologic events (stroke) indicate or lead to?
back: Monitor using cerebral oximetry

front: In Perioperative Cardiac Risk Stratification, what should be recalled about RCRI (6)?
back: Ischemic heart disease, CHF, CVA/TIA, insulin use, CKD (Cr > 2), high risk surgery (intraperitoneal, intrathoracic or suprainguinal vascular)

front: In Perioperative Cardiac Risk Stratification, what should be recalled about NSQIP (5)?
back: Surgery type, abnormal Cr, old age, ASA class, dependent functional status

front: In Atrial Flutter, what should be recalled about Atrial flutter with 4?
back: 1 AV conduction

front: In Atrial Flutter, what should be recalled about Stable?
back: Beta-blockers, CCB, or ibutilide (Class 3)

front: In Atrial Flutter, what should be recalled about Unstable?
back: Electrical cardioversion

front: In Atrial Flutter, what does Atrial flutter > 48 hours indicate or lead to?
back: Possibility of LA thrombus → TEE before cardioversion

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Oxygenated blood: Placenta indicate or lead to?
back: Umbilical vein → ductus venosus → IVC → RA → most of it crosses foramen ovale → LA → LV → fetal systemic circulation

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Deoxygenated blood: SVC indicate or lead to?
back: RV → pulmonary artery → patent ductus arteriosus → descending aorta → fetal systemic circulation

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Previously, high PVR and low SVR indicate or lead to?
back: Blood shunted from right side to left side

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does With breathing, alveoli expand indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased PVR

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Increased PaO indicate or lead to?
back: Closure of ductus arteriosus

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Removal of low-resistance placental bed indicate or lead to?
back: Increased SVR

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Increased SVR + decreased PVR indicate or lead to?
back: Closure of foramen ovale

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Disorders that require a patent ductus arteriosus and/or VSD indicate or lead to?
back: “single-ventricle physiology”

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Restricted LV outflow indicate or lead to?
back: Blood needs to be shunted from left to right side → PDA → systemic circulation

front: In Neonatal Circulation, what does Restricted pulmonary outflow indicate or lead to?
back: Blood needs to be shunted from the right to left side for oxygenation → PDA → pulmonary circulation

front: What are the risk factors of Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB)?
back: Reactive airway disease (COPD, asthma), smoking, endotracheal intubation

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what does But in severe bronchospasm indicate or lead to?
back: P can be elevated, V will fall

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what distinction applies to Auscultation may reveal expiratory wheeze?
back: In severe bronchospasm, it can become silent

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what value(s) complete this note: “Hand ventilate with ___ oxygen, stop stimulation/surgery, consider anaphylaxis (look for rash, etc.)”?
back: Hand ventilate with 100% oxygen, stop stimulation/surgery, consider anaphylaxis (look for rash, etc.)

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what should be recalled about Deepen anesthesia?
back: Increased volatile agent concentration, administer induction agent and opioids

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what should be recalled about Bronchodilation among volatile agents?
back: Sevoflurane > Isoflurane > Desflurane

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what does Inhaled bronchodilators indicate or lead to?
back: Most of it gets stuck in circuit → 8-10 puffs initial dose in adult

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what does Decreased bioavailability of inhaled drugs indicate or lead to?
back: Only < 10% reaches lungs from ETT/LMA

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what does IV epinephrine indicate or lead to?
back: 10-100 mcg dose repeated as needed in adults, especially in anaphylaxis

front: In Intraoperative Bronchospasm (IOB), what does IV steroids indicate or lead to?
back: Delayed effects that manifest hours later

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what value(s) complete this note: “Pulmonary system does not mature to viability till ___ weeks of gestation”?
back: Pulmonary system does not mature to viability till 24-26 weeks of gestation

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what value(s) complete this note: “Alveoli continue to increase in number till ___ years of age”?
back: Alveoli continue to increase in number till 8 years of age

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what does Shortly after birth indicate or lead to?
back: Neonate generates negative intrathoracic pressure of 40-60 cmH O to expand alveoli

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what should be recalled about TV?
back: Same as adults per kg of (ideal) body weight

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what does Closing volumes: higher indicate or lead to?
back: Airway collapses more easily

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what does MV: higher (TV same but RR is higher) indicate or lead to?
back: To compensate for higher O consumption in the neonate

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what should be recalled about FRC?
back: Same as an adult, but MV/FRC ratio is higher →

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what does Relatively less FRC oxygen reserve indicate or lead to?
back: Shorter apnea time

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what does Lung compliance is lower (stiffer lungs) but chest wall compliance is higher (pliable ribcage) indicate or lead to?
back: More retractions → less efficient gas exchange → increased work of breathing

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what does Intercostal muscles are poorly developed indicate or lead to?
back: Diaphragm provides most of the ventilation

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what does But diaphragm is immature with lower levels of Type 1 fibers (slow-twitch, high oxidative) indicate or lead to?
back: Faster fatigue

front: In Neonatal Physiology: Respiratory, what should be recalled about ABA Content Outline?
back: II.D.2.f.1

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what should be recalled about There are two types of disposal systems?
back: Active and passive

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Active indicate or lead to?
back: Uses an active vacuum/suction system

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Passive indicate or lead to?
back: Waste gases passively go to a downstream non-recirculating HVAC system → outside air

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what should be recalled about There are two types of scavenging interfaces?
back: Open and closed

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Open interface indicate or lead to?
back: Open to the atmosphere

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Closed interface indicate or lead to?
back: Isolated from the atmosphere by pressure valves

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Open interface indicate or lead to?
back: Necessarily has to be connected to an active disposal system (suction/vacuum)

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Closed interface indicate or lead to?
back: Can be connected to either an active system (suction) or passive system

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Passive indicate or lead to?
back: Needs only a positive-pressure relief valve in case of downstream obstruction

front: In Waste Gas Scavenging Systems, what does Active indicate or lead to?
back: Needs both positive- (for downstream obstruction) and negative-pressure (for excessive suction) relief valves

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what should be recalled about Apnea?
back: Cessation of ventilatory effort at passive end-expiration (FRC)

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what should be recalled about Biot?
back: Ventilatory gasps interposed between periods of ventilation apnea (“agonal ventilation”)

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what should be recalled about Apneusis?
back: Cessation of ventilatory effort with lungs filled at TLC

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what should be recalled about Apneustic ventilation?
back: Apneusis with periodic expiratory spasms

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what does Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG) ⇒ inspiratory center indicate or lead to?
back: Pacemaker for breathing

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what does Electrical zero occurs at end-inspiration indicate or lead to?
back: Apneustic ventilation occurs if DRG inputs are blocked

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what does Pneumotaxic respiratory (upper pons) indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibits inspiratory center

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what does Apneustic Center (mid and lower pons) indicate or lead to?
back: Activates inspiratory center

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what should be recalled about Swallowing and vomiting?
back: Inhibition of inspiration to prevent aspiration

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what does Coughing: tracheal stimulation indicate or lead to?
back: Deep inspiration → forced exhalation against a momentarily closed glottis to increase intrathoracic pressure → expulsive expiratory maneuver

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what does Dyspnea: pressure changes (edema, atelectasis) indicate or lead to?
back: Smooth muscle spindle receptors → dyspnea

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what does Lungs filled indicate or lead to?
back: Chest wall muscles stretched → Golgi tendon organs (tendon spindles) → inhibit inspiration

front: In Central Control of Ventilation, what should be recalled about Normal breathing?
back: ⅔ central chemoreceptors (pCO and pH) + ⅓ peripheral chemoreceptors (O and CO )

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what does Reduced CNS activity indicate or lead to?
back: Impaired ventilatory responses to hypoxia, hypercapnia, and mechanical stress

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what does Loss of lung elastic recoil + altered surfactant production indicate or lead to?
back: Increase in lung compliance → ↓ maximal expiratory flow and ↓ ventilatory response to exercise

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what does Loss of airway elasticity indicate or lead to?
back: Early collapse of the small airways → air trapping and hyperinflation

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what does Loss of alveolar surface area indicate or lead to?
back: Increased anatomic dead space + decreased diffusing capacity → ↓ PaO

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what should be recalled about Decreased?
back: Inspiratory capacity (IC), Vital capacity (VC), total lung capacity (TLC)

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what should be recalled about Increase?
back: Functional residual capacity (FRC), residual volume (RV), closing capacity (CC)

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what does Change in the relationship between FRC and CC (↑) indicate or lead to?
back: V/Q mismatch → increased A-a gradient → ↓ PaO

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what value(s) complete this note: “Progressive decrease in FEV (___ per decade)”?
back: Progressive decrease in FEV (6-8% per decade)

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what does Decrease in the cross-sectional area of the pulmonary capillary bed indicate or lead to?
back: Increases in PVR and PA pressure

front: In Aging: Respiratory Changes, what does Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) is blunted in older adults indicate or lead to?
back: Difficulty with one-lung ventilation

front: In Preoperative Antidepressants, what distinction applies to MAOI antidepressants were historically discontinued preoperatively?
back: Led to withdrawal

front: In Preoperative Antidepressants, what does Can decrease cholinesterase activity indicate or lead to?
back: Need smaller doses of succinylcholine

front: In Preoperative Antidepressants, what does Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) indicate or lead to?
back: QT prolongation → preoperative ECG required

front: In Preoperative Antidepressants, what does Block reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin indicate or lead to?
back: Augmented responses to vasopressor drugs

front: In Preoperative Antidepressants, what does Lithium indicate or lead to?
back: Evaluate electrolytes, BUN, and creatinine

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Macintosh blade?
back: Curved

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Seward?
back: Straight tongue with a curve near the tip

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Miller?
back: Straight blade

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Robertshaw?
back: Gently curved over the distal third, designed to lift the epiglottis like a Mac blade

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Wisconsin?
back: Straight blade with a curved flange that the ETT can pass through

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Polio pattern?
back: Mac blade at a 135 degree angle (instead of 90)

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Oxford infant blade?
back: Straight, flange for ETT McCoy blade: Mac blade with a hinge at the end

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Straight?
back: Miller, Oxford, Wisconsin

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Curved?
back: Macintosh, McCoy

front: In Laryngoscopy Blades, what should be recalled about Mixed (straight + curved at end)?
back: Robertshaw, Seward

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what distinction applies to Rare (1%)?
back: Lethal syndrome associated with propofol infusion at 4 mg/kg/hour or more for >48 hours

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what should be recalled about Pathogenesis?
back: Mitochondrial dysfunction, muscle injury and release of intracellular toxic contents

front: What are the risk factors of Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS)?
back: Poor oxygen delivery, sepsis, serious cerebral injury

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what should be recalled about Predisposing factors?
back: Age (children), impaired fatty acid metabolism and low carbohydrate supply

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what should be recalled about FDA Definition?
back: Metabolic acidosis and/or rhabdomyolysis with progressive myocardial failure

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what should be recalled about Progressive myocardial failure?
back: Bradycardia, cardiac failure, dysrhythmias, and asystole

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what value(s) complete this note: “Metabolic acidosis (base deficit >___ mmol/L)”?
back: Metabolic acidosis (base deficit >10 mmol/L)

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what does Rhabdomyolysis indicate or lead to?
back: Hyperkalemia

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what does Hyperlipidemia indicate or lead to?
back: Pancreatitis

front: In Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS), what does Enlarged or fatty liver indicate or lead to?
back: Hepatomegaly

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what should be recalled about Best (4)?
back: Pulmonary artery, distal-third esophagus, tympanic membrane, nasopharynx

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what should be recalled about Acceptable outside extreme ranges?
back: Oral, axillary, rectal, bladder

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what should be recalled about Less reliable?
back: Skin and rectal

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what does Rectal temperatures lag behind core temperature changes indicate or lead to?
back: “intermediate”

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what does At low urine flows, correlates with rectal indicate or lead to?
back: “intermediate”

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what does At high urine flows, correlates with pulmonary artery indicate or lead to?
back: “core”

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what value(s) complete this note: “General anesthesia > ___ minutes”?
back: General anesthesia > 30 minutes

front: In Intraoperative Temperature Monitoring, what value(s) complete this note: “Target intraoperative temperature to >___°C (unless therapeutic hypothermia is indicated)”?
back: Target intraoperative temperature to >36°C (unless therapeutic hypothermia is indicated)

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what does Size: infants have larger tongues relative to oropharynx indicate or lead to?
back: More obstruction and difficult laryngoscopy

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what does Location: larynx is higher (more cephalad C3/4 vs. C5/6) indicate or lead to?
back: Straight blades more useful than curved blades

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what should be recalled about Epiglottis?
back: Shaped differently (short, stubby, omega-shaped, angled over the laryngeal inlet)

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what does Vocal cords: angled indicate or lead to?
back: Blindly passed tube gets stuck in anterior commissure instead of sliding into trachea

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what does Shape: larynx is funnel-shaped indicate or lead to?
back: The narrowest portion is at the cricoid cartilage

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what should be recalled about Classic teaching?
back: Adult larynx is cylindrical vs. infant larynx is funnel-shaped.

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what value(s) complete this note: “However, in ___ of adults narrowest portion is also at the level of the cricoid cartilage”?
back: However, in 70% of adults narrowest portion is also at the level of the cricoid cartilage

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what value(s) complete this note: “Difficult pediatric airways (Hagberg and Benumof's Airway Management ___e Ch ___)”?
back: Difficult pediatric airways (Hagberg and Benumof's Airway Management 4e Ch 36)

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what should be recalled about Congenital cervical spine instability?
back: Trisomy 21, and Hurler, Hunter, and Morquio syndromes

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what should be recalled about Limited Cervical Spine Mobility?
back: Klippel-Feil syndrome, Goldenhar syndrome, juvenile RA

topic: Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences
front: In Pediatric Airway vs. Adult Airway: Differences, what should be recalled about Micrognathia?
back: Treacher-Collins, Goldenhar, Pierre-Robin sequence

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what does Hemoglobin (Hb) binds CO 200x more readily than oxygen indicate or lead to?
back: Carboxyhemoglobin (HbCO) → ↓ O delivery

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what does Less hemoglobin available for oxygen transport indicate or lead to?
back: Functional anemia

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what does CO binds intracellular pigments (e.g., cytochrome) indicate or lead to?
back: Oxidative stress

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what does Triggers intravascular platelet-neutrophil aggregation + neutrophil activation indicate or lead to?
back: Toxicity to multiple organ systems

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what should be recalled about Clinical effects?
back: Headache, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, MI, AMS, and fetal distress

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what should be recalled about Risk factors for persistent sequelae?
back: Older age (≥36 years) and duration of CO exposure

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what should be recalled about Diagnosis?
back: History of exposure + confirmed by elevated HbCO level

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what does Mixed-venous oxygen saturation: Miller 8e Ch 51 says COHb indicate or lead to?
back: ↓ oxygen delivery → ↓ SvO

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what should be recalled about Note?
back: Only true when using a multi-wavelength CO-oximeter capable of detecting COHb

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what does If measured without co-oximetry indicate or lead to?
back: SvO2 is falsely elevated because it includes COHb

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what should be recalled about pO on ABG?
back: Normal

front: How is Carbon Monoxide Poisoning treatment handled?
back: Oxygen therapy +/- HBOT

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what value(s) complete this note: “HbCO >___”?
back: HbCO >25%

front: In Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, what does HBOT is not contraindicated in pregnancy indicate or lead to?
back: HbF binds CO more avidly → will be beneficial

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what should be recalled about Three basic tests?
back: Typing, screening and crossmatching

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what should be recalled about Typing (ABO-Rh)?
back: Checks for RBC AB antigens, RBC RhD antigen, and serum AB antibodies

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what does Take patient’s RBCs indicate or lead to?
back: Add commercial anti-A and anti-B antibodies → detect A and B antigens

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what does Take patient’s RBCs indicate or lead to?
back: Add commercial anti-RhD antibodies → detect RhD antigen

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what does Take patient’s serum indicate or lead to?
back: Add commercial A and B RBCS → detect anti-A and anti-B antibodies

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what should be recalled about Screening?
back: Checking for other antibodies in patient serum

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what does Take patient’s serum indicate or lead to?
back: Add commercial RBCs with known antigens → detect antibodies (shown below) Source: The Blood Project

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what does Immediate-spin serologic crossmatch (donor RBC + recipient serum indicate or lead to?
back: Centrifuge to check agglutination) → faster than full antiglobulin crossmatch

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what does Otherwise, a full antiglobulin crossmatch is required indicate or lead to?
back: Takes longer

front: In Transfusion Preparation: Blood Compatibility Testing, what does Donor RBC + recipient serum incubated at 37 C indicate or lead to?
back: Anti-human IgG’s (Coombs antibodies) added

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what does Methylmethacrylate (MMA) is a pressurized bone cement used in hip and knee replacements indicate or lead to?
back: Expands

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what should be recalled about Bone-cement implantation syndrome (BCIS)?
back: Potentially fatal condition with hypotension and hypoxia

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what does Increased intramedullary pressure indicate or lead to?
back: Pulmonary microembolization → increased PVR → RV failure

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what does Cardiac: Decreased MAP and SV, increased PVR indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased RVEF, arrhythmias

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what does Pulmonary: emboli indicate or lead to?
back: Increased PVR → V/Q mismatch → decreased end-tidal CO

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what does Hematology: activation of coagulation, vasodilation, platelet aggregation indicate or lead to?
back: Thrombosis

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what should be recalled about CNS?
back: Altered mental status, paradoxical emboli and stroke with PFO

front: What are the risk factors of Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome?
back: Old age, preexisting pulmonary hypertension, cardiac disease, osteoporosis

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what value(s) complete this note: “Increase FiO2 to ___.”?
back: Increase FiO2 to 100%.

front: In Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome, what does Cardiovascular collapse indicate or lead to?
back: RV failure → fluids, pulmonary vasodilators, inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone)

front: How is Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome prevention handled?
back: Try to avoid MMA cementing in high-risk patients, consider advanced monitoring (TEE, a-line, etc.)

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does If every molecule of drug entering the liver is metabolized (HER 1) indicate or lead to?
back: Hepatic clearance = liver blood flow

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what distinction applies to This is true for very few drugs?
back: Very nearly the case for propofol

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does If extraction ratio is 50% indicate or lead to?
back: Hepatic clearance is 50% of liver blood flow

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does High HER indicate or lead to?
back: Liver completely metabolizes drug → clearance is proportional to hepatic blood flow

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what should be recalled about Propofol?
back: Hepatic blood flow doubles, then the clearance of propofol doubles

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does Drugs with high HER indicate or lead to?
back: Flow-dependent clearance

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what should be recalled about Examples?
back: Etomidate, propofol, ketamine, morphine, fentanyl, meperidine, bupivacaine, metoprolol, diltiazem, nifedipine

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does Low HER indicate or lead to?
back: Slowly cleared by the liver

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does If liver enzymes are induced indicate or lead to?
back: Clearance will increase

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does Liver damage (e.g. cirrhosis) indicate or lead to?
back: Metabolism and clearance is reduced

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what does Drugs with low HER indicate or lead to?
back: Capacity-dependent clearance

front: In Hepatic Extraction Ratio (HER), what should be recalled about Drugs with low HER?
back: Diazepam, lorazepam, methadone (10%), rocuronium, alfentanil (15%), thiopental

topic: Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries
front: In Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries, what does Free air under the diaphragm indicate or lead to?
back: Hollow viscus rupture

topic: Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries
front: In Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries, what does Rib fractures indicate or lead to?
back: Predisposition to hepatic/splenic injury

topic: Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries
front: In Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries, what does NG tube in the thoracic cavity and/or stomach/intestine in thorax indicate or lead to?
back: Diaphragmatic rupture → risk of bowel ischemia → needs emergent surgical repair

topic: Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries
front: In Chest X-ray Findings in Trauma associated with Abdominal Injuries, what should be recalled about Clinical signs?
back: Chest and abdominal pain + shortness of breath

front: In Fire Safety, what should be recalled about Fire triad?
back: Ignition source, oxidizer, and fuel source

front: In Fire Safety, what should be recalled about Oxidizers in the OR?
back: Oxygen and nitrous oxide

front: In Fire Safety, what should be recalled about Ignition sources?
back: Electrosurgical or electrocautery devices, lasers (fastest ignition source), argon beam coagulators, fiber-optic light cables, defibrillator pads, heated probes, and drills and burrs.

front: In Fire Safety, what should be recalled about Fuel sources?
back: Endotracheal tubes, sponges, drapes, gauze, alcohol-containing preparation solutions, patient's hair, surgical dressings, gastrointestinal tract gases, and packaging materials

front: In Fire Safety, what should be recalled about High-risk procedures for fires (proximity to the airway)?
back: ENT surgeries (tonsillectomies, tracheostomies, removal of laryngeal papillomas), eye surgeries (cataracts), burr hole surgery, and removal of lesions in the head area

front: In Fire Safety, what value(s) complete this note: “Avoid nitrous oxide and use the lowest FiO possible (keep FiO < ___)”?
back: Avoid nitrous oxide and use the lowest FiO possible (keep FiO < 30%)

front: In Fire Safety, what does Stop the procedure indicate or lead to?
back: Stop the flow of all airway gases → remove the endotracheal tube.

front: In Fire Safety, what value(s) complete this note: “Mask-ventilate the patient with ___ O and reintubate”?
back: Mask-ventilate the patient with 100% O and reintubate

front: In Fire Safety, what does All electric power that feeds an electrical fire must be cut indicate or lead to?
back: Converts it into an ordinary fire

front: In Fire Safety, what does Water must never be thrown or sprayed on an electrical fire or onto burning flammable liquids indicate or lead to?
back: Dry chemical extinguisher or carbon dioxide extinguishers (for burning oils and liquids)

front: In Gas Laws, what should be recalled about Ideal Gas Law?
back: PV = nRT

front: In Gas Laws, what does Boyle’s Law indicate or lead to?
back: Constant temperature (“water Boyle's at a constant temperature”)

front: In Gas Laws, what does Charles’ Law indicate or lead to?
back: Constant pressure (“Prince Charles is under constant pressure to be king”)

front: In Gas Laws, what does Gay-Lussac’s Law indicate or lead to?
back: Constant volume

front: In Gas Laws, what does Henry’s Law indicate or lead to?
back: Amount of gas dissolved in a liquid is equal to its partial pressure (at a constant temperature)

front: In Gas Laws, what should be recalled about Dalton’s Law?
back: P = P + P + … + P

front: In Gas Laws, what should be recalled about Critical Temperature?
back: Highest temperature at which a gas can exist in liquid form

front: In Gas Laws, what does Oxygen indicate or lead to?
back: -118°C → can only exist as a gas at room temperature (since it is above -118°C)

front: In Gas Laws, what does Critical temperature of nitrous oxide is 36.5°C indicate or lead to?
back: Gas + liquid at room temperature

front: In Gas Laws, what does At 40°C indicate or lead to?
back: Temperature is above critical temperature of N O → so only exists as a gas at this temp

front: In Tranexamic Acid, what should be recalled about Normal fibrinolysis?
back: Inactive plasminogen binds to fibrin (clot) through an active site that binds lysine

front: In Tranexamic Acid, what does Plasmin breaks down fibrin indicate or lead to?
back: Fibrinolysis

front: In Tranexamic Acid, what does Tranexamic acid (TXA) and aminocaproic acid (ACA) are structural analogues of lysine indicate or lead to?
back: Bind irreversibly to the lysine-binding sites on plasminogen → inhibit its fibrinolytic activity and stabilize clots

front: In Tranexamic Acid, what should be recalled about Uses?
back: Reduce surgical bleeding (ortho, spine), trauma within three hours (CRASH-2 trial)

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what should be recalled about Bacillus anthracis?
back: Aerobic gram-positive, spore-forming, rod-shaped organism

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what should be recalled about Three forms?
back: Cutaneous, gastrointestinal, and pulmonary (most relevant to biological warfare uses)

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what should be recalled about Findings?
back: Nonspecific

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what should be recalled about CXR?
back: Effusion or edema, mediastinal widening (from spores in lymph nodes)

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what does Asymptomatic phase: incubation period 1-3 days indicate or lead to?
back: Anthrax toxin released into circulation

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what does Initial symptomatic insidious phase 1-4 days indicate or lead to?
back: Flu-like symptoms

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what does Next phase: necrotizing hemorrhagic mediastinitis indicate or lead to?
back: Chest discomfort, dyspnea, stridor

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what does Untreated indicate or lead to?
back: Multiple organ failure follows → death within 24 to 36 hours

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what should be recalled about Treatment and exposure prophylaxis?
back: Ciprofloxacin or doxycycline

front: In Inhalational Anthrax, what should be recalled about High mortality?
back: 80% untreated, 50% with treatment

front: In Polyhydramnios, what should be recalled about Concerns?
back: Associated with preterm labor, preterm rupture of membranes, postpartum uterine atony

front: In Polyhydramnios, what does Neuromuscular disease indicate or lead to?
back: Inhibiting fetal swallowing

front: In Polyhydramnios, what does One twin receives more blood indicate or lead to?
back: Develops polyhydramnios

front: In Polyhydramnios, what does Other receives less blood indicate or lead to?
back: Develops oligohydramnios

front: In Polyhydramnios, what does Treatment indicate or lead to?
back: Indomethacin or amnioreduction

front: In Amniocentesis, what should be recalled about Second Trimester?
back: Usually for genetic testing or for neural tube defects (AFP, acetylcholinesterase)

front: In Amniocentesis, what should be recalled about Fetal lung maturity?
back: Measure lecithin and sphingomyelin

front: In Amniocentesis, what should be recalled about Intra-amniotic infection?
back: Look for pathogenic bacteria

front: In Amniocentesis, what does Confirm preterm premature rupture of membranes (PROM) indicate or lead to?
back: Amniodye test

front: In Amniocentesis, what value(s) complete this note: “Greatest risk is spontaneous abortion (___ in ___)”?
back: Greatest risk is spontaneous abortion (1 in 300-500)

front: In Amniocentesis, what does Transient leakage of amniotic fluid (1%-2%) indicate or lead to?
back: Usually stops after 48 to 72 hours

front: In Amniocentesis, what does Early amniocentesis (<15w gestation) indicate or lead to?
back: Not recommended (use chorionic villus sampling instead) → higher risk of pregnancy loss rates, rupture of membranes, club foot, and amniocyte culture failure

front: In Complications After Peripheral Nerve Blocks, what should be recalled about Risk factors contributing to neurologic deficit after regional anesthesia include?
back: Neural ischemia, traumatic injury to the nerves during needle or catheter placement, infection, and choice of local anesthetic solution

front: In Complications After Peripheral Nerve Blocks, what does Use low injection pressure: high injection pressures indicate or lead to?
back: Fascicular injury

front: In Complications After Peripheral Nerve Blocks, what does Previously injured nerves (diabetes, MS) indicate or lead to?
back: Increased risk for block-related nerve injury

front: In Complications After Peripheral Nerve Blocks, what does If damage to protective tissue barriers is suspected (excess pain on injection) indicate or lead to?
back: Consider stopping

front: In Train-of-Four Count and Receptor Blockade, what does 0 to 80-85% receptor blockade indicate or lead to?
back: 4 twitches

front: In Train-of-Four Count and Receptor Blockade, what does Above 80-85% blockade indicate or lead to?
back: T or T disappears → 3 or 2 twitches

front: In Train-of-Four Count and Receptor Blockade, what does Above 85-90% blockade indicate or lead to?
back: T disappears → 1 twitch

front: In Train-of-Four Count and Receptor Blockade, what does After 90-95% blockade indicate or lead to?
back: T disappears → 0 twitches

front: In Corticosteroid Myopathy, what should be recalled about Clinical?
back: Proximal and respiratory muscle weakness and atrophy (legs > arms) + cushingoid appearance

front: In Corticosteroid Myopathy, what should be recalled about Diagnostic studies?
back: Serum CK, LHD and needle EMG are normal

front: In Corticosteroid Myopathy, what value(s) complete this note: “Muscle biopsies show type ___ fiber atrophy without inflammation”?
back: Muscle biopsies show type 2 fiber atrophy without inflammation

front: In Corticosteroid Myopathy, what value(s) complete this note: “Hypophosphatemia (usually level <___ mg/dL) associated with refeeding syndrome”?
back: Hypophosphatemia (usually level <1 mg/dL) associated with refeeding syndrome

front: In Corticosteroid Myopathy, what should be recalled about Critical illness myopathy?
back: Flaccid diffuse weakness including facial muscles and diaphragm

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what should be recalled about Gold standard?
back: Overnight polysomnography (PSG) aka sleep study

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what should be recalled about Apnea and hypopnea?
back: At least 10 sec + 3-4% ↓in SpO or EEG arousal

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what does Apneas indicate or lead to?
back: Reduction in the rate of nasal airflow (from nasal pressure signal) of >90%

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what does Hypopneas indicate or lead to?
back: Reduction in the rate of nasal airflow between 50-90%

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what does Obstructive indicate or lead to?
back: Thoracoabdominal motion out of phase or airflow limitation is observed

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what does Central indicate or lead to?
back: Thoracoabdominal motion is in-phase and no evidence of airflow limitation

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what does Mixed indicate or lead to?
back: Events that begin as central (>10s) and end as obstructive with 3+ obstructive efforts

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what should be recalled about Apnea-hypopnea index (AHI)?
back: Average number of abnormal breathing events per hour of sleep

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what should be recalled about Mild?
back: AHI 5-15

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what should be recalled about Moderate?

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what should be recalled about Severe?
back: AHI >30

topic: Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
front: In Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), what should be recalled about Clinical diagnosis of OSA?
