back: AHI >15 or AHI >5 + symptoms (excessive daytime sleepiness, unintentional sleep during wakefulness, unrefreshing sleep, loud snoring, or observed obstruction during sleep)

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what does Gravid uterus indicate or lead to?
back: Pushes stomach and pylorus cephalad → intraabdominal portion of the esophagus is now pushed intrathoracic → decreased lower esophageal sphincter (LES) competence

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what does Placenta indicate or lead to?
back: Secretes gastrin → increased gastric acid secretion → lower (more acidic) gastric pH

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what does Enlarged uterus indicate or lead to?
back: Increased intragastric pressure

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what value(s) complete this note: “Metoclopramide ↓ gastric volume in about ___ minutes (effectiveness decreased by opioids)”?
back: Metoclopramide ↓ gastric volume in about 15 minutes (effectiveness decreased by opioids)

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what value(s) complete this note: “H -receptor antagonists increase gastric pH in pregnant women ___ hour after administration”?
back: H -receptor antagonists increase gastric pH in pregnant women 1 hour after administration

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what value(s) complete this note: “___x Alk Phos due to placental production”?
back: 2x Alk Phos due to placental production

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what does ↓ protein/albumin levels indicate or lead to?
back: Elevated free blood levels of highly protein-bound drugs

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what value(s) complete this note: “↓ plasma cholinesterase activity (___) from ___th week of gestation up to ___ weeks postpartum”?
back: ↓ plasma cholinesterase activity (25-30%) from 10th week of gestation up to 6 weeks postpartum

front: In Gastrointestinal Changes in Pregnancy, what should be recalled about Gallbladder disease more likely?
back: Incomplete gallbladder emptying and changes in bile composition

front: In Propofol: Contraindications, what does Propofol is only slightly soluble in water indicate or lead to?
back: Therefore it is formulated in an oil-in-water emulsion

front: In Propofol: Contraindications, what value(s) complete this note: “Soybean oil (___ mg/mL)”?
back: Soybean oil (100 mg/mL)

front: In Propofol: Contraindications, what value(s) complete this note: “Glycerol (___ mg/mL)”?
back: Glycerol (22.5 mg/mL)

front: In Propofol: Contraindications, what does Egg lecithin (12 mg/mL) indicate or lead to?
back: Derived from egg yolk

front: In Propofol: Contraindications, what does Disodium edetate (0.005%) indicate or lead to?
back: To prevent bacterial growth

front: In Propofol: Contraindications, what distinction applies to Usually allergies are to egg proteins?
back: Propofol is formulated in egg fats (lecithin)

front: In Propofol: Contraindications, what does However, AAAAI statement: “The bottom line: Patients with soy allergy or egg allergy can receive propofol without any special precautions” indicate or lead to?
back: E.g. okay to use propofol for TIVA in patient with concern for MH

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what should be recalled about Anatomy?
back: Stellate ganglion lies anterior to the neck of the first rib and the C7 transverse process

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what should be recalled about Approach?
back: Anterior paratracheal approach at C6 (stellate ganglion lies at C7) using surface landmarks

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Performing block at C6 indicate or lead to?
back: Reduces likelihood of pneumothorax (higher risk at C7)

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Anterior tubercle of C6 transverse process (Chassaignac tubercle) is palpated between the trachea and sternocleidomastoid muscle indicate or lead to?
back: Needle is advanced and seated on the tubercle → LA injection

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what value(s) complete this note: “Increase in temperature of the blocked limb by at least ___° C”?
back: Increase in temperature of the blocked limb by at least 1° C

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what should be recalled about Neuropathic pain?
back: Ischemic neuropathies, herpes zoster (shingles), early PHN, post-radiation neuritis

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what should be recalled about Vascular insufficiency?
back: Intractable angina pectoris, Raynaud disease, frostbite, vasospasm,

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Can block adjacent RLN indicate or lead to?
back: Hoarseness, lump in the throat, SOB, difficulty swallowing

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Do not perform bilateral stellate ganglion block indicate or lead to?
back: Bilateral RLN blocks → respiratory compromise

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Can block phrenic nerve indicate or lead to?
back: Unilateral diaphragmatic paresis

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Can cause somatic block of upper extremity indicate or lead to?
back: Ranges from sensory loss to complete brachial plexus block

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what value(s) complete this note: “Patients must be monitored for at least ___ minutes after stellate ganglion block because inadvertent neuraxial blockade can take ___ minutes to manifest”?
back: Patients must be monitored for at least 30 minutes after stellate ganglion block because inadvertent neuraxial blockade can take 15-20 minutes to manifest

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Intravascular injection indicate or lead to?
back: Carotid artery (medial) or vertebral artery (lateral) → generalized seizures

front: In Stellate Ganglion Block, what does Vertebral artery passes anterior to C7 transverse process without bony foramen for protection indicate or lead to?
back: Enters the C6 transverse process bony foramen

front: In Oxygen Analyzers, what should be recalled about Two types in ventilator gas analysis?
back: Fuel cell (galvanic) and paramagnetic

front: In Oxygen Analyzers, what does Mechanism: O permeates membrane indicate or lead to?
back: Enters KOH solution → O supplied to lead anode → electrical potential is established between lead anode and noble metal cathode → measured voltage between is proportional to oxygen tension (Faraday’s first law)

front: In Oxygen Analyzers, what should be recalled about Disadvantages?
back: Temperature compensation required, slow response rate, limited life span

front: In Oxygen Analyzers, what does Most gases are diamagnetic (repelled by magnetic field) indicate or lead to?
back: Oxygen is paramagnetic (attracted to magnetic field)

front: In Oxygen Analyzers, what does Air (reference) and gas sample are drawn through two tube indicate or lead to?
back: Electromagnet placed over junction of the tubes → magnetic field attracts oxygen in proportion to their concentrations → intermittent pressure difference can be measured upstream from this change in flow → O concentration

front: In Oxygen Analyzers, what should be recalled about Advantages?
back: Unlimited life span, very fast response, accurate

front: In Oxygen Analyzers, what should be recalled about Disadvantages?
back: Sensitive to water vapor (causes falsely low oxygen concentration)

front: In Tetanus Toxin, what should be recalled about Clinical?
back: Muscle spasms, spastic paralysis , respiratory failure, autonomic dysfunction, sensory disturbances

front: In Tetanus Toxin, what does Mechanism: tetanus toxin indicate or lead to?
back: Heavy chain + light chain.

front: In Tetanus Toxin, what does Heavy chain indicate or lead to?
back: Toxin entry into peripheral neurons → retrograde axonal transport → central inhibitory interneurons → light chain released

front: In Tetanus Toxin, what does Light chain indicate or lead to?
back: Cleaves SNARE proteins → inhibits release of GABA and glycine → inhibitory neurons lose

front: In Tetanus Toxin, what does Botulinum toxin only affects peripheral nerves indicate or lead to?
back: Flaccid paralysis

front: In Tetanus Toxin, what does Autonomic dysfunction: sweating, vasoconstriction, hemodynamic swings indicate or lead to?
back: Major cause of mortality

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does ↑ Blood volume (BV) indicate or lead to?
back: But less BV on a volume-to-weight basis (50 mL/kg vs. 70 mL/kg in normal adult males)

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does Fatty infiltration of the conduction system indicate or lead to?
back: Cardiac dysrhythmias

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does ↑ SNS activity indicate or lead to?
back: Insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, hypertension

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what should be recalled about ECG changes?
back: Low QRS voltage, LVH, LA enlargement, T-wave flattening, QT prolongation

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does ↑ Renin–angiotensin system indicate or lead to?
back: ↑ angiotensinogen, aldosterone, ACE

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does Ventricular dilation + ↑ SV indicate or lead to?
back: Cardiac Output (CO) → hypertrophy → reduced compliance → diastolic dysfunction → systolic dysfunction (“obesity cardiomyopathy”) → eventual biventricular failure results

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does Expanded blood volume indicate or lead to?
back: Increased cardiac output → lower SVR

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does Hypertension indicate or lead to?
back: Eccentric dilation in obese individuals (vs concentric hypertrophy in normal-weight individuals)

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does Adipose tissue indicate or lead to?
back: Releases of bioactive mediators → dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, inflammation, coagulopathy

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does ↑ fibrinogen, factor VII, factor VIII, vWF, PAI-1 indicate or lead to?
back: Hypercoagulability.

front: In Physiologic Changes in Obesity: Cardiovascular, what does Insulin indicate or lead to?
back: Endothelial dysfunction → ↑ vWF and factor VIII levels → predisposing to fibrin formation

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what should be recalled about Paraplegia?
back: Very rare (1 per 100,000)

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what should be recalled about Anterior spinal artery syndrome?
back: Painless loss of motor and sensory function, sparing of proprioception

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what should be recalled about Cauda Equina Syndrome?
back: Very rare (1 per 100,000)

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what does Small spinal catheters indicate or lead to?
back: Reduce risk of headache but predispose to pooling of local anesthetic around lumbosacral nerve roots

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what does Slow, low-pressure injections indicate or lead to?
back: More pooling

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what does Hyperbaric, concentrated LA use indicate or lead to?
back: More pooling and concentration

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what does Catheters pointed caudally (dependent-side) indicate or lead to?
back: More pooling

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what value(s) complete this note: “Epidural Hematoma < ___”?
back: Epidural Hematoma < 0.005%

front: What are the risk factors of Epidural Hematoma < 0.005%?
back: Difficult or traumatic needle or catheter insertion, coagulopathy, old, female gender

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what should be recalled about Nerve Injury?
In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what value(s) complete this note: “Frequency of transient nerve injury ___ and permanent nerve injury ___”?
back: Frequency of transient nerve injury 0.1% and permanent nerve injury 0.02%

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what should be recalled about Procedure-related risk factors?
back: Radicular pain or paresthesia occurring during the procedure

front: In Neuraxial Anesthesia: Neurologic Complications, what does Patient with space-occupying lesions (tumors, severe stenosis, lipomatosis, ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, ependymoma) indicate or lead to?
back: Most at risk for permanent new or worsening neurologic injury from neuraxial anesthesia

front: In Laryngospasm, what should be recalled about Definition?
back: Reflex closure of both the true and false vocal cords

front: In Laryngospasm, what does Complete laryngospasm: closure of the false vocal cords (vestibular folds) + apposition of the epiglottis and interarytenoids indicate or lead to?
back: Complete cessation of air movement

front: In Laryngospasm, what does Incomplete (partial) laryngospasm: incomplete apposition of the vocal cords with a residual small gap between the cords (posteriorly) indicate or lead to?
back: Inspiratory stridor

front: In Laryngospasm, what should be recalled about Afferent?
back: Internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve

front: In Laryngospasm, what should be recalled about Efferent?
back: Recurrent laryngeal nerve to the three main intrinsic laryngeal muscles

front: In Laryngospasm, what does Reminder: external branch of SLN indicate or lead to?
back: Cricothyroid muscle → causes glottic tension + elongation

front: In Laryngospasm, what does Supraglottic soft tissues pulled down to glottis indicate or lead to?
back: Obstruction → can be alleviated by CPAP

front: In Laryngospasm, what should be recalled about Age?
back: Infants > older children and adults; risk decreases with increasing age

front: In Laryngospasm, what value(s) complete this note: “Recent URI (<___ wks)”?
back: Recent URI (<2 wks)

front: In Laryngospasm, what value(s) complete this note: “Positive-pressure ventilation (APL valve to ___ cmH O) and ___ O”?
back: Positive-pressure ventilation (APL valve to 20 cmH O) and 100% O

front: In Laryngospasm, what does “Larson maneuver”: Pressure in the laryngospasm notch (behind the pinna of the ear) indicate or lead to?
back: Cephalad and medial pressure → pain → triggers crying → opening of the vocal cords

front: In Laryngospasm, what does If the above steps fail: in order indicate or lead to?
back: IV/IM atropine (0.02 mg/kg) (in kids) → IV propofol (1 mg/kg) → succinylcholine IV (1-2 mg/kg) or IM (4-5 mg/kg)

front: What is the mechanism of Flumazenil?
back: Benzodiazepine antagonist

front: In Flumazenil, what should be recalled about Most common side effects?
back: Nausea/vomiting (10-20%) and headache

front: In Flumazenil, what does Short half-life: flumazenil (about 60 mins) indicate or lead to?
back: Vs. longer half-lives of most benzodiazepines

topic: Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
front: What is the mac in Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)?
back: Is a sedation service provided by anesthesia personnel that covers the whole continuum of sedation

topic: Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
front: In Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC), what does No airway intervention (if any airway intervention indicate or lead to?
back: Automatically defined as deep sedation)

topic: Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
front: In Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC), what should be recalled about Minimal (anxiolysis) sedation?
back: Normal response to verbal stimulation

topic: Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
front: In Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC), what should be recalled about Moderate sedation?
back: Purposeful response* to verbal commands or light tactile stimulation

topic: Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
front: In Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC), what should be recalled about Deep sedation?
back: Purposeful response* after repeated verbal or painful tactile stimulation + possible airway intervention 1,2,3

topic: Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
front: In Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC), what should be recalled about General anesthesia?
back: Unarousable with painful stimulus

topic: Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
front: In Moderate Sedation vs. Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC), what does Patient responds to repeated shouting (verbal) but no airway intervention indicate or lead to?
back: Deep sedation

front: In Morquio Syndrome, what does Morquio's syndrome (mucopolysaccharidosis IV) indicate or lead to?
back: Accumulation of keratan sulfate and chondroitin-6- sulfate

front: In Morquio Syndrome, what does Normal at birth indicate or lead to?
back: Spine dysplasia within 12-18 months → severe thoracolumbar kyphoscoliosis + atlantoaxial instability (difficult airway) with possible cord compression

front: In Morquio Syndrome, what does Limited development of the trunk indicate or lead to?
back: Dwarfism

front: In Morquio Syndrome, what should be recalled about Other skeletal issues?
back: Joint laxity, abnormal facies, and valgus/varus deformity of the knees

front: In Morquio Syndrome, what should be recalled about CNS?
back: Usually not involved, normal mental status

front: In Cardioplegia, what does Blood vs. non-blood (crystalloid): blood is more common indicate or lead to?
back: Delivers oxygen + scavenges free radicals

front: In Cardioplegia, what should be recalled about Temperature?
back: Cold (10C) vs. tepid (29C) vs. warm (37C)

front: In Cardioplegia, what does Cold (10-12 C) indicate or lead to?
back: Decreases metabolism very effectively

front: In Cardioplegia, what should be recalled about Disadvantages?
back: Increased edema, impaired function of membrane receptors, increased plasma viscosity, decreased RBC deformability (can’t get to microvascular spaces), left-shifted O -Hb dissociation curve (decreased oxygen delivery)

front: In Cardioplegia, what should be recalled about Advantage?
back: No need for continuous cardioplegia

front: In Cardioplegia, what should be recalled about Route of Delivery?
back: Anterograde vs. retrograde

front: In Cardioplegia, what does Antegrade indicate or lead to?
back: Cardioplegia catheter in aortic root and coronary arteries → like normal coronary flow

front: In Cardioplegia, what should be recalled about Disadvantage?
back: Cardioplegia may not get past severely stenosed arteries

front: In Cardioplegia, what does Needs a competent aortic valve, otherwise cardioplegia solution will leak into LV instead of getting into the aortic sinuses indicate or lead to?
back: Coronary arteries

front: In Cardioplegia, what does Retrograde indicate or lead to?
back: Cardioplegia catheter in coronary sinus (venous drainage)

front: In Cardioplegia, what value(s) complete this note: “Perfusion pressure must be kept < ___ mmHg to limit perivascular edema and hemorrhage”?
back: Perfusion pressure must be kept < 40 mmHg to limit perivascular edema and hemorrhage

front: In Cardioplegia, what should be recalled about Not usable in some anatomic variants?
back: Persistent left superior vena cava (left SVC drains into coronary sinus instead of the RA)

front: In Cardioplegia, what does Retrograde cardioplegia is inefficient in producing arrest of the beating heart indicate or lead to?
back: Induction of arrest must be achieved by a single antegrade infusion of cardioplegia first

front: In Enteral Nutrition: Routes of Delivery, what should be recalled about Nasogastric vs. Orogastric tubes?
back: Increased risk of sinusitis and otitis media with prolonged user

front: In Enteral Nutrition: Routes of Delivery, what should be recalled about Post-pyloric feeding tube?
back: Decreased risk of pneumonia but does not decrease risk of aspiration

front: In Enteral Nutrition: Routes of Delivery, what does Concomitant gastroparesis is common in critically ill patients indicate or lead to?
back: Elevate head of bed, use promotility agents

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Release of cytokines indicate or lead to?
back: Mainly IL-1, IL-6, and TNF-α

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Neuroendocrine indicate or lead to?
back: Activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis (HPA)

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Sympathoadrenal indicate or lead to?
back: Catecholamine release → increased epinephrine and norepinephrine

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Adrenal medulla indicate or lead to?
back: Epinephrine release

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Sympathetic nerve endings indicate or lead to?
back: Norepinephrine release

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Cardiovascular indicate or lead to?
back: Tachycardia, hypertension, and increase in cardiac workload

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Gastrointestinal indicate or lead to?
back: Postoperative ileus

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Renal indicate or lead to?
back: ADH → Increased risk of oliguria and urinary retention

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Increased G-CSF indicate or lead to?
back: Leukocytosis

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Increased thrombopoietin indicate or lead to?
back: Thrombocytosis

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Hepcidin indicate or lead to?
back: Reduced activity of ferroportin → iron sequestration in macrophages

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Increased thrombin generation and fibrin deposition indicate or lead to?
back: Hypercoagulable state

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Decreased physiologic anticoagulants indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased AT3, TFPI, protein C

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Upregulated procoagulant pathways indicate or lead to?
back: Increased tissue factor (TF)

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does ↓ t-PA + ↑ plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 (PAI-1) indicate or lead to?
back: Decreased fibrinolysis

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Regional anesthesia indicate or lead to?
back: Less hypercoagulability (vs. general anesthesia) → less DVT

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Immunologic indicate or lead to?
back: Impaired immune function

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Muscular indicate or lead to?
back: Muscle weakness and fatigue.

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does ↓ insulin + ↑ catecholamines, cortisol, glucagon indicate or lead to?
back: Gluconeogenesis + insulin resistance → hyperglycemia

front: In Surgical Stress Response, what does Reproductive Hormones indicate or lead to?
back: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism → low testosterone, FSH, LS; high estradiol

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Highly lipophilic indicate or lead to?
back: Allows them to cross the BBB and have rapid central effects

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Redistribution to inactive tissue sites indicate or lead to?
back: Termination of the drug effect

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Midazolam formulated in aqueous solution indicate or lead to?
back: Less pain on injection → once in body, becomes lipophilic

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Diazepam and lorazepam formulated in propylene glycol indicate or lead to?
back: Pain on injection

front: In Benzodiazepines, what should be recalled about Metabolism?
back: Oxidation (Phase I) and glucuronide conjugation (Phase II).

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Midazolam and diazepam undergo oxidation indicate or lead to?
back: More susceptible to age, cirrhosis, other P450 modulators

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Diazepam indicate or lead to?
back: Oxidized to active metabolism → prolonged drug effects

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Midazolam indicate or lead to?
back: Oxidized by CYP34A → 1-OH-midazolam which is an active metabolite but undergoes rapid glucuronidation and clearance

front: In Benzodiazepines, what value(s) complete this note: “___-OH-midazolam can become an issue with prolonged infusions (ICU)”?
back: 1-OH-midazolam can become an issue with prolonged infusions (ICU)

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Lorazepam does not undergo oxidation indicate or lead to?
back: Single-step conjugation to glucuronic acid → excretion

front: In Benzodiazepines, what does Remimazolam indicate or lead to?
back: Rapidly hydrolyzed by tissue esterases

front: In Benzodiazepines, what should be recalled about Compared to midazolam?
back: Smaller V , faster clearance, and clearance independent of body weight

front: In Benzodiazepines, what should be recalled about Oral midazolam in children?
back: Onset in 10 mins, peaks in 20 to 30 mins, effects begin to dissipate at 45 mins

front: In Benzodiazepines, what should be recalled about Intravenous midazolam?
back: Peaks in 10 minutes after administration

front: In Benzodiazepines, what should be recalled about Diazepam?
back: Onset is more than twice as rapid as that of midazolam but duration is much longer

front: In Myotonia Congenita (MC), what should be recalled about Percussion myotonia?
back: Indenting-appearing myotonia triggered by tapping the muscle

front: In Myotonia Congenita (MC), what distinction applies to No association with malignant hyperthermia?
back: Can have masseter spasm with D-NMBA administration

front: In Myotonia Congenita (MC), what should be recalled about Avoid hypothermia?
back: Shivering can trigger the myotonic reaction

front: In Hydromorphone, what value(s) complete this note: “Hydromorphone is a semisynthetic analog of morphine with ___x potency (IV form)”?
back: Hydromorphone is a semisynthetic analog of morphine with 8-10x potency (IV form)

front: In Hydromorphone, what does Less hydrophilic than morphine indicate or lead to?
back: More rapid onset of analgesia: 5 mins; peak in 10-15 minutes

front: What is the duration of action shorter than morphine in Hydromorphone?
back: 4-5 hours

front: In Hydromorphone, what value(s) complete this note: “Main metabolite is hydromorphone-___-glucuronide (instead of the ___-glucuronide like morphine)”?
back: Main metabolite is hydromorphone-3-glucuronide (instead of the 6-glucuronide like morphine)
